gmw_00003075.name: B2 0912+29
gmw_00003075.overview: B2 0912+29 is a blazar [arXiv:1603.02410] classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1701.04305]. It exhibits a featureless optical spectrum [arXiv:1701.04305] and is categorized as a high-frequency peaked BL Lac object [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper reports B2 0912+29 as the highest-significance blazar in the VERITAS sample, but follow-up did not strengthen the excess enough for a claim.

quotes.quote: B2 0912+29 (HBL, z=0.36 ) This blazar shows the highest significance in our dataset ( 3.5 in 11.7 hours). This excess was confirmed at the same significance level by the cross-check analysis chain. Further observations were taken during the 2013 and 2014 observing seasons, but the initial excess did not increase. While VHE blazars are known to be variable, and one could interpret the lack of a detection in 2013-2014 as due to variability, we also note that the probability of a 3.5 excess reduces to only 2.0 when 103 trials (the sources from Tables Sourcelist and 2FGLlist ) are taken into account, and it is thus not enough to make any claim.

sources.summary: The paper confirms BZB J0915+2933 remains featureless in a high-SNR optical spectrum and sets a spectroscopic redshift lower limit.

quotes.quote: BZB J0915+2933 : wills1976 showed a continuous optical spectrum for the source and classified it as a BLL. The featureless behaviour was also found by white2000 and by shaw2013a . Through our high SNR optical spectrum, we confirm the featureless spectrum and set a lower limit to the redshift of z > 0.13.

gmw_00003076.name: 1H 1934 603
gmw_00003076.overview: The radio-quiet galaxy 1H 1934 603 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1802.06056, arXiv:2204.06075] that exhibits a spiral morphology [arXiv:2006.01700]. It is characterized by extreme X-ray variability [arXiv:1802.06056, arXiv:1909.13242], relativistic disk reflection [arXiv:1908.07272, arXiv:1909.13242], and a stable ultra-fast outflow [arXiv:2204.06075].

sources.summary: The paper studies 1H 1934-063 as a highly variable narrow-line Seyfert 1, finding dramatic X-ray flux changes, strong reflection, low black-hole spin, and a Fe-K reverberation lag.

quotes.quote: We introduce the case study of 1H 1934-063 (z = 0.0102), a Narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) among the brightest and most variable AGN ever observed with XMM-Newton . This work includes spectral and temporal analyses of a concurrent XMM-Newton and NuSTAR 2015 observation lasting 130 kiloseconds, during which the X-ray source exhibited a steep (factor of 6) plummet and subsequent full recovery of flux level, accompanied by deviation from a single log-normal flux distribution. We rule out Compton-thin obscuration as the cause for this dramatic variability observed even at NuSTAR energies. In order to constrain coronal geometry, dynamics, and emission/absorption processes, we compare detailed spectral fitting with Fourier-based timing analysis. Similar to other well-studied, highly variable Seyfert 1s, this AGN is X-ray bright and displays strong reflection features.

We find a narrower broad iron line component compared to most Seyfert 1s, and constrain black hole spin to be < 0.1, one of the lowest yet discovered for such systems. Combined spectral and timing results are consistent with a dramatic change in the continuum on timescales as short as a few kiloseconds dictating the nature of this variability. We also discover a Fe-K time lag, measuring a delay of 20 seconds between relativistically-blurred reflection off the inner accretion flow and the hard X-ray continuum emission.

sources.summary: The paper presents 1H 1934-063 as a radio-quiet NLS1 with a central SMBH, a strong X-ray flux dip, a warm absorber, a stable UFO, and unexplained soft X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: We present a high-resolution spectral analysis of a simultaneous \ and \ observation of the narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) AGN 1H 1934-063, during which the X-ray flux dropped by a factor of 6 and subsequently recovered within 140 kiloseconds. By means of the time-resolved and flux-resolved X-ray spectroscopy, we discover a potentially variable warm absorber and a relatively stable ultra-fast outflow (UFO, v_ UFO 0.075 ) with a mild ionization state ( ( / erg cm s^ 1 ) 1.6 ). The detected emission lines (especially a strong and broad feature around 1 keV) are of unknown origin and cannot be explained with emission from plasmas in photo- or collisional-ionization equilibrium. Such emission lines could be well described by a strongly blueshifted ( z 0.3 ) secondary reflection off the base of the equatorial outflows, which may reveal the link between the reprocessing of the inner accretion flow photons and the ejection.

1H 1934-063 (hereafter 1H 1934; also known as IGR J19378-0617, SS 442 and IRAS 19348-0619) is a radio-quiet NLS1 2011Panessa galaxy at a redshift of z=0.0102 2008Rodriguez , which ranked seventh in 10 - 20 ks excess variance among 161 AGN in the \ archive CAIXA; 2012Ponti . It hosts a central supermassive black hole (SMBH) with a mass of M_ BH =3 10^6 M_ , estimated from FWHM H 2000Rodriguez-Ardila,2008Malizia .

sources.summary: The paper presents IGR J19378-0617 as a gamma-ray-selected AGN and bona-fide NLS1, notes prior classification as 1H1934-063, and estimates near-Eddington accretion.

quotes.quote: In this work we present a sample of 5 NLS1 (3 bona-fide and 2 candidates): IGR J14552-5133, IGR J16185-5928, IGR J16385-2057, IGR J19378-0617 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654. All 5 objects are gamma-ray selected AGNs discovered by INTEGRAL /IBIS and Swift/BAT; all 5 are reported in the last INTEGRAL /IBIS survey catalogue (Bird et al. 2007). The large (arcmin) gamma-ray error box has been reduced to arcsec uncertainty using X-ray follow-up observations performed by the Swift/XRT telescope (Malizia et al. 2007, Rodriguez et al. 2008 and this work)

Overall we can conclude saying that 3 objects (IGR J16385-2057, IGR J19378 and Swift J21247.4+5654) in our sample are bona-fide NLS1 while the remaining 2 can be considered at this stage only NLS1 candidates with IGR J14552-5133 probably a more convincing case than IGR J16185-5928. Clearly more detailed optical spectroscopy is necessary and encouraged to refine and confirm the optical classification of these last two objects.

We obtained 1.3, 0.5, 1.2, 0.9, 0.4 for IGR J14552-5133, IGR J16185-5928, IGR J16385-2057, IGR J19378-0617 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654 respectively; these values support the claim that our NLS1 accrete close to the Eddington rate, as expected (e.g. Boroson Green 1992).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 1934 603 as a fast-variable NLS1 with a reported reverberation lag, prior supersolar iron abundance, and a visible broad iron-band emission line.

quotes.quote: 1H 1934 603 1H 1934 603 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy NLS1, nagao01 that shows fast variability in the X-ray band. Previously by analysing the archival \ and \ observations of 1H 1934 603, frederick18 discovered that the disc reflection component lags behind the coronal power-law continuum by 20 s. By conducting a novel spectral analysis using a fixed disc density reflection model, a super solar iron abundance ( Z_ Fe >9Z_ ) is required for the reflection spectral modelling frederick18 . A ratio plot for an averaged EPIC-pn spectrum of 1H 1934 603 against an absorbed power-law model is shown in Figure pic_fe . A broad emission line feature is visible in the iron band.

sources.summary: The paper says J1937 0613 has diffuse radio emission around the core, low luminosity and black-hole mass, steep spectrum, and optical spiral morphology matching the radio emission.

quotes.quote: J1937 0613 This target exhibits diffuse emission around the central core at kpc-scale (Fig. w right panel) with a flux density of S_ int 12.12 mJy. The luminosity and black hole mass are L_ int 38.20 and (M_ BH / M_ ) 6.03 respectively, which are lower than the average values. It was also detected at 1.4 GHz associating with a counterpart of NVSS J193733 061304. The flux density and luminosity are S_ 1.4 42.2 mJy and L_ 1.4 38.15 respectively. The spectrum is steep with _ in-band 0.64 and _ 1.4-5.5 0.91.

sources.summary: J1937 is classified as complex because a power law fits badly and the broad-band spectrum shows variability, soft excess, relativistic reflection, broad Fe K, and a Compton hump.

quotes.quote: J1937 (IRAS 19348-0619) We classify J1937 as a C-NLS1 since a simple power-law can not reproduce the data between 2-12 keV resulting in a very bad spectral fit of ^2_ = 3.25. Indeed, its broad-band X-ray spectrum (0.3-60.0 keV) exhibits extremely complex features, such as dramatic X-ray variability, strong soft excess below 2 keV, evident relativistic reflection, broad Fe K line at around 6-7 keV, and Compton hump above 10 keV Frederick2018 . The combined XMM-Newton EPIC-PN (0.3−10.0 keV) and NuSTAR FPMA+FPMB (3.0−60.0 keV) time-integrated spectra of J1937 can be modeled by a Galactic absorption, a multi-blackbody accretion disk, and a relativistic reflection model for a lamp post geometry as well as an incident cutoff power-law continuum. Details are analyzed by Frederick2018 .

sources.summary: The paper classifies IGR J19378-0617 as an NLSy1 and highlights its prominent soft spectrum with absorption edges and significant intrinsic absorption.

quotes.quote: This is the case of IGR J16185-5928 and IGR J19378-0617 with H _ > 2000 km s ^ 1 but classified as NLSy1 based on their [ OIII ] 5007/H _ and FeII/H _ ratios.

The most complex and prominent soft spectrum is shown by IGR J19378-0617 where two absorption edges are needed to model the spectral features at 1.23 0.03 keV ( =0.13 0.02) and 1.57 0.03 keV ( =0.16 0.03); such features have been previously detected in AGN and interpreted in terms of highly blue-shifted absorption material, resonance absorption by Fe L (see Leighly 1999), as expected from partially ionized absorption by a warm absorber and/or reflection off the accretion disc.

Intrinsic absorption was measured in four sources of the sample, in three objects it partially covers the continuum and in IGR J19378-0617 this value is significantly high ( 3 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 ), similar to the amount of obscuration measured in NGC 5506.

gmw_00003077.name: Mrk 1310
gmw_00003077.overview: Mrk 1310 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1908.07272, arXiv:2208.03880] that exhibits a flat X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2311.13135] and a strong soft excess [arXiv:1908.07272]. Its nuclear region is associated with millimeter-wave emission [arXiv:2208.03880] and displays a short H-alpha time lag [arXiv:1910.01061].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1310 as a Seyfert 1 with one XMM observation, weak iron-band evidence, and a strong soft excess fit by high-density disc reflection.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1310 Mrk 1310 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 and has only one \ observation with a net pn exposure of 35 ks. The iron band does not show strong evidence for emission features. A power-law model can offer a very good fit for the spectra between 3--10 keV with C-stat/ =52.02/46.

In the soft band, Mrk 1310 however shows a strong soft excess, as in other AGN in our sample. We model the broad band spectrum of Mrk 1310 with MODEL2 . MODEL2 offers a very good fit with C-stat/ =109.90/94 . The best-fit model and corresponding ratio plot are shown in Figure pic_final1 . The high density disc reflection component accounts for the soft excess emission.

sources.summary: The paper monitors Mrk 1310 with medium-band photometry, finding the shortest H time lag in the sample and consistency with previous reverberation results.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1310 It has the shortest time lag among five targets. The peak of the DCF is 1 day and the JAVELIN result shows a broad lag distribution. Ignoring the weak second peak and using the lag distribution less than 18 days we find the time lag of 1.5^ +8.2 _ 3.3 days. The large error is not surprising, considering our average sampling interval of 3 days.

In comparison, 2010ApJ...716..993B find the time lag between H and the V band continuum is 4.5^ +0.7 _ 0.6 days. Their time lag result with respect to B band continuum is also of order of 4.6^ +0.7 _ 0.6 days. Our time lag is consistent with the 2010ApJ...716..993B result within error.

sources.summary: The paper reports that Mrk 1310 light curves are noisier than Arp 151, but its inversion is stable, with a peaked integrated response and a 2.7-day median delay.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1310 Results for Mrk 1310 are presented in Fig. fig:result-mrk1310 . There is more scatter in the light curves of Mrk 1310 compared to Arp 151. Because of the smoothing constraint, RLI is not able to fit the emission line light curve exactly.

The integrated response function for is more peaked than that of Arp 151. The scatter in the derived response functions from our error analysis is also smaller, indicating that the inversion is stable under perturbations in the input light curves. The median time delay of the response function is = 2.7^ +0.3 _ 0.3 days.

sources.summary: The paper includes Mrk 1310 among luminous AGNs with flat X-ray spectra and uses a smaller thermal-energy fraction in the hybrid-corona fit.

quotes.quote: However, there were four objects (Ton 730, Mrk 290, SBS 1136 + 594, and Mrk 1310) that were not well reproduced because their X-ray spectra are excessively flat. The X-ray spectra of these objects can be fitted by a power-law with photon indices _ 2-10 keV in the range of 1.53 - 1.64 . The accretion rates of these objects are around m 0.04-0.18 . Besides, PG 1138+222 is also included due to its accretion rate m =0.07 and its X-ray spectrum fitted by a power-law with photon index _ X =1.92^ +0.07 _ 0.07 2019MNRAS.487.3884C . In this section, we apply our model to explain the observational spectra of these AGNs in the optical/UV (UVOT) and X-ray ( 2 - 10 keV) wavelengths.

For SBS 1136+594 and Mrk 1310, their observed luminosity ratio L_ 2-10 keV /L_ UVOT are 0.81 and 1.15, respectively. A smaller f_ th number ( 0.2-0.3 ) is adopted to reproduce their flat X-ray spectra.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Mrk 1310 as a type-1 Seyfert whose Gaia position supports associating the offset ALMA mm-wave emission with the nucleus.

quotes.quote: Unlike the result based on the Chandra observations, several objects (NGC 6240, Mrk 1310, NGC 4945, Mrk 520, and NGC 1365) appear to be outliers against a Rayleigh distribution fitted to the entire histogram. Although the reason for this discrepancy for Mrk 1310 is unclear, the mm-wave emission cross-matched with the WISE position possibly originates around the nucleus. This argument is based on the fact that Mrk 1310 is a type-1 Seyfert galaxy, and its optical Gaia position Gai18 , which would locate the nucleus, is close to the mm-wave emission with a separation angle of 0 06. Taking into account the above results, we eventually adopted the mm-wave peaks identified by Chandra and WISE in this order, and then Gaia, particularly for Mrk 1310.

gmw_00003078.name: NGC 4748
gmw_00003078.overview: NGC 4748 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2101.04546] located in an interacting pair [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463] and hosting a low-mass supermassive black hole [arXiv:2101.04546]. The galaxy exhibits circumnuclear star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0605463] and is extremely variable in both soft and hard X-ray emissions [arXiv:2101.04546].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4748 with MCG 02-33-034, summarizes its steep X-ray spectrum and ionized reflection, and fits its continuum and Iron K emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 4748 (z=0.0146) is also known as MCG 02-33-034. It has a steep spectrum with a photon-index =2.50 0.17 2001A&A...368..797P and =2.20 0.11 according to Swift /XRT data 2010MNRAS.403..945L . 2011MNRAS.417.2426P from the analysis of INTEGRAL/IBIS data found for this source a spectrum with =2.01 0.13 and no high energy cut-off, no Iron K emission line and no reflection component. Using the (EPIC and OM), Swift /BAT and INTEGRAL/ISGRI observations Vasylenko2018 found the presence of a strong ionized reflection that could be responsible for the soft-excess component.

NGC 4748 We tested st first the phenomenological Model A. This first fit ( ^2 /dof=531/494) resulted in a steep continuum, =1.93 0.05 with a high energy cut-off which show a lower limit of E_c>152 keV and the reflection fraction which show an upper limit of: R_ refl <0.12 . Moreover the Iron K line shows a flux of 4.29 0.97 10^ 6 ph cm^ 2 s^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4748 as a little-studied NLS1 whose single XMM observation shows a broad iron feature, soft excess, intermediate disc density, and high preferred spin.

quotes.quote: NGC 4748 NGC 4748 is a NLS1 veron06 that is not well studied in the X-ray band. Only one \ observation with a net pn exposure of 26 ks is available in the archive. The spectrum of NGC 4748 in the iron band shows a broad emission feature.

One relativistic reflection model is able to model both the soft excess mission and the broad iron line feature. An intermediate disc density is required (n_ e )=16.6^ +0.4 _ 0.5 . A high BH spin of a_*>0.8 is preferred by our model.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4748 as an extremely variable narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, finding changing X-ray hardness behavior, soft excess emission, and a low-mass SMBH.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed timing and spectral study of an extremely variable narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 4748 using observations in the year 2017 and 2014 performed with AstroSat and XMM-Newton , respectively. Both observations show extremely variable soft and hard X-ray emission that are correlated with each other. In the 2014 data set, the source retains its general behaviour of ``softer when brighter" while the 2017 observation exhibits a ``harder when brighter" nature.

NGC 4748 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy 2009ApJ...705..199B located at a redshift of z=0.014 . It harbours one of the lowest mass SMBH ( M_ BH =2.6_ 1 ^ +1.6 10^ 6 M_ ) among AGNs which makes it interesting and important in terms of its observed variability time scales. This nearby AGN has been found to be highly variable on short and long timescale as seen by XMM-Newton and Swift missions 2018Ap&SS.363..228V, 2017BTSNU..56...18F .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4748 as the only XRT source in the IBIS circle and describes it as an unabsorbed narrow-line Seyfert with radio and ROSAT counterparts.

quotes.quote: NGC 4748 Within the IBIS error circle (see Figure fig11 ), we find only one X-ray source at 24 confidence level, which coincides with NGC 4748, classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy in NED with redshift z=0.01463 . It is reported in the 2MASS Extended catalogue (2MASX J12521245--1324528) and also in the ROSAT Bright Survey (1RXS J125212.5--132450). The XRT localization is also compatible with a NVSS radio source (NVSS J125212--132452) having a 20 cm flux of 14.0 0.6 mJy. A more carefully analysis of the source references in the literature shows that NGC 4748 is better classified as a Narrow Line Seyfert galaxy (V eron-Cetty, V eron Gon calves 2001). The X-ray spectroscopy (see Table tab3 ) indicates an unabsorbed power law having a photon index 2, a 2--10 keV flux of 3.3 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a flux variation of a factor of 1.5 on time-scale of years during the XRT observations, i.e. fully compatible with its Seyfert class.

sources.summary: The paper finds a 12-day DCF peak and 11.9-day JAVELIN lag for NGC 4748, with small variability causing larger lag uncertainty.

quotes.quote: NGC 4748 The peak value in the DCF of NGC 4748 is 12 days. From the JAVELIN analysis, we find 11.9^ +5.3 _ 2.6 days using the time lag distribution from 0.05 to 22.0 days. It shows the smallest magnitude variations among five AGNs. Contrary to the result of NGC4593, the small variability makes the time lag uncertainty larger (discussed in section 6). However, the result is consistent within uncertainty with the previous measurement of 10.8^ +3.1 _ 3.1 days by 2010ApJ...716..993B .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4748 as a worked comparison of rms-spectrum construction, finding weighted and maximum-likelihood rms spectra similar while the mean spectra are nearly identical.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:compare_rms compares rms spectra of NGC 4748 generated with the unweighted rms method, the S/N weighted method, and the maximum likelihood method, after removing two bad epochs as described in 3.2. As expected, the S/N weighted rms spectrum is less noisy than the unweighted rms spectrum. The rms spectrum based on the maximum likelihood method is similar to but slightly noisier than the S/N weighted spectrum. In particular, the maximum likelihood method generates noisy patterns around the [ O 3 ] line region, presumably due to the fact that the error statistics have changed owing to the subtraction of the strong [ O 3 ] line signals. In the case of the mean spectrum, all three methods produce almost identical results.

sources.summary: The atlas treats NGC 4748 as an individually observed active or starburst galaxy, summarizing its prior classification and near-infrared continuum, emission-line, and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 4748 . Classified as NLS1 by goo89 , this object had been previously cataloged as a Seyfert 1/1.5 see for example, odr85 . It resides in an interacting pair, in contact, as evidenced by the H + [ N ii ] image of mwt96 . It displays considerable soft X-ray emission rus96 . The L band spectroscopy of iw04 shows 3.3 m PAH emission in this object, confirming the presence of circumnuclear star formation.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4748 as an NLSy1 with multiple XRT observations, no spectral variation between them, and a soft-to-hard luminosity ratio slightly above unity.

quotes.quote: The optical spectral data of NGC 4051, Mrk 766, NGC 4748 and Mrk 783 have been taken from V e ron-Cetty V e ron (2006) (see this work for detailed references on each source).

1H 0323+342, NGC 4748, Mrk 783 and IGR J14552-5133 have multiple observations with XRT and, to increase the spectral statistics, we fit the data sets together, tying the different parameters only in case of no or little variability.

In NGC 4748, no spectral variation is found between the two 2 ks XRT observations, therefore we tie the XRT photon indeces to the same value in the fitting procedure and report in Table table=best the fluxes and cross-calibration constants.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4748 as a worked FUVT case, showing its max-CCF results, common single-AGN trends, and a mock MBHB spectrum based on it.

quotes.quote: The max-CCF( ) obtained following this procedure are shown in figure fig:CCF1 for NGC 4748.

The trends discussed for NGC 4748 are common for all the nine AGN we examined: CCF is always found in the bulk of the line (hereby defined as the region in between the third and the fifth values of , i.e. when the less luminous part of the BEL has at least 3/8 of the total BEL flux), and it is always 0.75 .

The upper panel of figure fig:shift shows an example of mock MBHB spectrum based on the observations of NGC 4748, without the application of any time shift for clarity purposes, and for a binary with _ orb = 50 yr.

gmw_00003079.name: PG 0804 + 761
gmw_00003079.overview: PG 0804+761 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1908.07272] and a luminous Type 1 quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0504423] that exhibits silicate emission features [arXiv:astro-ph/0504423, arXiv:2405.08960]. Its active nucleus shows a strong soft X-ray excess [arXiv:1908.07272] and lacks absorption variations [arXiv:2104.11263].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0804 + 761 as a Seyfert 1 with usable 2010 observations, two narrow iron lines, strong soft excess, and only an upper limit on disc density.

quotes.quote: PG 0804 + 761 PG 0804 + 761 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 and has three \ observations in the archive. The first observation in 2000 (obsID 0102040401) was entirely dominated by flaring particle background. The other two observations were taken in 2010 and have a total net pn exposure of 32 ks. By fitting the stacked pn spectrum of PG 0804 + 761 with an absorbed power-law model, two strong emission lines are shown in the iron band. A strong soft excess is found below 2 keV.

The relativistic disc reflection model accounts for the soft excess. See Fig. pic_final2 for the best-fit model and corresponding ratio plot. Only an upper limit of the disc density (n_ e )<15.8 is found.

sources.summary: The paper includes PG 0804+761 among five luminous Type 1 PG quasars with prominent 10 and 18 micron silicate emission, notes continuing red-end dust emission, and finds the lowest 18-to-10 feature ratio.

quotes.quote: Here, we report the first spectroscopic detection of both silicate features at high signal to noise in five PG quasars. Quasars are the high luminosity counterparts of Seyfert 1 galaxies, showing the same broad emission lines that define Type 1 AGNs. The five sources presented in this letter (PG 0804+761, PG 1211+143, PG 1351+640, I Zw 1 [ =PG 0050+124 ] and 3C 273 [ =PG 1226+023 ] ) were selected from a sample of 12 AGNs based upon their prominant silicate emission features at both 10 and 18 .

For 3C 273 and PG 0804, the spectral structure at the red end of the IRS spectral range does not show a similar change in slope. This may indicate that dust emission is still contributing at these wavelengths. Therefore, we have assumed that the continuum at the red end runs somewhat below the observed spectrum.

Comparison of the silicate emission spectra in Figure fig2 reveals that the ratio of the 18 and 10 \ features varies significantly within the sample. It is highest for 3C 273 and lowest for PG 0804 (see Table tab2 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses PG0804+762 as its example quasar for power-spectrum and autocorrelation analyses, comparing uninterpolated, fully interpolated, and partially interpolated lightcurve spectra.

quotes.quote: As an example, we show in Figure pg0804spec three power spectra of PG0804+762. Figure pg0804spec a is the power spectrum resulting from the uninterpolated lightcurve. Due to the suppression of the low frequencies, it appears almost like a white-noise spectrum. Figure pg0804spec b is the power spectrum of the fully interpolated lightcurve. Here the higher frequencies are attenuated by the smoothing effect of the interpolation.

Figure pg0804spec shows the power spectrum resulting from our partial interpolation scheme. This power spectrum is similar to the ``fully-interpolated'' spectrum (Fig. pg0804spec b) at low frequencies and to the ``uninterpolated'' spectrum (Fig. pg0804spec a) at high frequencies, with a smooth transition between them.
As an example of the autocorrelation analysis, the ZDCF with its polynomial fit for PG0804+762 is shown in Figure pg0804 .

sources.summary: The paper lists PG 0804+761 among outliers to the proposed dust composition, with residuals near the 10 micron feature and requiring CDEOl1, DL01, and Min3 instead of the proposed olivine components.

quotes.quote: We can see that five objects (Mrk 1210, NGC 7213, PG 0804+761, PG 1211+143, and PG 1351+640) are outliers in this distribution with ^2_ >2 . The middle panel of Figure fig:all_residuals shows the residuals of the spectral fits for our 49 objects using this combination, and it highlights these five objects. Comparing these residuals with the ones obtained in the best fits (top panel), we can see that the main difference is that the latter have larger ones ( >10 ) at 9 m < < 12 m for NGC 7213, PG 0804+761, PG 1211+143 and PG 1351+640.

For NGC 7213, PG 0804+761, PG 1211+143, and PG 1351+640, the residuals of the particular combination differ from the ones of the best fit mainly at 8 m < < 13 m , meaning that the former can not reproduce well the 10 \ m silicate feature in the spectra of these objects. To obtain an acceptable fit model, NGC 7213 needs MieOl2, CDEOl1, CDEOl3, and DL2 instead of MieOl01 and MieOl3; PG 0804+761 needs CDEOl1, DL01, and Min3 instead of MieOl01 and MieOl3; PG 1211+143 needs CDEOl1 instead of MieOl01; and PG 1351+640 needs CDEOl1, DL01, and Min3 instead of MieOl3.

sources.summary: The paper presents FUSE spectroscopy of the bright low-redshift quasar PG 0804 +761, using its sight line to study Galactic and intergalactic absorption and noting an associated absorber near its host environment.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present measurements of absorption lines in the spectrum of the bright low-redshift quasar PG 0804 +761 ( V=15.20, z_ em =0.100 , Schmidt Green 1983) obtained with FUSE in January 2000. The line of sight to PG 0804 +761 in the Galactic direction l=138 3, b=+31 0 passes through local Galactic gas and through the Galactic halo above nearby and distant spiral arms. The FUSE spectrum of PG 0804 +761 thus provides an opportunity to investigate the ISM along a path extending over the outer regions of the Milky Way.

While collisional ionization is favoured for the intervening O vi absorber toward PG 0953+415 (Tripp Savage 2000), the associated system toward PG 0804 +761 could also be photoionized by the quasar itself or other surrounding photon sources in combination with the overall EUV background field. Additional absorption line measurements with higher resolution in combination with optical data are required to study structure, abundances and ionization conditions in the associated system toward PG 0804 +761 in more detail.

sources.summary: The paper groups PG 0804+761 with Sy1 sources lacking absorption variations, preferring smooth gas and dust in a thin dust disk plus thicker gas torus despite possible line-of-sight interception.

quotes.quote: Panel 2 of Figure fig:cartoon_Sy1 (e.g. PG 0804+761 and PG 1211+143): Sy1 sources without absorption variations, small covering factors, small half opening angles of the dust, and low-to-intermediate half opening angles of the gas distribution. A smooth distribution of both dust and gas is preferred for these objects. All the above signatures could be explained with a geometrically-thin and smooth disk of dust and a geometrically-thick and smooth torus of gas, coexisting within the same radii Interestingly, inclination angles might intercept this thin disk along the LOS, despite its optical classification as Sy1.

gmw_00003080.name: PG 1229 + 204
gmw_00003080.overview: PG 1229 + 204 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1908.07272] that exhibits a strong soft X-ray excess and an emission feature in the iron band [arXiv:1908.07272]. Radio observations reveal a southern curved jet or lobe-like structure [arXiv:2301.07929] with magnetic fields aligned parallel to the jet direction [arXiv:2301.07929].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 1229 + 204 as a short-exposure Seyfert 1 with soft excess and uncertain iron-band emission, ultimately better fit with an added distant reflector.

quotes.quote: PG 1229 + 204 PG 1229 + 204 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 and has only one \ observation with a net pn exposure of only 17 ks. Fig. pic_fe shows a ratio plot of the EPIC-pn spectrum of PG 1229 + 204 against an absorbed power-law model. The spectrum shows a strong soft excess below 2 keV and an emission feature in the iron band.

Because of the uncertainty of the nature of the emission feature in the iron band, we first fit the broad band spectrum with MODEL2 to check if the relativistic disc reflection model is able to model both the soft excess emission and the emission feature in the iron band. MODEL2 can offer a good fit with C-stat/ =122.23/129. However there are still residuals in the iron band indicating that the emission feature can not be modelled with a relativistic disc reflection model. Second, we fit the spectrum with MODEL1 , including a distant neutral reflector. MODEL1 is able to improve the fit by C-stat=11 with one more parameter with no structural residuals in the iron band.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG 1229+204 has a southern curved jet or lobe-like structure, matching VLA extension, steep VLA core, and polarization indicating B-fields parallel to the jet.

quotes.quote: The uGMRT images of PG 1229+204 and PG 1244+026 reveal kpc-scale curved jets or lobe-like emission to the south. The VLA image of PG 1229+204 also detects extended emission in the same direction, resembling a curved jet. This extension is weak however, and should be treated with caution. The VLA image of PG 1244+026 reveals only a compact core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image of PG 1229+204 reveals a flat spectrum in the uGMRT core whereas its in-band VLA spectral index image reveals a steep spectrum VLA core. The 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image detects a steep spectrum uGMRT core in PG 1244+026. PG 1426+015 appears compact in both the uGMRT and VLA images, and exhibits a flat spectrum uGMRT core in its 2-frequency uGMRT-VLA spectral index image. The in-band VLA spectral index images of PG 1244+026 and PG 1426+015 reveal flat spectrum in their cores, which could suggest synchrotron self-absorbed bases of small-scale jets (such as on sub-arcsec scales). Within errors, the possibility of flat spectrum ( _ in-band = -0.11 0.08 ) arising from the thermal free-free emission processes from AGN or HII regions cannot be ruled out in PG 1244+026. Radio Polarization Properties of Individual Sources Out of the 11 sources, we detect marginal linear polarization in four sources, viz., PG 0050+124, PG 0923+129, PG 0934+013, and PG 1229+204; this needs confirmation with deeper observations. The fractional polarization varies between 2 and 25 in them. The polarised emission in PG 0050+124 is found to be offset from the core; this could suggest jet-medium interaction. The in-band VLA spectral index image suggests this region to be optically thin. Therefore, the inferred B-fields are perpendicular to the vectors in this region. PG 0923+129 shows two distinctly polarized regions, one in the core and the other slightly offset from the core (annotated as C1 and C2 in Figure fig:2 , top right panel, respectively). The in-band VLA spectral index image reveals the C1 component to be optically thin and the C2 component to be optically thick. Since it is difficult to establish the origin of radio emission (among jet/AGN wind/starburst wind; see Section morphology ) in this source with the current data, we cannot as yet ascertain the relationship between the B-field structures and the direction of the radio outflow. Nevertheless, the polarization structures suggest the presence of a marginally resolved bent jet on the basis of (i) the drastically varying orientation of the EVPA vectors across the polarized knots, and (ii) the suggestion of jets in quasars being threaded by poloidal magnetic fields in the literature Miller12, MehdipourCostantini19 as well as from our previous polarization studies Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . Interestingly, the recent VLBA study by Chen23 has revealed three discrete components in PG 0923+129 at 1.4 and 4.9 GHz. The central component exhibits a flat spectrum, suggesting core emission while the extended components reveal a steep spectrum, most likely arising from a radio outflow. The polarized knots in PG 0934+013 and PG 1229+204 are slightly offset from their radio cores. From their in-band spectral index images, the polarized region is marginally steep in PG 0934+013 and very steep in PG 1229+204, suggesting these regions to be optically thin. The inferred B-fields in PG 0934+013 are transverse to the tentative jet feature to the south. Recent studies of RQ sources with the uGMRT and VLA have reported toroidal B-fields in the kpc-scale radio cores Silpa21a, Silpa21b, Silpa22 . In PG 1229+204, the inferred B fields are parallel to the direction of the southern jet.

Signatures of jet emission are revealed by the uGMRT-VLA study in PG 0923+129, PG 1119+120 and PG 1229+204. The current study cannot discriminate between unresolved jet or lobe emission and emission arising from AGN or starburst-driven wind in PG 0934+013 and PG 1011 040. For the 11 PG RQ quasars studied with the VLA, we estimate the 5 GHz jet kinetic power using the MerloniHeinz07 relation. These values are provided in Table Table3 and they range from 10 ^ 42 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The jets with these powers are typically susceptible to either stellar-mass loading e.g., Komissarov94, Bowman96 or the growth of Kelvin-Helmholtz (KH) instabilities triggered by recollimation shocks/jet-medium interactions e.g., PeruchoMarti07, Perucho14 . In the former scenario, the jets while propagating through the host galaxy, encounter numerous stars that inject matter into the jets via stellar winds. The injected stellar material mixes with the jet plasma, causing the jets to decelerate. Silpa22 had estimated the typical scales of the electron density fluctuations in an inhomogeneous Faraday screen which was most likely causing the depolarization of the lobe emission in a RQ quasar from the Quasar Feedback Survey QFeedS; Jarvis21 sample. Interestingly, the lower limit obtained on those scale sizes ( 10^ 5 parsec) matches the sizes of red giant stars, thereby suggesting the possibility of the stellar-mass loading effect in RQ quasars. KH instabilities also facilitate entrainment and mixing between the ambient gas and the jet plasma, causing the jets to decelerate and decollimate. KH instabilities could also result in jet bending e.g., Hardee87, Savolainen06 . Knotty polarization structures and the presence of poloidal B fields are typical signatures of KH instabilities e.g., in the `Teacup' quasar Mukherjee20, Silpa22 . Polarization is detected as discrete knots in our sources. The EVPA vectors within each knot have the same orientation while they differ between knots. Moreover, poloidal B-fields are inferred in the jet of PG 1229+204.

gmw_00003081.name: Swift J2127.4 + 5654
gmw_00003081.overview: Swift J2127.4 + 5654 is a radio-quiet [arXiv:2101.01497] Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1805.11314] located at a redshift of z = 0.0144 [arXiv:2101.01497]. It hosts a supermassive black hole with an intermediate spin [arXiv:1410.3357] and exhibits a highly ionized outflow [arXiv:2005.03307] alongside variable X-ray emission [arXiv:1807.11445].

sources.summary: The paper describes Swift J2127.4 + 5654 as a well-studied Seyfert 1 with broad and narrow Fe K emission, soft excess, reverberation lags, and high inferred disc density.

quotes.quote: Swift J2127.4 + 5654 Swift J2127.4 + 5654 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 that has been well studied in the X-ray band. miniutti09 analysed the \ observations of this source and obtained a BH spin of a_*=0.6 0.2 . This result has been confirmed by marinucci14 as well where \ observations are considered. A combination of broad Fe K emission line and a narrow core is found in the iron band. The \ spectrum of Swift J2127.4 + 5654 shows a blackbody-shaped soft excess miniutti09 .

An intermediate spin of a_*=0.72^ +0.14 _ 0.20 is found and is consistent with previous spin measurements e.g. miniutti09,marinucci14 . A higher inclination angle ( i=67^ +3 _ 2 ^ ) is found by fitting with a high density disc reflection model compared with previous analysis e.g. 40 ^ , miniutti09 . A lower limit of the disc density ( (n_ e )>18.7 ) is obtained, indicating a potential high disc density.

sources.summary: The paper studies SWIFT J2127.4+5654 as a radio-quiet NLS1 whose Ecut-photon-index relation shows a break-shaped pattern and weak pair-production behavior.

quotes.quote: While in E_ cut monotonically increases with , the E_ cut - relation exhibits a shape in SWIFT J2127.4+5654 ( E_ cut increasing with at 2.05, but reversely decreasing at 2.05), indicating more than a single underlying mechanism is involved. Meanwhile both galaxies show softer spectra while they brighten in X-ray, a common phenomenon in Seyfert galaxies. Plotting all 7 AGNs with E_ cut variations ever reported with observations in the E_ cut - diagram, we find they could be unified with the pattern. Although the sample is small and is the only source with varying across the break point thus the only one exhibiting the complete pattern in a single source, the discoveries shed new light on the coronal physics in AGNs.

Meanwhile, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, a radio-quiet NLS1 Malizia_2008 at z = 0.0144, has been observed by NuSTAR in two campaigns, with four exposures in 2012 and five in 2018. We dropped the observation 60402008002 (not listed in Tab. tab:details ) of SWIFT J2127.4+5654, which has an issue flag = 1, indicating possible contamination from solar activity or other unexpected issues. Marinucci_2014 performed a joint spectral fitting of the four 2012 observations with (quasi-)simultaneous XMM-Newton data and an average = 108^ +11 _ 10 keV was reported.
Consequently the ``cooler-when-softer/brighter'' trend or the pattern only exists in sources lacking strong pair production, like the NLS1 SWIFT J2127.4+5654 and Ark 564.

sources.summary: The paper studies SWIFT J2127.4+5654 as an NLS1 galaxy whose XMM-Newton and NuSTAR timing data show Fe K reverberation and a Compton-hump lag.

quotes.quote: SWIFT J2127.4+5654 ( z=0.0144 ) is a NLS1 galaxy that was first detected with the Swift /BAT in the 15--150 keV band tueller05 .

marinucci14 presented the 300 ks joint XMM-Newton / NuSTAR observation of this source, confirming the broadened Fe K emission line and discovering a clear Compton hump. Also in that work, we analysed the XMM-Newton data and found a high-frequency Fe K reverberation lag that was not as broad spectrally as those found in sources with maximally spinning black holes. This reverberation result independently confirmed a narrower relativistically broadened iron line and a compact X-ray source in SWIFT J2127.4+5654.

We have presented the lag analysis of the joint XMM-NuSTAR observations of SWIFT J2127.4+5654 and NGC 1365. Our main findings are: SWIFT J2127.4+5654, with an intermediate spin black hole, shows a narrower Fe K lag than sources with maximally spinning black hole, and also shows a clear lag associated with the Compton hump. The amplitude of the iron K lag and the Compton hump lag in SWIFT J2127.4+5654 are consistent with each other, and can be well described by a light travel time of 1000 s between the corona and the accretion disc.

sources.summary: The paper includes SWIFT J2127.4+5654 among gamma-ray-selected AGN, treats it as a bona-fide NLS1, and finds cutoff/reflection evidence in broadband fitting.

quotes.quote: In this work we present a sample of 5 NLS1 (3 bona-fide and 2 candidates): IGR J14552-5133, IGR J16185-5928, IGR J16385-2057, IGR J19378-0617 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654. All 5 objects are gamma-ray selected AGNs discovered by INTEGRAL /IBIS and Swift/BAT; all 5 are reported in the last INTEGRAL /IBIS survey catalogue (Bird et al. 2007). The large (arcmin) gamma-ray error box has been reduced to arcsec uncertainty using X-ray follow-up observations performed by the Swift/XRT telescope (Malizia et al. 2007, Rodriguez et al. 2008 and this work)

Overall we can conclude saying that 3 objects (IGR J16385-2057, IGR J19378 and Swift J21247.4+5654) in our sample are bona-fide NLS1 while the remaining 2 can be considered at this stage only NLS1 candidates with IGR J14552-5133 probably a more convincing case than IGR J16185-5928. Clearly more detailed optical spectroscopy is necessary and encouraged to refine and confirm the optical classification of these last two objects.

From this analysis we found that a high energy cut-off and a reflection hump is required by the data in IGR J16385-2057 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654 at 99.99 and 98 /97 respectively; the cutoff lies in the range 23-52 keV while the broken power law break occurs at at around 30 keV (see Table 4) as expected in the presence of the a reflection component.

sources.summary: The paper models SWIFT J2127.4+5654 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 with soft X-ray spectrum, cutoff energy near 40 keV, and thermal-corona temperature around 10-20 keV.

quotes.quote: We study X-ray spectra of four Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s), Mrk 110, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, IGR J16185 5928, and WKK 4438, using Suzaku and NuSTAR data. The spectra of the four sources are reproduced with a model consisting of a cutoff power law component, a reflection component, and a soft excess component. The photon indices of all the sources are found to be 2 . The cutoff energies are constrained to be 40 keV for Mrk 110, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, and WKK 4438, whereas a lower limit of 155 keV is obtained for IGR J16185 5928. We find that the NLS1s in our sample have systematically softer spectra and lower cutoff energies than Broad Line Seyfert 1 galaxies reported in the literature.
The fits give kT_ e 10 - 20 keV for Mrk 110 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654, and lower limits of kT_ e > 18 keV for IGR J16185 5928 and kT_ e > 5 keV for WKK 4438.

sources.summary: The paper uses XMM-Newton and NuSTAR flux-flux plots to decompose SWIFT J2127.4+5654 into variable continuum/reflection components plus constant soft and neutral-reflection components.

quotes.quote: We study the flux-flux plots (FFPs) of the Seyfert galaxy SWIFT J2127.4+5654 which was observed simultaneously by XMM-Newton and NuSTAR . The 0.7--2 keV FFPs show a non-linear behaviour, while they are well fitted by a straight line, in the 2-40 keV range. Without any additional modelling, this result strongly suggests that neither absorption nor spectral slope variations can contribute significantly to the observed variability above 2 keV (on time scales 5 days, at least). The FFP method is capable of separating the variable and the constant X-ray spectral components in this source. We found that the low-, average- and high-flux spectra of the variable components are consistent with a power law varying in normalisation only plus a relativistic reflection component varying simultaneously with the continuum.

sources.summary: The paper reports a highly ionized outflow feature for SWIFT J2127.4+5654 and fits Swift J2127.4+5654 spectra with variable inner disc radius and a lamp-post model, finding a constrained coronal height.

quotes.quote: They reported highly ionised outflow features with velocities of 0.185c, 0.231c, 0.007c and 0.061c, respectively in 4C +74.26, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, MCG-6-30-15 and Mrk 766.
We also fitted the spectra of the sources IC 4329A and Swift J2127.4+5654 by varying the inner disc radius instead of fixing it at the innermost stable orbit.

We fitted the spectra of the sources IC 4329A and Swift J2127.4+5654 with relxilllp and obtained the source height in units of gravitational radius R_ g . The height of the corona in Swift J2127.4+5654 is found to be 39(50) R_ g for a=0 (0.998) and in the case of IC 4329A the parameter is poorly constrained and has an upper limit of 23(22) R_ g .

sources.summary: The paper models SWIFT J2127.4+5654 as a bare Seyfert with a high-energy roll-over and possible small ionized-line excesses.

quotes.quote: We methodically model the broad-band Suzaku spectra of a small sample of six 'bare' Seyfert galaxies: Ark 120, Fairall 9, MCG-02-14-009, Mrk 335, NGC 7469 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654.

The cut-off energy for the pexrav component is fixed at 1000 keV, except for NGC 7469 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654 which show indications of a roll-over at high energies, occuring at E_ =119^ +65 _ 31 keV and E_ =49^ +43 _ 14 keV respectively.

In the case of SWIFT J2127.4+5654, for which there are no statistically significant ionized emission lines in Model A, there are small excesses at these energies, indeed suggesting the possible presence of these lines.

sources.summary: The paper finds Swift J2127.4+5654 is the only sample source with a measured low high-energy cut-off and constrained reflection, plus a broad relativistic Fe line.

quotes.quote: A constraint on the high energy cut-off and on the reflection component is achieved only in one source, Swift J2127.4+5654 (E _ cut-off 50 keV, R=1.0 ^ +0.5 _ 0.4 ).
However, the best-fit model for all the sources in the sample is achieved by using a simple power-law model, except for Swift J2127.4+5654 for which a pexrav model better represents data.
A broad relativistic Fe line has been detected in a Suzaku observation of Swift J2127.4+5654 (Miniutti et al.

gmw_00003082.name: UGC 6728
gmw_00003082.overview: UGC 6728 is a local Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1908.07272, arXiv:2308.08528] hosting a low-mass central supermassive black hole [arXiv:1908.07272, arXiv:2311.15104]. This bare nucleus exhibits a variable X-ray spectral slope [arXiv:2311.15104] and a strong soft excess below 2 keV [arXiv:1908.07272].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 6728 as a low-black-hole-mass Seyfert 1 whose MOS spectra show tentative iron features and strong soft excess mainly fit by relativistic reflection.

quotes.quote: UGC 6728 UGC 6728 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy veron06 that has one of the known lowest black hole masses 7.1 10^ 5 M_ , bentz15 . Only one \ observation is available in the archive. The EPIC-pn observation of UGC 6728 is dominated by flaring particle background. Therefore, we use EPIC-MOS observations instead.

The MOS spectra of UGC 6728 show tentative evidence for emission features in the iron band, similar with PG 1426 + 015, and a strong soft excess below 2 keV. We model the broad band spectrum with MODEL2 . The relativistic reflection model accounts mainly for the soft excess.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 15 years of UGC 6728 X-ray observations, finding a bare low-mass AGN with variable spectral slope, SMBH mass constraints, and weak Compton hump evidence.

quotes.quote: We present a comprehensive analysis of approximately 15 years ( 2006-2021 ) of X-ray observations of UGC 6728, a low-mass bare AGN, for the first time. Our study encompasses both spectral and temporal aspects of this source. The spectral properties of this source are studied using various phenomenological and physical models. We conclude that (a) the observed variability in X-ray luminosity is not attributed to the Hydrogen column density ( N_H ) as UGC 6728 exhibits a bare nucleus, implying a negligible N_H contribution along the line of sight, and (b) the spectral slope in the X-ray band demonstrates a systematic variation over time, indicating a transition from a relatively hard state to a comparatively soft state.

By performing X-ray spectral fitting, we estimate the mass of the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) in UGC 6728 to be M_ BH =(7.13 1.23) 10^5 M _ with spin a=0.97^ +0.20 _ 0.27 and inclination angle i=49.5 14.5 degree. Based on our spectral and temporal analysis, we suggest that UGC 6728 lacks a prominent Compton hump or exhibits a very subtle hump that remains undetectable in our analysis.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 6728 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: UGC 6728 is a local (z=0.067) Seyfert 1.2 AGN which is yet to be explored. This source has an ultra-hard X-ray tail (14-195 keV) explored by Bernete2019 . We adopted the black hole mass of the central region as 7.1 10^5 M _ Bentz2016 . XMM-Newton observed UGC 6758 once in 2006 and Swift /XRT observed the AGN multiple times from 2016 to 2021. The observation details are given in Table table:obs_log and the data reduction procedure is discussed in Section sec:RandD .

sources.summary: The paper adds GTC/CanariCam N-band spectra for Seyfert galaxy UGC 6728 and uses pseudo-nuclear spectra, noting the spectrum is noisy but nearly identical to the AGN component.

quotes.quote: New Observations Gran Telescopio CANARIAS/CanariCam We obtained subarcsecond resolution N-band spectra (7.5-13 m) of two Seyfert galaxies (NGC 4138 and UGC 6728) using the low spectral resolution (R 175) grating available in the instrument CanariCam (CC; Telesco03,Packham2005 ), on the 10.4 m Gran Telescopio CANARIAS (GTC). NGC 4138 and UGC 6728 were observed in 2016 March and the slit, of width 0.52 arcsec, was oriented at PA= 145 and 150 degrees, respectively.

Note that we also use the ``pseudo-nuclear'' spectra for NGC 4138, NGC 4945, NGC 7172 and UGC 6728. In the case of NGC 4945 and NGC 7172, their nuclear spectra show a strong contribution from the host galaxy, while those of NGC 4138 and UGC 6728 are very noisy and practically identical in spectral shape to the AGN component.

gmw_00003083.name: J000613-5620.7
gmw_00003083.overview: J000613-5620.7 is a likely high-redshift dusty background galaxy [arXiv:2205.01846]. While a cataloged redshift of 0.2627 belongs to a nearby radio galaxy that may act as a gravitational lens, spectral energy distribution fitting suggests J000613-5620.7 is actually located at a redshift of approximately 4.2 [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper treats J000613-5620.7 as a likely high-redshift dusty background galaxy: its flux ratio argues against the catalog redshift and SED fitting gives z about 4.2.

quotes.quote: In the Everett2020 catalogue, a z=0.2627 is ascribed to J000613-5620.7, which is 5''.6 apart from the SPT position. This redshift was measured for a radio-galaxy, WISEA J000613.47-562042.4. However, the 250 m /1.4 mm flux density ratio of J000613-5620.7 is indicative of a much higher redshift.

The z=0.2627 may belong to the galaxy acting as the lens. The top-left panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo shows the SPT plus SPIRE photometry together with the data on the radio galaxy reported in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED). The global SED can be interpreted as the sum of a background dusty galaxy at z 4.2 with the radio galaxy.

gmw_00003084.name: WISEA J000613.47-562042.4
gmw_00003084.overview: WISEA J000613.47-562042.4 is a foreground radio galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.2627 [arXiv:2205.01846]. The galaxy exhibits a blazar-like synchrotron component and may act as a gravitational lens for a background dusty galaxy [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper describes WISEA J000613.47-562042.4 as a foreground radio galaxy with a blazar-like synchrotron component that may lens the z about 4.2 dusty source.

quotes.quote: This redshift was measured for a radio-galaxy, WISEA J000613.47-562042.4. However, the 250 m /1.4 mm flux density ratio of J000613-5620.7 is indicative of a much higher redshift. The z=0.2627 may belong to the galaxy acting as the lens. The top-left panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo shows the SPT plus SPIRE photometry together with the data on the radio galaxy reported in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED). The global SED can be interpreted as the sum of a background dusty galaxy at z 4.2 with the radio galaxy.

The synchrotron component of the latter has a blazar SED, which, in the considered frequency range, can be represented as Massardi2022 : S_ =A/ _0 ^ + _0 ^ , with 0.5 and > 1 . The fit shown has = 0.15 , = 1.20 , and ( _0/ Hz ) 12.9 . Note that, here and in the following, we do not include in fit the sub-GHz (SUMSS or RACS) flux densities because they are frequently affected by self-absorption or by excesses due to other components. The blazar dominates the 95 GHz flux density and contributes substantially also at 150 GHz.

gmw_00003085.name: J015539 5829.1
gmw_00003085.overview: J015539 5829.1 is a galaxy associated with the radio source PMN J0155 5829 and the galaxy WISEA J015539.00-582859.9 [arXiv:2205.01846]. It exhibits a blazar-shaped global spectral energy distribution and has a near-infrared-optical excess likely originating from its host galaxy [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J015539 5829.1 with radio and WISEA counterparts, finds a blazar-shaped global SED, and removes it from the dusty high-redshift sample.

quotes.quote: The source J015539 5829.1 may be identified with the radio source PMN J0155 5829 (separation of 10.7'' ), with a flux density of 44 8 mJy at 4.85 GHz, and with the galaxy WISEA J015539.00-582859.9 (separation of 10'' ). The global SED has a blazar shape [ Equation ( eq:blazar ) ] with 0.38 , = 1.20 , and ( _0/ Hz ) 13.3 ; therefore we have removed it from our sample. The NIR-optical excess, likely due to the host galaxy, is fitted by a SWIRE Ell2 template at z 0.44 with a bolometric luminosity (L_ bol /L_ ) 11.7 (see the top-middle panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo ).

gmw_00003086.name: J032837-6447.4
gmw_00003086.overview: The galaxy J032837-6447.4 is located approximately 10 arcseconds away from WISEA J032839.00-644728.6 [arXiv:2205.01846]. If identified with this nearby source, it has an estimated photometric redshift of about 1.0 and is likely an unlensed ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper says J032837-6447.4 may be identified with WISEA J032839.00-644728.6, whose SED fit gives z about 1.0 and qualifies it as a ULIRG.

quotes.quote: J032837-6447.4 is 10'' away from the galaxy WISEA J032839.00-644728.6. If this galaxy can be identified with the SPT source, a fit using the SWIRE Spi4 SED yields a photometric redshift z 1.0 (see the top-right panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo ) and (L_ IR /L_ ) 12.7 , qualifying it as an ultra-luminous IR galaxy (ULIRG).

We have included the 3 additional likely strongly lensed sources with photometric redshift estimates in the redshift distribution for S_ 1.4 mm, best 20 mJy (J032837 6447.4 is probably an unlensed ULIRG).

gmw_00003087.name: J201445 4152.0
gmw_00003087.overview: J201445 4152.0 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy detected in all three SPIRE bands [arXiv:2205.01846]. Its spectral energy distribution is well fitted by a proto-spheroidal model at a redshift of approximately 3.6, and it is located near a possible gravitational lens galaxy [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper describes J201445 4152.0 as SPIRE-detected and well fit by a z about 3.6 proto-spheroidal SED, with a nearby possible lens galaxy.

quotes.quote: J201445 4152.0 was detected in all three SPIRE bands (nominal separations from the SPT position in the range 10''.2 - 11''.7 , depending on the SPIRE band). The SPT and SPIRE data are reasonably well fitted by the Cai2013 proto-spheroidal SED at z 3.6 , with ( L_ IR /L_ ) 13.7 . It is 11''.5 away from WISEA J201446.90-415207.3, which may be the lens. Its SED is fitted by the SWIRE TQSO1 template at z 0.5 with (L_ bol /L_ ) 11.2 (see the bottom-left panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo ).

gmw_00003088.name: J213230-4537.8
gmw_00003088.overview: J213230-4537.8 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy with an estimated redshift of approximately 3.9 [arXiv:2205.01846]. Its spectral energy distribution is consistent with the source WISEA J213231.44-453749.0, and its 95 GHz flux density may be affected by a nearby blazar [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper fits J213230-4537.8 as a z about 3.9 infrared source consistent with a WISEA identification, while a nearby blazar likely affects mainly 95 GHz flux.

quotes.quote: For J213230-4537.8 we found a SPIRE detection at 250 m (separation of 21'' ). Our fit of the SPT photometry at > 95 GHz and of the SPIRE photometry yielded z 3.9 and ( L_ IR /L_ ) 13.7 ; the fitting SED is fully consistent with the WISE flux densities of WISEA J213231.44-453749.0 (separation of 13'' from the nominal SPT position), which is the likely identification (see the bottom-middle panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo ). This galaxy is 37''.2 away from WISEA J213228.32-453738.2, which may be identified with the blazar AT20G J213227-453740 (the separation among the nominal positions of the two objects is 2''.9 ). Thus the blazar lies within the FWHM of the 95 GHz beam ( FWHM =1'.7 ) and can therefore account for the flux density excess at this frequency (it has a flux density of 73 4 mJy at 20 GHz). Its continuum spectrum, however, must drop rapidly above 20 GHz (the SPT flux density at 95 GHz is 7.3 2.5 mJy) and its observed flux density is substantially attenuated at the higher SPT frequencies because of the lower FWHM's; it is thus likely that the blazar contribution at these frequencies is small.

gmw_00003089.name: J232216-4836.2
gmw_00003089.overview: J232216-4836.2 is a blazar identified by its spectral energy distribution [arXiv:2205.01846]. The object is located 2.2 arcseconds away from the radio source RACS-DR1 J232215.9-483615, which is itself associated with WISEA J232215.92-483616.2 [arXiv:2205.01846].

sources.summary: The paper links J232216-4836.2 to nearby RACS and WISEA radio counterparts, finds a blazar SED, and excludes it from the dusty-galaxy sample.

quotes.quote: J232216-4836.2 is 2''.2 away from the radio source RACS-DR1 J232215.9-483615 McConnell2020, Hale2021 with a flux density of 11.0 0.3 mJy/beam at 0.855 GHz. In turn, the RACS source is 1''.4 apart from WISEA J232215.92-483616.2. Identifying the 3 sources we get the SED shown in the bottom-right panel of Figure fig:e20_fit_z_photo , indicating that this source is a blazar. It was therefore excluded from our sample.

gmw_00003090.name: source 02
gmw_00003090.overview: Source 02 is a low-luminosity radio galaxy [arXiv:1406.5255]. Its photometric redshift matches a detected overdensity, and two additional field overdensities are located at redshifts of 0.66 and 3.94 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper uses source 02 as a PPM example: its photometric redshift matches an overdensity, while two additional field overdensities appear at different redshifts.

quotes.quote: In Panel (a) we report the PPM plot for the LLRG 02. The photometric redshift of source 02 and that estimated for the overdensity perfectly match. Other two overdensities are detected in the field of 02 at redshifts z=0.66 and 3.94 , respectively. They are clearly identified at their estimated redshift by visual inspection of the PPM plot. Interestingly, the lower redshift cluster is present ( 20 arcsec far from the location of our FR I) in both the z 1 group catalogs of , who estimated a redshift of z=0.69 for the overdensity.

gmw_00003091.name: source 03
gmw_00003091.overview: Source 03 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2 [arXiv:1406.5255]. It is associated with two distinct Mpc-scale overdensities [arXiv:1406.5255] and exhibits a strongly absorbed X-ray spectrum [arXiv:1110.4631].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 03 as a z~2 HLRG with two associated overdensities, cross-matched to group/protocluster catalogs and possibly part of a larger structure.

quotes.quote: In Panel (b) we report a similar example for the z 2 HLRG 03. Despite the high photometric uncertainties for this source two distinct overdensities are clearly detected within the redshift uncertainties of the source 03 at z=1.82 and 2.39 , respectively. Another overdensity is detected at z=0.56 , as also clearly identified by visual inspection of the plot. Interestingly, it is present (with an angular offset of 20 arcsec from the coordinates of our FR I) in the z 1 group catalogs of , who estimated a redshift of z=0.66 for the overdensity.

The cluster candidate associated with our source 03 is also present in the proto-cluster and group catalog of . They estimated a redshift of 2.44, that is in good agreement with our estimate ( z=2.39 ) for one of the two Mpc-scale overdensities associated with the source 03. spitler2012 found a cluster candidate that is about 3.8-5.4 arcmin from the source 03. The authors estimated a redshift of z = 2.2 , on the basis of photometric redshift information.

sources.summary: The paper identifies FRI 3 as the best-count X-ray source among the FRI candidates and finds a strongly absorbed spectrum with intrinsic column density near 10^23 cm^-2.

quotes.quote: All the examined sources are consistent with being absorbed at the level of a few 10 ^ 22 cm ^ 2 , with source FRI 3 having N_H= 3.5 0.5 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 . We further explore the spectrum of FRI 3, which is the source with the largest number of counts. We relax the constraints on the intrinsic power law , finding = 1.6 0.8 and N_H= 2.6 0.4 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 , with a slight improvement in the quality of the fit.

gmw_00003092.name: source 05
gmw_00003092.overview: The object source 05 is a high-redshift radio galaxy that exhibits a complex radio morphology [arXiv:1406.5255]. It resides in a Mpc-scale overdensity at a redshift of approximately z~2, which is 1.7 times denser than the mean COSMOS density [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 05 as a confused high-redshift radio galaxy field, using higher-resolution VLA-COSMOS imaging and finding it resides in a significant z~2 overdensity.

quotes.quote: For these cases we consider the VLA COSMOS maps to clearly identify the radio sources, as described in the following for source 05. In Figure fig:FRI_005_NVSSmap we show the NVSS radio map of the field around the object 05. Visual inspection reveals the presence of a complex radio morphology, which might be (erroneously) identified with either the narrow-angle tail NAT, e.g. NGC 1265, Odea_Owen1986 or the wide-angle tail radio morphology. The NVSS catalog reports sources at distance of 60 and 67 arcsec to the SW and SE from the VLA-COSMOS coordinates of the source 05, and fluxes of 3.4 and 3.7 mJy, respectively.

C10 suggested the presence of overdensities around three of our highest redshift sources, namely sources 03, 05, 226. Based on galaxy number counts, the authors found that the Mpc-scale environments of these source are 1.7 times denser with respect to the mean COSMOS density. They translated this into a 4- overdensity significance. Interestingly, we find this is in full agreement with our results, since we find that all of the three sources reside in high significance ( 2.5 ) and high redshift ( z 2 ) Mpc-scale overdensities.

gmw_00003093.name: source 25
gmw_00003093.overview: Source 25 is a low-luminosity radio galaxy classified as a Fanaroff-Riley class I source [arXiv:1406.5255]. The field surrounding the galaxy contains multiple projected overdensities, including two cluster candidates at redshifts of 1.23 and 1.37 that are physically associated with the radio source [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper finds multiple projected overdensities around LLRG source 25, with the two redshifts matching the radio galaxy interpreted as real or one cluster candidate.

quotes.quote: In Panel () we report the PPM plot for the LLRG 25. A clear overdense (i.e. 2 ) region extends in the PPM plot from z_ centroid = 0.40 to z_ centroid =1.51 . Due to such a large redshift range we interpret the overdense region in the plot as due to a projection effect, where multiple overdensities are present in the field of 25 at different redshifts. Our peak finding procedure detects in fact four overdensities within such a redshift interval, at z=0.46 , 0.80, 1.23 and 1.37, respectively. Only the last two redshifts agree with the redshift of the radio galaxy, consistently with our association criterion.

As mentioned above, two overdensities are associated with the source 25 (see also Figure fig:ppm_plots , panel ). They have similar significances ( 2.5 ) and they are also both detected starting from 50 arcsec from the location of the FR I. Such an angular separation corresponds to 400 kpc at the redshift of the LLRG.

gmw_00003094.name: source 28
gmw_00003094.overview: Source 28 is a radio galaxy with a high photometric redshift [arXiv:1406.5255]. It is associated with two Mpc-scale environmental overdensities detected at redshifts of z=2.71 and 2.98 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper keeps source 28 despite its formal high photometric redshift, finding two associated overdensities near z=2.7-3.0 and discussing whether they are distinct structures.

quotes.quote: Conversely, we do not exclude those sources (e.g. 28 and 32) that have a photometric redshift formally above z 2 . This is because, even if they are at redshifts well outside the fiducial range of our interest, they were not rejected during the C09 selection. Therefore, they could comprise similar properties to those of the other galaxies in our sample. Furthermore, since such sources populate the high redshift tail of our sample, their Mpc-scale environments are still worth to investigate (see also Sect. sec:papovich_test for further discussion about source 28).

Two overdensities are associated with the source 28. They are detected at similar (but different) redshifts z=2.71 and 2.98, and with similar significances ( 2.0-2.5 ). We also estimate similar sizes for both of them (i.e. 0.8-1.0 Mpc, see Table tab:PPM_results ) However, we find that the overdensity at the lower redshift starts to be detected from 87 arcsec from the radio galaxy. This corresponds to 700 kpc at the redshift of the overdensity.

gmw_00003095.name: source 01
gmw_00003095.overview: Source 01 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.88 [arXiv:1406.5255]. It resides within a compact rich group, where a nearby bright arc is likely strongly lensed by the dark matter halo of the galaxy pair [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper describes source 01 at z=0.88 in a compact rich group, near a bright arc that may be strongly lensed by the galaxy pair or group halo.

quotes.quote: A bright arc in the field of 01 In this section we discuss the serendipitous discovery of a bright arc detected with the ACS camera on board of HST in the in the field of the source 01, at z_ spec =0.88 . In Figure fig:acs_01_zoom we report the ACS image koekemoer07 of the field of 01. The source 01 and the arc are marked in Figure with the left and the right ellipses, respectively. The arc is clearly visible about 5 arcsec westward of the pair formed by the radio galaxy host and a larger elliptical companion. Such a projected angular separation corresponds to 39 kpc at the redshift of the source.

The arc is very close to the radio galaxy, and it resides within the core of the Mpc--scale overdensity associated with the source 01. Strong lensing phenomena are expected to be originated close to the densest regions of dark matter halos. Since such a projected separation is consistent with the typical size i.e. 60 kpc, halkola2007 of the dark matter halos of BCGs, it is likely that the arc is originated by the dark matter halo of the galaxy pair.

gmw_00003096.name: source 31
gmw_00003096.overview: COSMOS-FRI 31 is a radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.91 [arXiv:1406.5255]. Color-color plots and spectral energy distribution modeling indicate that the galaxy is star-forming [arXiv:1812.09997], and it resides within a relatively low-mass group [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper includes COSMOS-FRI 31 among the COSMOS radio-galaxy targets, reporting marginal WISE W4 photometry, 12.4 hr of IRAM observing, and color/SED evidence near the star-forming boundary.

quotes.quote: COSMOS-FRI 31, in the W4 channel, and COSMOS-FRI 16, in both W3 and W4 channels, are only marginally detected by WISE, at S/N<2. We therefore converted the associated fluxes into 3 upper limits. In Table tab:COSMOS_FRI_photometry we summarize the IR-to-UV photometry of COSMOS-FRI 16, 31, and 70.

The five target sources were observed for an on-source observing time of 71 hr in total, distributed among the five sources as follows: 6.8 hr (DES-RG 399), 17.8 hr (DES-RG 708), 14.0 hr (COSMOS-FRI 16), 12.4 hr (COSMOS-FRI 31), and 20.1 hr (COSMOS-FRI 70). Sources COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31 were observed during August 17-22, 2016, in bad weather conditions. Observations were carried out with an average precipitation water vapor (pwv) value of 12 mm, as well as high average system temperatures T_ sys = 615 K and 898 K, for COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31, respectively.

COSMOS-FRI 16 and 31 are formally at the edge of the dividing line separating quiescent from star-forming galaxies, while COSMOS-FRI 70 is safely classified as a star-forming galaxy, according to the color-color plots. These results are consistent with those found by the SED modeling, which suggests that the three COSMOS-FRIs are indeed star forming, on the basis of their FIR and UV emission.

sources.summary: The paper notes a group at the spectroscopic redshift of FR I source 31, but says PPM does not recover the relatively low-mass system.

quotes.quote: A group is also present in the field of our source 31 at an estimated redshift z=0.91 in . This is exactly the spectroscopic redshift of the FR I. Based on spectroscopic redshifts, associated only two members with this group. They also estimated a relatively low mass of M =8.9 10^ 12 M _ . The PPM does not find this group.

gmw_00003097.name: source 224
gmw_00003097.overview: Source 224 is a low-luminosity radio galaxy with a photometric redshift of 1.10 [arXiv:1406.5255]. Its field contains three detected overdensities at redshifts of 0.46, 2.58, and 3.88 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 224 as an LLRG at photometric redshift 1.10 and finds three field overdensities at other redshifts in its PPM plot.

quotes.quote: Source 224 is a LLRG with a photometric redshift z=1.10^ +0.10 _ 0.04 . In Figure fig:ppm_plots (panel d), we report the corresponding PPM plot. Three overdensities are detected in its field at redshifts z=0.46 , 2.58, and 3.88, respectively. There high significance patterns are in fact clearly visible in the PPM plot. Their significance levels are 2.3 , 2.5 , and 2.6 .

gmw_00003098.name: source 234
gmw_00003098.overview: Source 234 is a radio galaxy located in the COSMOS field [arXiv:1406.5255]. It is associated with a 2.5-sigma overdense region of sources at a redshift of approximately 0.93 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper finds a 2.5-sigma overdensity near radio galaxy 234 only with the clean catalog, attributing the discrepancy to masked foreground sources.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we find evidence for a 2.5 overdense region associated with the radio galaxy 234 only if the clean catalog is adopted, while no overdensity is found if the complete I09 catalog is adopted. We visually inspect the HST image of that field and verify that some sources have been masked southern of the location of source 234 because they are most likely foreground bright sources. We also find evidence for a segregation of z 0.93 sources in the proximity of the radio galaxy 234. We believe that the discrepancy in adopting the two I09 catalogs is due to the fact that the estimated mean number density of the COSMOS field is lower if the clean catalog is adopted rather than if the full catalog is considered, while the number of masked sources in the field of 234 is low enough to detect the overdensity only if the clean catalog is used. For the sake of completeness, we report the overdensity associated with source 234 in Table tab:PPM_results .

gmw_00003099.name: source 30
gmw_00003099.overview: Source 30 is a low-luminosity radio galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 1.06 [arXiv:1406.5255]. Three field overdensities have been detected around the galaxy at higher estimated redshifts of 1.36, 1.82, and 2.30 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper describes source 30 as an LLRG with three detected field overdensities at redshifts higher than the radio galaxy photometric redshift.

quotes.quote: Source 30 is a LLRG with a photometric redshift z=1.06^ +0.11 _ 0.07 . Three overdensities are found in its fields. Their estimated redshifts are z=1.36 , 1.82, and 2.30, respectively. Their detection significances are 2.0 , 2.0 , 2.7 .

gmw_00003100.name: source 258
gmw_00003100.overview: Source 258 is a low-luminosity radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.9009 [arXiv:1406.5255]. Four field overdensities have been detected in its direction at higher redshifts ranging from 2.07 to 3.24 [arXiv:1406.5255].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 258 as an LLRG at spectroscopic redshift 0.9009 with four detected field overdensities at substantially higher redshifts.

quotes.quote: Source 258 is a LLRG with at z_ spec =0.9009 . Four overdensities are detected in this field at redshifts z=2.07 , 2.40, 3.03, and 3.24, respectively. They are detected with significances of 3.4 , 2.4 , 2.5 , and 2.3 .

gmw_00003101.name: NGC 4030
gmw_00003101.overview: NGC 4030 is an isolated, star-forming spiral galaxy with an intermediate inclination [arXiv:astro-ph/0308489, arXiv:astro-ph/0603682]. Its complex inner structure features multiple spiral arms and a resolved central source, transitioning to two outer spiral arms [arXiv:1204.5599, arXiv:astro-ph/0503693].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes MUSE data for the spiral galaxy NGC 4030, identifying compact forbidden-line sources as supernova-remnant candidates with small global but potentially biased local diagnostic effects.

quotes.quote: NGC 4030 is one of the star-forming galaxies targeted by the MUSE Atlas of Disks (MAD) survey Erroz-Ferrer+19,denBrok+20 , where its effective radius, stellar mass, star formation rate, and gas phase metallicity are estimated to be 31 arcsec (4.4 kpc), 10^ 11.2 M_ , 11 M_ yr^ 1 , and 12 + O/H 9.0 , respectively. More recently, these same data were part of the study by Lopez-Coba+20 on outflows in MUSE galaxies, although no outflow was found for NGC 4030. In this paper, we explore these same MUSE data, but with a very different focus. Inspection of emission-line maps reveals a population of compact sources whose pattern of line emission differs from that of the plethora of regions throughout the disc of NGC 4030.

We have reported the discovery of SNRs based on MUSE integral field spectroscopy of NGC 4030. As the storyline of this paper reveals, this discovery came not from an pre-planned search for SNRs, but from an exploratory investigation on the nature of compact sources with enhanced forbidden line emission (UFLOs) spotted in a cursory examination of emission-line images derived from the data (Fig. \ fig:RGBlines ).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4030 as a complex multi-arm spiral whose young cluster luminosity function may steepen, with central star formation affecting completeness and SFR estimates.

quotes.quote: NGC 4030: The inner part has a complex structure with multiple spiral arms. The main symmetric, two-armed spiral pattern is located in the region 49-70 \ with ( a_2 , i ) = (0.3, 26.5 ).

The histograms provide evidence of neither a significant high luminosity cut-off nor a steepening of CLF slopes for bright clusters, except possibly for young complexes in NGC 4030 and NGC 4321. In the magnitude range where both young and older cluster populations are complete, they have comparable sizes with two exceptions, namely NGC 1566 with an excess of young clusters and NGC 4030, which has a deficiency.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 4030 among four nearby intermediate-inclination spiral galaxies observed with major- and minor-axis long-slit spectroscopy for stellar disk kinematic modeling.

quotes.quote: We observed four galaxies: NGC 1068, NGC 2460, NGC 2775, and NGC 4030. All four are nearby, spiral galaxies of intermediate inclination ranging in Hubble type from Sa to Sbc. The main characteristics of each galaxy are listed in Table t:sample . Long-slit spectra were obtained along the photometric major and minor axes of each galaxy, in multiple exposures of 30 minutes each.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a clear central source and surface-brightness excess in NGC 4030, and notes S04 had classified its cluster as resolved.
