The central component has a flatter spectrum, with _ 1.6 GHz ^ 15 GHz = 0.3 Su96,Kukula95,WilsonKeel89 and is close to the position of the optical nucleus, and hence is likely to be the core. The epochs of these observations are August 1993 (1.6 GHz), November 1992 (5 GHz), June 1990 (8.4 GHz) and March 1989 (15 GHz).

gmw_00003199.name: X051--43
gmw_00003199.overview: X051--43 is a likely X-ray source counterpart with a relatively bright optical magnitude of 21.44 [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. Its featureless spectrum suggests it may be an anomalous radio-quiet BL Lacertae object [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper treats X051--43 as a likely X-ray counterpart with a featureless spectrum, suggesting an anomalous radio-quiet BL Lac candidate.

quotes.quote: We also took a spectrum for the relatively bright object ( m_B = 21.44), with LR values greater than 5.5, which is right at the center of the error box of source X051--43. Its spectrum does not show any clear evidence for convincing emission or absorption features, so that, even if we do consider it as the likely counterpart of the X-ray source, we have not been able to spectroscopically classify it. The absence of features in the spectrum suggests that it might be a BL Lac object. If so, it would be a somewhat anomalous BL Lac object. In fact, being not detected in the radio at a flux limit of about 0.2 mJy, it would be classified as a radio quiet BL Lac, with a radio to optical spectral index _ ro < 0.26.

gmw_00003200.name: X043--12
gmw_00003200.overview: X043--12 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.80 [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. It is classified as a high-redshift analogue of a low-redshift type-2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper identifies X043--12 with a single extended/point-like object whose narrow lines imply z=2.80 and a high-redshift type-2 AGN analogue.

quotes.quote: X043--12 : the 95 error box contains only one object, classified as extended in the blue band and as point-like in the V and R bands. Its noisy spectrum shows two narrow lines well coincident with Ly and CIV at z = 2.80. It appears to be a high redshift analogue of the low--z type--2 AGNs, similar to the QSO 2 at z = 2.35 found by Almaini et al. (1995) in the optical follow--up of other deep ROSAT fields. Although a higher quality spectrum would be needed to confirm the nature of this object, given its relatively large LR values, we consider it as the correct identification.

gmw_00003201.name: X211--22
gmw_00003201.overview: X211--22 is an active galaxy classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. Located at a redshift of z = 0.281, it appears point-like in the B band and extended in the R band [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper reports spectra of three nearby objects for X211--22 and selects the z=0.281 Seyfert 1 galaxy as the most likely optical identification.

quotes.quote: X211--22 : we took spectra for three objects, all of them just outside the 95 radius. The brightest one is an early type galaxy at z = 0.180, while the other two, separated by about 2 arcsec from each other, are a Sey 1 galaxy (classified as point--like in the B band and as extended in the R band) at z = 0.281 and a B star. We consider the Sey 1 galaxy as the most likely identification.

gmw_00003202.name: X233--45
gmw_00003202.overview: X233--45 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. It is located at a redshift of z = 1.180 [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper observed two extended candidates near X233--45; one blue spectrum lacked a redshift, while the second object is identified as an AGN at z=1.180.

quotes.quote: X233--45 : we took spectra of the two brightest objects ( m_B 22.5) within 15 from the X-ray position. Both of them are classified as extended. One spectrum is very blue, with low S/N and no redshift was determined; the second object is an AGN at z = 1.180, which we consider to be the identification.

gmw_00003203.name: X022--48
gmw_00003203.overview: X022--48 is a galaxy with a possible redshift of 0.32 based on a narrow [OII] emission line [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. CCD imaging reveals clear signs of interaction between this galaxy and a fainter, blue extended companion located approximately 3 arcseconds to the south [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper links X022--48 to a galaxy with a possible [OII] redshift of 0.32 and clear CCD signs of interaction with a fainter companion.

quotes.quote: X022--48 : the noisy spectrum of the galaxy close to the center of the X-ray error box shows a narrow emission line which, if interpreted as [ OII ] , corresponds to z = 0.32. Both the blue and the red CCDs show clear signs of interaction with a fainter, blue extended object located about 3 south. Also on the basis of the LR values, we consider this complex of interacting galaxies as a likely identification.

gmw_00003204.name: X264--50
gmw_00003204.overview: The object X264--50 is identified as a relatively bright starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.568 [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335]. A much fainter, blue, point-like source nearby may be an active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/9904335].

sources.summary: The paper identifies X264--50 with a relatively bright z=0.568 starburst galaxy, while noting a fainter blue point-like AGN candidate nearby.

quotes.quote: X264--50 : there is one object in the error box with LR 1. It is a relatively bright ( m_R = 20.77) starburst galaxy at z = 0.568. Despite the presence of a much fainter ( m_R 23) blue, point-like source which may be an AGN candidate, we consider the galaxy as a possible identification.

gmw_00003205.name: NGC 4691
gmw_00003205.overview: NGC 4691 is a non-active star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1803.00740] that exhibits an amorphous structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0104087]. Its highly concentrated central dust and starburst activity likely result from a past merger rather than simple bar-driven processes [arXiv:astro-ph/0104087].

sources.summary: NGC 4691 is used as a non-AGN star-forming test case for NebulaBayes, yielding nuclear metallicity, pressure, and ionization-parameter estimates consistent with its stellar mass.

quotes.quote: To demonstrate the capability of NebulaBayes in constraining physical parameters for \ regions, we include an analysis of the nuclear spectrum of the galaxy NGC 4691. This galaxy was included in the S7 sample due to a mis-classification in the source catalogue, and does not show any evidence for optical AGN activity. The results of the analysis are shown in Figure fig:NGC4691_nuc_posterior . The parameter estimates (with 68 credible bounds) are 12 + O/H = 8.74_ 0.05 ^ +0.12 , P/k (cm ^ 3 K) = 6.8_ 1.8 ^ +0.2 and U = -2.8_ 0.2 ^ +0.1 . The approximately Solar nuclear metallicity is reasonable considering the estimated stellar mass of 10^ 9.6 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper derives extinction for NGC 4691, notes blended central knots in MIR maps, and selects H-map pixels to define an equivalent diameter for normalization.

quotes.quote: NGC 4691 - We combined the Br measurement of Pux90 with the H flux integrated within the same region from an H + [ N ii ] map to derive the extinction, and assumed that it remains the same within our larger aperture. The [ N ii ] / (H + [ N ii ] ) ratio in the central hot spots is 0.3 Kee83, Gar95, Gar99 . As the central structure contains multiple knots which are partly blended in the ISOCAM maps, and is not well represented by a single gaussian Rou01a , we strayed from our general definition to determine the area normalization. As the MIR and H emission of NGC 4691 lacks in the disk and is very diffuse outside the central star-forming knots, we simply selected pixels above the 3 brightness level in the H map and added their areas to compute an equivalent diameter.

sources.summary: The paper separates NGC 4691 from normal bar-driven cases, arguing that its central dust concentration and interstellar tracers likely reflect a past merger.

quotes.quote: NGC 4691 and 1022 have an amorphous structure and highly centrally concentrated interstellar tracers. We also qualify that observation by noting that two galaxies, NGC 1022 and NGC 4691, have likely experienced a merger; gas may therefore have sunk to the center as a result of the violent energy dissipation in the merger, and not simply under the influence of the bar, which actually may have been formed during the interaction. We warn that NGC 1022 and NGC 4691 should be considered apart: their dust emission comes almost exclusively from central regions of 1 kpc, but this is more likely due to a past merger than to the influence of the bar, which may have been formed or transformed simultaneously as the starburst event was triggered.

gmw_00003206.name: NGC 520
gmw_00003206.overview: NGC 520 is an intermediate-stage merging galaxy system containing two nuclei embedded within a common stellar envelope [arXiv:astro-ph/0011428, arXiv:1107.3280]. Its primary nucleus is heavily obscured by a dust lane [arXiv:astro-ph/0309377, arXiv:astro-ph/0011428], while the system features northern and southern tidal tails containing young star clusters [arXiv:1504.05585].

sources.summary: The NGC 520 observations reveal a dust-hidden southern nucleus with patchy circumnuclear dust and a clearly visible knotty extended nuclear disk skewed relative to the large dust lane.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 was also observed in two pointings. The NIR observations of the southern nucleus (NGC 520 S) are quite striking (Figure f:fivetwentyavk ), revealing a nucleus completely hidden in the optical behind an enormous dust complex. The J band observations show a considerable amount of dust present around the nuclear region. At longer wavelengths ( H and K ) we peer deeper through the dust toward the nucleus, but even the K image is affected by patchy dust structure around the circumnuclear region.

However, the nucleus is clearly visible, appearing as an extended disk composed of at least seven major knots. The brightest knot, which we identify as the nucleus, is slightly extended toward the north. Interestingly, the nuclear disk, which appears almost edge-on in the E-W direction, is slightly skewed with respect to the large scale dust lane. Such a misalignment is also seen in Centaurus A schr98 .

sources.summary: NGC 520 has a primary nucleus hidden by dust and molecular gas, weak H emission at that position, blue scattered light, and a disrupted post-starburst secondary nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 The southeastern component of NGC 520 is hidden behind a prominent and intricate dust lane (Figure f:n520n1 ). This dust is likely associated with the dense edge-on molecular disk imaged in CO (1--0) by san88b and yun01 . Adopting standard conversion factors, the observed peak CO flux density suggests that the nucleus of NGC 520 is hidden beneath A_V 300 magnitudes of extinction. It is thus not surprising that the position of the primary nucleus is impossible to determine from our data.

Practically no H emission is seen in our H image at the position of the primary nucleus. Red V-I colors are seen along most of the primary nucleus. However, at the location of the most dust-affected regions, as seen in the I image, we see relatively blue colors of 0.8 in the color index image. Again, as in the case of NGC 4676A, we interpret this as scattering of emission from young stars, hinted at in the V band image (not shown here).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 520 as a merging system with northern and southern tidal tails, using HST imaging to study tail clusters and compare their ages.

quotes.quote: We study the stellar content in the tidal tails of three nearby merging galaxies, NGC 520, NGC 2623, and NGC 3256, using BVI imaging taken with the Advanced Camera for Surveys on board the Hubble Space Telescope . The tidal tails in all three systems contain compact and fairly massive young star clusters, embedded in a sea of diffuse, unresolved stellar light.

Figure sample shows that NGC 520 has two tidal tails, commonly referred to as the north and south tails. Both of these tails extend beyond the field of view in Figure sample . Not seen in Figure sample is the dwarf galaxy UGC 957, at about 6' north-west of NGC 520.
NGC 520, the next youngest merger, exhibits a slight age difference among clusters in different tails. NGC 520N has clusters around _ clus 100 Myr, while NGC 520S clusters has _ clus 260 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as a nearby merging system with two nuclei and compact radio structure, and places its kilomaser in the innermost nuclear region while noting no clear AGN evidence.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 is a nearby (D 30 Mpc, 1 \ 145 pc, for H_0 =75 kms Mpc ^ 1 ) merging system, considered to be the result of an encounter between a gas rich and a gas poor disk galaxy n520HvG . The remnant nuclei are clearly visible in near infrared K-band (2 m ) images n520stan90,n520koti01 . At optical wavelengths, the primary nucleus, which is four times more massive than the other n520stan90 , is hidden behind a pronounced dust lane, while the secondary less massive one is seen 40 (6 kpc) to the north-west n520stan90,n520laine03 .

Our measurements place the kilomaser in NGC 520 in the innermost nuclear region of the galaxy. In order to associate the maser emission with either enhanced star formation or with nuclear activity, we have first to locate and identify the galaxy's true nucleus and check whether it exhibits (low-luminosity) AGN activity. So far, no clear evidence for the presence of an AGN in NGC 520 has been reported.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as an IR-luminous disturbed merger pair with two nuclei, a dust-obscured primary nucleus, and high near-infrared extinction in its nuclear region.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 (Arp 157, UGC 966, VV 231) at v_ sys = 2059 km s ^ 1 ( D = 27.3 Mpc, 1 '' = 130 pc, for H _0= 75 Mpc ^ 1 km s ^ 1 and q _0 = 0.5) is an IR--luminous peculiar pair of galaxies with a highly disturbed flattened morphology (e.g. Stanford Balcells 1990; Hibbard van Gorkom 1996). The inclination of NGC 520 is 66 (Rownd Young 1999). NGC 520 is at an intermediate merger stage (Hibbard van Gorkom 1996), with the two nuclei embedded within a common stellar envelope. The primary nucleus (hereafter PN) is optically hidden behind a dust lane at PA = 95 , while the second, optically bright nucleus (hereafter NWN) is situated 40 '' (5.3 kpc) to NW.
For NGC 520, the extinction is much higher, A _K = 1.2 -- 1.6.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as a mid-stage merger with strong silicate absorption, concentrated neutral PAHs, low warm-H2 temperature in its southern component, and altered extinction-corrected H2 flux.

quotes.quote: Only for NGC 6240 and NGC 520 we find a three times smaller H _2 flux with no-extinction than for the screen model, which is due to the large silicate absorption in these galaxies ( _ 9.7 1.3 ).

For instance, NGC 4676 (primarily ionized PAHs) and NGC 520 (primarily neutral PAHs) show a very concentrated distribution in the PAH interband strength ratios, while, e.g., NGC 6621 (ionized and neutral PAHs) and NGC 3921 (primarily neutral PAHs, including the nucleus) exhibit more widespread PAH populations.

We find for our sample a mean temperature of the warm H _2 phase of 255K, but with a large variance between different regions, ranging from 87 K (NGC 520 South) to 348 K (NGC 7592 South). The mean temperature of the hot phase for our sample is 830 K, ranging from 550K (NGC 520 South) to 1079 K (NGC 7592 South).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as a merging galaxy with two nuclei, a dust-obscured primary nucleus, and ionized-gas plumes from a starburst-dominated nucleus.

quotes.quote: Using long slit optical photometry and near-infrared imaging, 1990ApJ...355...59S uncovered two separate nuclei in NGC 520, establishing this as a galaxy in the process of merging. 1991ApJ...370..118S went on to determine that NGC 520 is most likely the result of a galaxy collision of a gas-rich and a gas-poor galaxy that happened 3 10^8 years ago. A thick dust lane at PA=95 ^ completely obscures the primary nucleus at optical wavelengths, but the secondary nucleus is visible to the north-west of the primary. There are strong plumes of ionised gas, thought to represent a bipolar outflow away from the starburst-dominated nucleus 1996ApJ...472...73N .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as an interacting system with Br emission at the primary nucleus and uses matched Br and H apertures to derive extinction.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 - Br line emission in this interacting system is detected at the primary nucleus within a region of 5 3 Sta91, Kot01 . We combined the Br flux of Sta91 in the central 6 8 with the H flux measured within the same aperture from an H + [ N ii ] map, to derive the extinction. We made use of the H /(H + [ N ii ] ) ratio of 0.58 found by Vei95 at the nucleus. Since the primary nucleus suffers from very large extinction, the use of H photometry in a larger aperture is especially uncertain.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 520 as an intermediate-stage interacting system with central radio emission, a northwest protrusion, and a current phase between encounter and final coalescence.

quotes.quote: NGC 520 -- This system represents an intermediate stage of galaxy interactions according to Toomre ( ). The radio emission at 1.4 GHz is strong in the central part of the system and shows a protrusion to the NW along the optically bright ridge. According to Stanford Barcells ( ), the system is at present 3 10^8 yr past the encounter and prior to the final coalescence.

sources.summary: The paper says the GBT formaldehyde absorption toward NGC 520 agrees with Araya et al., its H111 non-detection is consistent, and CO imaging shows kpc-scale structures.

quotes.quote: NGC 520: Araya2004 first detected the H _2 CO 1_ 10 1_ 11 transition in absorption, in good agreement with the GBT transition properties (Figure fig:ExgalFormSingles-OnePage ). Our non-detection of H111 (RMS 0.25 mJy in v 40 km s ^ 1 ) is consistent with the 0.3 mJy detection of H110 reported by Araya2004 . Interferometric measurements of the CO J= 1-0 emission from NGC 520 by Sanders1988 and Yun2001 reveal kpc-scale structures over a region 7--12^ in extent.

gmw_00003207.name: NGC 1003
gmw_00003207.overview: NGC 1003 is a low-surface-brightness [arXiv:astro-ph/0703646], edge-on galaxy [arXiv:2210.09383] featuring an extended neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1102.3065, arXiv:2210.09383] with a strong line-of-sight warp [arXiv:1409.7599, arXiv:1909.04048]. This disk is surrounded by several distinct, high-velocity cloud analogues that lack optical counterparts [arXiv:1409.7599, arXiv:2210.09383].

sources.summary: The HALOGAS overview uses NGC 1003 to show line-of-sight warping, distinct H I clouds without stellar counterparts, and HVC-like gas that contributes little to the galaxy's star formation.

quotes.quote: In the other galaxy, NGC 1003, the disk structure recovered from a detailed 3D analysis shows a substantial warp ( 15-20 ) along the line of sight, causing the disk to appear thicker in projection than the actual vertical distribution of the \ layer.

As a representative example, we highlight NGC 1003 as shown in Figure fig:fig1 . A small number of morphologically and kinematically distinct gas features have been identified from an inspection of the 3D \ dataset; these are shown with black contours in Fig. fig:fig1 . These features do not appear to have stellar counterparts, even in our deep optical images. The \ masses and distances from NGC 1003 of these clouds are similar to the properties of the HVCs around the MW. The mass of these HVC analogues adds up to only M_ HI =4 10^6 M_ , which over a dynamical time ( _ dyn 500 Myr ) contributes only 2 of the star formation rate of NGC 1003 SFR =0.40 M_ yr^ 1 ; heald_etal_2012 .
Alternatively, if the gas contained in these clouds were used to completely fuel the current SFR, they would need to be replenished after only _ repl 10 Myr .

sources.summary: The HALOGAS paper reports that NGC 1003 has an extended H I disk, substantial H I mass, a warped flaring thick disk, and distinct H I features.

quotes.quote: NGC 1003 NGC 1003 has a star formation rate of 0.34 M_ yr ^ 1 ( ). The width of the \ disk is 21^ , compared to the roughly 5^ optical disk (see Figure Figure:NGC1003 ). Assuming D = 11 Mpc , we obtain a total \ mass of 5.34 10^9 M_ . Our tilted-ring modeling (described below) indicates a strongly warped, flaring thick disk. Several distinct \ features are detected, as shown in Figure Figure:NGC1003 .

sources.summary: The paper excludes NGC 1003 from EPG modeling because a strong line-of-sight warp makes ring overlap defeat the separation method.

quotes.quote: Three galaxies out of 15 were left out from the analysis: NGC 1003, NGC 4274 and NGC 4448. A visual inspection of the \ datacube of NGC 1003 revealed the presence of a strong line-of-sight warp in the eastern part of the galaxy, where the orientation becomes nearly edge-on. This effect seems to be less prominent on the western side. This line-of-sight warp, already noticed by Heald+11b , also explains the appearance of the total \ map (Fig. fig:maps ), which suggests a much higher inclination than that inferred from the optical image ( 70 75 ). As our EPG separation method fails when consecutive rings overlap along the line of sight, we decided to discard this system from our study.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1003 as an edge-on galaxy with an H I disk beyond the optical disk, warped outer gas, and nearby HVC-like clouds.

quotes.quote: This is an edge-on galaxy of which the \ disk extends far beyond the visible optical disk. In the outer parts of the disk there are hints of warping and several clouds, considered to be HVC analogues, can be seen around the main disk Heald2011b . These clouds are all detected however only one of them is separated from the emission in the disk (see section implicatcold on how this affects our results). Even though this counter rotating source is separated by more than a FWHM from the mask encompassing the emission of NGC 1003, it is still close to the disk. The HALOSTARS image (Figure fig: ) does not show an optical counterpart at its location, but the field is crowded with foreground stars making identification of low level optical emission difficult.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 1003 as a difficult low-surface-brightness aperture-photometry case, comparing raw, surface-photometry, and fitted profile intensity estimates from a near-infrared J-band image.

quotes.quote: Figure 9 display the results for all three techniques for the galaxy NGC 1003. The black symbols are the raw intensities summed from the image file. The blue symbols are the intensities determined from the surface photometry. The orange symbols are the intensities determined from the fits to the surface photometric profile. This was one of the worst case scenarios due to the fact the original image is very LSB (in the near-IR J band).

gmw_00003208.name: NGC 5023
gmw_00003208.overview: NGC 5023 is an isolated, late-type spiral dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0702486, arXiv:0710.0711] viewed almost edge-on [arXiv:1102.3065, arXiv:0710.0711]. It exhibits an extremely low star formation rate [arXiv:1102.3065], with its hydrogen gas extending beyond the optical disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0702486] and displaying extraplanar features [arXiv:1102.3065].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5023 as a small edge-on, slightly warped galaxy with extraplanar H I features, low projected rotation, and very low star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 5023 The edge-on galaxy NGC 5023 is a small ( v_ rot 80 km s ^ 1 ), slightly warped galaxy at a distance of 6.6 Mpc ( ). Figure Figure:NGC4244_NGC5023 shows an integrated \ (moment-0) map. The 21-cm line emission in this galaxy extends on average up to a projected distance of 80^ from the plane, with individual features extending to 100^ (indicated in the figure with arrows). At the adopted distance, these vertical distances translate to 2.6 kpc and 3.2 kpc, respectively.

The most vertically extended features also clearly show up in the individual channel maps of the data. Even though the bulk of the high-latitude emission could be projected above the major axis due to a line-of-sight warp, it is more difficult to explain the individual features in this manner. The extraplanar gas also has lower projected rotational velocities than the gas in the mid-plane.
The star formation rate in NGC 5023 is extremely low ( 0.032 M_ yr ^ 1 ; ).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5023 as a pure-disk dwarf with the weakest truncation evidence in the sample, H I extending beyond the optical disk, and no observed warp.

quotes.quote: NGC 5023 is a pure disk galaxy and has been shown to be a late-type dwarf system (see also Bottema et al., 1986). In the van der Kruit \ Searle (1982a) sample the evidence for the truncation is the weakest of all; in Fig. 8 of that paper we see in the righthand panel that the averaged radial profile at the last measured point is only a few tenth of a magnitude below the extrapolated exponential disk. Although this galaxy still is believed to have a truncation, it may be no surprise that it is not readily seen with this method.

NGC 5023 is a dwarf galaxy with the weakest evidence for a truncation in the stellar disk among these galaxies. The observations of Bottema et al. (1986) show that the \ extends somewhat beyond the optical disk, but there is no warp and no clear features in the rotation curve or in the surface density distribution have been observed.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5023 as an isolated edge-on late-type spiral with an HST TRGB distance and H-alpha emission confined to its disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 5023 . This isolated late-type spiral galaxy seen almost edge-on was observed with ACS at HST by Seth et al. (2005), who estimated its distance from the TRGB luminosity to be 6.61 Mpc. All visible H emission from NGC 5023 is concentrated in its disk without signs of extra-planar gas motions.

gmw_00003209.name: SDSS J080413.87+470442.8
gmw_00003209.overview: SDSS J080413.87+470442.8 is a radio-quiet quasar located at a redshift of z=0.51 [arXiv:2211.03947]. It exhibits an optical periodicity of 407.5 days, very weak broad Balmer lines, and features consistent with a merging binary supermassive black hole [arXiv:2211.03947].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J080413.87+470442.8 as a radio-quiet quasar with optical periodicity, weak Balmer lines, and features consistent with a merging binary SMBH.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:spec shows the light curve of SDSS J080413.87+470442.8 with z=0.51 (RA: 121.058 Dec: 47.079) its SDSS spectrum. This is a radio-quiet quasar. We make use of LS method to obtain an optical period of P_ opt =407.5 days with a periodicity power of 0.92 . Interestingly, this object shows very weak broad Balmer lines, but relatively strong \ line ( EW 153.0 12.0 ) but significantly weaker than that of quasar composite spectra ( 50 ).

There is no radio light curves to test its periodicity so far. Since the possible orbital periods of P_ orb P_ opt /20 20 days if optical periodicity is driven by the binary, it should be very trivial to test radio periodicity through monitoring this target in radio within a few months. SDSS J080413.87+470442.8 is in agreement with the major features of the merging binaries as a consequence of current deficit of UV continuum as ionizing sources.

gmw_00003210.name: S928
gmw_00003210.overview: S928 is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1405.7823]. It is characterized by a high dynamical mass-to-light ratio that exceeds its stellar population mass-to-light ratio by a factor of 2.3 [arXiv:1405.7823].

sources.summary: The thesis discusses S928 as one of two high-M/L Virgo UCDs observed in K-band CO absorption; the data show no offset from the Galactic-GC CO-metallicity relation.

quotes.quote: The primary targets, the Virgo cluster UCDs S928 and S999, which have a dynamical M/L exceeding the stellar population M/L by a factor of = M _ dyn / M _ pop = 2.3 and 4.4, respectively 2013A&A...558A..14M , were observed for 8.2 hours in total.

For the highest M/L UCDs, such as the Virgo cluster UCDs S999 and S928, this offset should be 0.05 mag. The data show no such offset and all targets show a CO index compatible within 3 with the empirical relation of Galactic GCs. The observations were originally designed to yield uncertainties in the CO index <0.05 mag for the two Virgo cluster UCDs. However, as already mentioned, the modifications to the spectrum extraction that were necessary to suppress systematic biases in the low signal to noise regime, caused the uncertainties to be larger than the expected effect. In summary, the CO absorption in the high M/L Virgo cluster UCDs S999 and S928 is in agreement with that of the lower M/L comparison GCs/UCDs in NGC 5128 when the empirical dependence of the CO index on metallicity is taken into account.

gmw_00003211.name: S999
gmw_00003211.overview: S999 is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1405.7823]. It exhibits a moderate metallicity [arXiv:1511.03264] and an extremely high dynamical mass-to-light ratio [arXiv:1405.7823, arXiv:1511.03264], which may be the result of a tidal stripping event [arXiv:1511.03264].

sources.summary: The thesis discusses S999 as one of two high-M/L Virgo UCDs observed in K-band CO absorption; the data show no offset from the Galactic-GC CO-metallicity relation.

quotes.quote: The primary targets, the Virgo cluster UCDs S928 and S999, which have a dynamical M/L exceeding the stellar population M/L by a factor of = M _ dyn / M _ pop = 2.3 and 4.4, respectively 2013A&A...558A..14M , were observed for 8.2 hours in total.

For the highest M/L UCDs, such as the Virgo cluster UCDs S999 and S928, this offset should be 0.05 mag. The data show no such offset and all targets show a CO index compatible within 3 with the empirical relation of Galactic GCs. The observations were originally designed to yield uncertainties in the CO index <0.05 mag for the two Virgo cluster UCDs. However, as already mentioned, the modifications to the spectrum extraction that were necessary to suppress systematic biases in the low signal to noise regime, caused the uncertainties to be larger than the expected effect. In summary, the CO absorption in the high M/L Virgo cluster UCDs S999 and S928 is in agreement with that of the lower M/L comparison GCs/UCDs in NGC 5128 when the empirical dependence of the CO index on metallicity is taken into account.

sources.summary: The paper describes S999 as a possible stripped object with an extreme mass-to-light ratio, stellar-population evidence for excess dynamical mass, and moderate metallicity.

quotes.quote: S999, while not featuring a very high metallicity, may also fall into the category of stripped objects. Hasegan05 found an extremely high mass-to-light ratio for S999, which was recently confirmed by Janz15 . The latter study also analysed the stellar populations of S999, and argued that the stars alone (in dynamical equilibrium) cannot account for the mass-to-light ratio. The authors concluded that the apparently too high dynamical mass probably relates to a tidal stripping event. The moderate metallicity of S999 ( [ Z/ H ] =-0.95 0.12 ) shows that excessive amounts of metals may be seen as sufficient for suggesting stripping formation but not absolutely necessary.

gmw_00003212.name: Ursa Major I
gmw_00003212.overview: Ursa Major I is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy located approximately 100 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:0705.4622, arXiv:0907.4758]. It is dominated by an ancient, metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:0804.2976, arXiv:1310.0824] and exhibits a highly irregular, distorted morphology that suggests it is undergoing tidal disruption [arXiv:0804.2976, arXiv:2208.07948].

sources.summary: The paper discusses Ursa Major I as a faint dwarf satellite with poorly constrained structure, previous velocity measurements, new DEIMOS coverage, and overlapping foreground contamination.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major I was discovered by willman05b from an automatic search of faint substructures in the SDSS. Though the parameters of this satellite are not well constrained given the low number of stars on its red giant branch and the absence of deep observations that reach the more populated main sequence turn-off, UMaI appears to be at a distance of 100 , with M_V 6.75 , r_ hb =250 and a metallicity roughly estimated to be within 2.1 1.7 . kleyna05 observed 7 stars near the tip of the red giant branch with the HIRES spectrograph mounted on the Keck I telescope and concluded that 5 of these are likely members of the galaxy. They derive a mean systemic velocity of v_r=-52.4 4.3 and a velocity dispersion of =9.3_ 1.2 ^ +11.7 through a maximum-likelihood algorithm and subsequently use these values to infer a mass-to-light ratio of the order of 500 in solar units for UMaI. To increase the number of observed stars in the dwarf galaxy, two DEIMOS fields were observed at the center of UMaI, roughly covering the region within the half-light radius of the dwarf (Figure 1 ). As before, Figure 2 shows the CMD taken from the SDSS for this region of the sky along with the selected targets and the Na i doublet discrimination is presented Figure 3 .

Contrary to Bo otes, Ursa Major I has a velocity distribution that overlaps with the Galactic contaminants (Figure 6). The dwarf galaxy stars however seem to produce a very narrow peak of 8 stars surrounded by a broader group of stars for which it is difficult to definitely conclude whether they belong to UMaI or to the Galactic contamination (only one star is removed from the sample since, with a metallicity of =-0.8 , it is inconsistent with the metal-poor population of the dwarf and is hence most probably a dwarf for which the determined has no meaningful sense). Applying the maximum likelihood technique that was applied on the Boo sample yields a systemic velocity of v_r=-57.0 3.5 and a velocity dispersion =11.9_ 2.3 ^ +3.5 for the stars aligned along the CMD features of the dwarf, in good agreement with the kleyna05 measurement Including the metal-rich star leads to a systemic velocity v_r=-59.1 4.1 and a velocity dispersion =12.8_ 2.5 ^ +3.6 . .

sources.summary: The paper describes Ursa Major I as an ultra-faint dwarf with old stellar populations and possible structural distortion, presenting first high-resolution abundance analysis of inner and outer member stars.

quotes.quote: UMa I UMa I is a UFD with total mass M 1.5 10^7 \ Simon2007 , apparent and absolute magnitudes of m_V 14.4 and M_V 5.5, half-light radius R_ h 235 pc, and it is at a distance of D 97 kpc McConnachie2012 . HST photometry showed that UMa I has an age within 1 Gyr of the oldest known MW globular cluster, M92 Brown12 . Furthermore, Brown2014 reconstructed its star formation history with a two-burst model, such that roughly half of the stars in UMa I were formed over 13 Gyr ago, and the other half by z 3 (11.6 Gyr ago). They also suggest that a physical distortion in the structure of UMa I may account for the apparent age spread, e.g., a tidal distortion, first noticed in a photometric study by Okamoto08 ,

Here, we provide the first HRS model atmospheres analysis of two stars in UMa I. Radial velocities are in good agreement with MRS estimates in the literature. Our two UMa I targets include one inner star (< 1R _ h ) and one in the outskirts ( 4R _ h ); see Fig. fig:RVFe . The two stars have similar metallicities and chemical abundances, suggesting that they formed from the same or similar interstellar gas.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major as a faint Local Group dSph comparison, noting its limited kinematic data, Draco-like central velocity dispersion, and scale-length-based surface-brightness profile.

quotes.quote: Over-plotted are data from two LG dSphs which bracket the observed range of brightness: Draco (crosses) and, the recently discovered, Ursa Major (squares). The data were taken from 2001ApJ...563L.115K , 2004ApJ...611L..21W , 2005ApJ...626L..85W and 2005ApJ...630L.141K . Ursa Major is so faint that it has only one data point for the kinematics, but this suggests a very similar central velocity dispersion to Draco, despite it being an order of magnitude fainter. For Ursa Major's surface brightness distribution, we use the measured value for the scale length and central brightness, but assume the same distribution as in Draco - hence the lack of error bars for these points.

Ursa Major has only one velocity dispersion measurement. It could be reasonably argued that this makes it as unreliable as the older data. However, unlike the older data, this one data point lies 250 pc away from the centre of the galaxy. This makes it more likely to be representative of the mean velocity dispersion than older measurements.

sources.summary: Subaru/Suprime-Cam photometry shows Ursa Major I is old, metal-poor, faint, extended, dark-matter dominated, and morphologically distorted, consistent with tidal disruption.

quotes.quote: We present deep and wide V, I CCD photometry of Ursa Major I (UMa I) dwarf spheroidal galaxy (dSph) in Local Group. The images of the galaxy were taken by Subaru/Suprime-Cam wide field camera, covering a field of 34 27  located at the centre of the galaxy. Colour-magnitude diagram (CMD) of the UMa I dSph shows a steep and narrow red giant branch (RGB), blue and red horizontal branch (HB), and main sequence (MS) stars.

The distance modulus is derived as (m-M)_0=19.93 0.1 (corresponding to a distance D =96.8 4 kpc) from the V-band magnitude of the horizontal branch (V _ 0,HB =20.45 0.02 ). The mean metallicity of the RGB stars is estimated by the V I colour to [ Fe/H ] 2.0 . The turn-off age estimated by overlaying the theoretical isochrones reveals that most of stars in the UMa I dSph are formed at very early epoch ( 12 Gyrs ago). The isopleth map of stellar number density of the UMa I dSph, based upon the resolved star counts of MS, RGB, HB stars as well as blue stragglers (BS), shows that the morphology of the UMa I dSph is quite irregular and distorted, suggesting that the galaxy is in a process of disruption.

sources.summary: The paper measures apparent line-of-sight elongation for Ursa Major I dSph and finds that a slightly smaller distance uncertainty is needed to make its result sensible.

quotes.quote: For Sextans dSph, Draco dSph, Ursa Minor Dwarf dE4 and Ursa Major I dSph, we find a D / l of 9.83, 14.48, 19.77, 4.84, respectively. Using Equ. eq:sigma_D_with_uncertainty with _D = 0.03D , we calculate the uncertainty-corrected (true) line-of-sight depth ( D)' = 2 '_D = 2 ( _D ^2- _D^2) = 2 ( _D ^2- (0.03D)^2) . We find thus ( D)'= 4.28 for the Sextans dSph, ( D)' = 1.79 for the Draco dSph, ( D)' = 1.41 for the Ursa Minor Dwarf dE4, ( D)' = 0.94 for the Ursa Major I dSph.

For the Ursa Major I dSph, _D must be 0.027D instead of 0.03D to make the result sensible. With the values for l given in Tab. tab:dwarfs and _D=0.03D , we calculate D / l = 7.78 for Sextans dSph, 6.36 for Draco dSph, 6.40 for Ursa Minor Dwarf dE4, 1.44 for Ursa Major I dSph. This means that introducing a distance uncertainty, which is appropriate as shown by , reduces the elongation in D direction, but is still far away from an axis ratio of 1.

sources.summary: Ursa Major I is a nearby ultra-faint dwarf in the HST program; the paper reports ancient, metal-poor stellar populations and synchronized star-formation histories across the sample.

quotes.quote: Our survey observed six UFD galaxies using two cameras on HST : the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) and the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3). We used the F606W (broad V ) and F814W ( I ) filters on each camera, with ACS surveying an area centered on each galaxy ( 202^ 202^ per tile), and WFC3 observing in parallel in the galaxy outskirts ( 162^ 162^ per tile). For the nearby ( < 100 kpc) satellites in the sample (Bootes I, Coma Berenices, Ursa Major I), we surveyed a relatively wide area (5--12 tiles in each camera) to a shallow depth (2 or 3 orbits, faint limit of V 28 mag).

It is clear that the CMD of each UFD looks like the CMD of an ancient globular cluster, and that each is dominated by an old (age > 12 Gyr) population of metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] < -2 ) stars. A prominent blue straggler sequence (BSS) is present in each galaxy -- ubiquitous in old populations.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major I cautiously because it is more massive and luminous than the model, but finds the model improves after excluding questionable members.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major I has half-light mass of 2.6 ^ +1.2 _ 1.1 10^7 M _ collins14 and a luminosity of 1.4 0.4 10^4 L _ , which is much more massive and luminous than our model and therefore the results from the model should be treated with caution. It is the only galaxy studied by brown14 with similar numbers of stars forming in each burst. As shown in Fig. f:afefeh , the model can explain the observed abundances except for the stars with [ Fe/H ] 1.5 . However, three of these higher-metallicity stars are included in vargas13 but not in brown14 because they are no longer believed to be members (J. Simon, private comm.). When these three stars are excluded, the model is a much better fit to Ursa Major I.

sources.summary: The paper revises the Ursa Major I spectroscopic membership sample, removes three RR Lyrae with variable velocities, and says the removals have little impact because the affected stars have large errors.

quotes.quote: We have made slight membership changes to the Simon2007ApJ...670..313S data for the following dSphs: Canes Venatici I, Coma Berenices, Leo IV, Ursa Major I, and Ursa Major II. We have removed RR Lyrae from the following (identified after the original spectroscopic analysis): Canes Venatici I 5 RR Lyrae identified in Kuehn2008ApJ...674L..81K , Coma Berenices 1 star; Musella2009ApJ...695L..83M , Leo IV 1 star; Moretti2009ApJ...699L.125M , and Ursa Major I 3 stars; Garofalo2013ApJ...767...62G . RR Lyrae are pulsating stars with variable velocity; these stars are dSph members, however, without additional phase information we cannot determine the star's bulk velocity. In Ursa Major II, a foreground dwarf has been removed that was identified in follow-up high-resolution spectroscopy analysis Frebel2010ApJ...708..560F . These removals do not have a large impact as the removed stars tend to have large error bars.

sources.summary: The paper finds UMaI structural parameters similar to deep main-sequence-turnoff data, but with a larger size and higher ellipticity, making it the most flattened satellite.

quotes.quote: UMaI - Deep data down to the main-sequence turn-off of this galaxy obtained by okamoto08 give structural parameters ( 78 and r_h 11.3' ) that are very similar to those yielded by the ML algorithm. We confirm the larger size of the system compared to the discovery paper r_h 7.75' willman05b but, once again, derive a higher ellipticity than the one okamoto08 estimate from their smoothed maps ( 0.54 ). Our value makes UMaI the most flattened MW satellite galaxy.

sources.summary: The review uses Ursa Major I as an ultra-faint dwarf example, noting its M_V=-5.5, 100 kpc distance, invisibility in raw star counts, and recovery through color-magnitude and spatial filtering.

quotes.quote: The Ursa Major I ultra-faint dwarf ( M_V = -5.5, d = 100 kpc) is not visible in the star count map on the left. After CM filtering, a slight overdensity of point sources becomes visible.
The result of this analysis step is illustrated in the right panel of Figure 2, which shows that Ursa Major I appears prominent in a spatially smoothed map of CM filtered stars.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major I as an ultra-faint dwarf whose inferred formation epoch is unusually late compared with other reionization-era fossil candidates.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major I stands out as the sole exception: it has a mean formation redshift indicating that it formed during, or even slightly after, reionization. In comparison, UFDs formed at systematically higher redshift in the EDE cosmology, with all but Ursa Major I consistent with being pre-reionization fossils.

Applying a similar shift to the mean ages in the EDE cosmology results in somewhat better agreement with expectations from galaxy formation theory, as only Ursa Major I is inconsistent with forming at z 6 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies U Maj I as the strongest MOND outlier, arguing Gaia DR2 recent-infall evidence undercuts earlier explanations based on early infall.

quotes.quote: Perhaps the most deviant of all the satellites is U Maj I standing about 7 from MOND's prediction. This deviation was attributed to the early infall time of this satellite in MW2010ApJ . While early analysis of the infall times of the satellites categorized this satellite as indeed one with an early infall time of about 8 Gyr However Rocha2012MNRAS , recent Gaia DR2 suggests that this satellite is actually entering the MW's halo recently in the past 1.5^ +5.1 _ 1.6 Gyr Fillingham2019 .

gmw_00003213.name: WISE J071634.59 190039.2
gmw_00003213.overview: WISE J071634.59 190039.2 is an elliptical [arXiv:1609.01419, arXiv:1611.09209] or early-type galaxy [arXiv:1806.00562] located at a redshift of approximately 0.492 [arXiv:1609.01419, arXiv:1806.00562]. It hosts a compact, persistent, and variable radio source [arXiv:1607.08257, arXiv:1610.09043] at its optical center [arXiv:1607.08257], which likely represents a weak active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1607.08257, arXiv:1806.00562].

sources.summary: The paper studies WISE J0716−19 as the proposed FRB 150418 host, detecting a compact nuclear radio source and discussing whether the radio emission is AGN-like.

quotes.quote: Methods. We observed WISE J0716−19 at 5.0 GHz with the European VLBI Network four times between 2016 March 16 and June 2. At one epoch, we simultaneously observed the source with e-MERLIN at the same frequency. Results. We detected a compact source in the EVN data in each epoch with a significance up to ∼ 7σ. The four epochs yielded consistent results within their uncertainties, for both peak surface intensity and positions.

Optical photometric and spectroscopic follow-up observations with the Subaru telescope identified an elliptical galaxy at z=0.492 0.008 consistent with the radio source within the 1 positional uncertainty. In more detail, the radio transient emission was observed in only the first two epochs of ATCA follow-up separated by 6-days (flux densities 0.2 mJy at 5.5 GHz) with three subsequent detections of essentially steady emission ( 0.1 mJy at 5.5 GHz) attributed to the emission from the host galaxy. This association of the prompt ms-duration emission from the FRB with the variable ATCA source has been questioned. hereafter Williams2016a Williams2016a argued instead that \ is consistent with being a random active galactic nucleus (AGN) found within the Parkes beam based on the known rate of variable (rather than transient) radio sources, that the steady radio emission component implies a large luminosity more typical of an AGN, and that the radio light curve is inconsistent with the evolution of a standard afterglow.

sources.summary: The paper finds WISE J071634.59 190039.2 hosts a compact, persistent radio source at its optical center, favoring a weak radio AGN over star formation.

quotes.quote: A fading radio source, coincident in time and position with the fast radio burst FRB 150418, has been associated with the galaxy WISE J071634.59 190039.2. Subsequent observations of this galaxy have revealed that it contains a persistent, but variable, radio source. We present e-MERLIN, VLBA, and ATCA radio observations and Subaru optical observations of WISE J071634.59 190039.2 and find that the persistent radio source is unresolved and must be compact ( <0.01 kpc), and that its location is consistent with the optical centre of the galaxy. We conclude that it is likely that WISE J071634.59 190039.2 contains a weak radio AGN.

The location of the compact radio source is consistent with our best estimate of the centre of its host galaxy. Thus, our data are consistent with the existence of a weak radio AGN within the galaxy (see gbp+13 for a discussion of the existence of AGN in `radio quiet' galaxies).

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISE J0716--19 as the early-type galaxy at the FRB 150418 radio-variable position, reports z=0.492, and argues its radio emission likely traces a low-luminosity AGN.

quotes.quote: Although no peculiar variable object was found within the error circle, an early type galaxy was clearly detected at the position of the fading object observed by ATCA. The galaxy is also detected by the WISE satellite Wright:2010a and catalogued as WISE J071634.59--190039.2 (hereafter WISE J0716--19). The subsequent spectroscopy of WISE J0716--19 using Subaru/FOCAS Kashikawa:2002a revealed that its redshift is z = 0.492 0.008 Keane:2016a .

The radio emission of WISE J0716--19 suggests that it hosts a radio faint AGN Williams:2016a, Vedantham:2016b, Bassa:2016a, Giroletti:2016a, Johnston:2017a . However, the optical spectrum of WISE J0716--19 shows no AGN signature Keane:2016a , suggesting that the disk luminosity of any putative AGN is low. Our non-detection of any optical variability also supports this interpretation.

sources.summary: The paper finds the radio source in WISE J071634.59 190039.2 has a 5.5 GHz mean flux density of 140 Jy, short-timescale variability consistent with ISS of a weak AGN, leaving the FRB association uncertain.

quotes.quote: We present observations made with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA), the Jansky Very Large Array (JVLA) and the Giant Metre-Wave Telescope of the radio source within the galaxy WISE J071634.59 190039.2, claimed to be host of FRB 150418 by Keane et al. (2016). We have established a common flux density scale between the ATCA and JVLA observations, the main result of which is to increase the flux densities obtained by Keane et al. At a frequency of 5.5 GHz, the source has a mean flux density of 140 Jy and is variable on short timescales with a modulation index of 0.36. Statistical analysis of the flux densities shows that the variations seen are consistent with refractive interstellar scintillation of the weak active galactic nucleus at the centre of the galaxy. It may therefore be the case that the FRB and the galaxy are not associated.

sources.summary: The paper describes WISE J071634.59 190039.2 as a possible elliptical host galaxy for FRB150418, while stressing controversy over whether the FRB and radio afterglow are truly associated with it.

quotes.quote: The fading radio afterglow lasted up to six days after the FRB, and could be linked to an elliptical host galaxy at z=0.492 0.008 (WISE J071634.59 190039.2). If connected to the afterglow, the energetics of FRB150418 suggest a cataclysmic origin of the bursts e.g. Zhang:2016 . However, alternative explanations for the temporal behavior of the radio flux have been suggested in the form of an unrelated active galactic nucleus (AGN) activity in the host galaxy Williams:2016 , or interstellar scintillation Akiyama:2016 .

The result shown in Fig. fig:UL has been derived using the redshift of the potential host galaxy of FRB150418, z=0.492 FRB150418_Keane . While this distance is consistent with the one derived from the dispersion measure of the FRB, there is still controversy as to the relationship between FRB150418 and WISE J071634.59 190039.2.

gmw_00003214.name: SMMJ123607+621143
gmw_00003214.overview: SMMJ123607+621143 is a submillimeter galaxy detected as a SCUBA source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is associated with weak radio emission detected by the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope and the Very Large Array [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper describes SMMJ123607+621143 as a SCUBA source with offset WSRT/VLA radio emission near a rare red galaxy, but rejects that counterpart outside the search radius.

quotes.quote: A weak radio source detected by WSRT lies 8 \ to the East. An inspection of the VLA map shows a 4 peak near this position, although it is 2 \ even further East from the SCUBA galaxy (but consistent with the offset between the catalogues as discussed previously). Interestingly, the weak VLA source is coincident with a red ( R-HK^ =3.3) galaxy. Given the rarity of such sources, it is tempting to associate the sub-mm galaxy with it, despite the offset. However, this is well outside our 7 \ search radius, so we dismiss it.

gmw_00003215.name: SMMJ123616+621516
gmw_00003215.overview: SMMJ123616+621516 is an infrared galaxy associated with likely matching radio sources detected by the WSRT and VLA [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It features a fairly faint optical counterpart with a faint red companion located to its northwest [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper identifies likely matching WSRT and VLA radio sources for SMMJ123616+621516, notes faint optical and X-ray counterparts, and flags possible blended sub-mm emission.

quotes.quote: 2 radio sources are found within 7 , one from the WSRT and one from the VLA. These are not spatially coincident, but given that the fluxes are comparable, and the WSRT position is West of the VLA source (again consistent with the offset between the catalogues), they are likely to be the same source. The optical counterpart is fairly faint, and appears to have a faint red companion 1 \ to the North-west. Coincident with the radio ID is a blended \ source. Note that this object is discussed in some detail in 2003AJ....125..383A .

gmw_00003216.name: SMMJ123618+621007
gmw_00003216.overview: SMMJ123618+621007 is a submillimeter-selected infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It lacks a secure counterpart identification because multiple optically detected galaxies and a faint source lie near its centroid [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper finds the nearest VLA/X-ray/optical source too distant for SMMJ123618+621007 and leaves the SCUBA source without a secure counterpart.

quotes.quote: The VLA source (detected in X-ray and optical images) 13 \ away is too far to comfortably call it the counterpart. Within 20 \ there are 3 LBGs, but the closest is 8.3 \ away. Since we find 2 optically detected galaxies within 6 , we cannot assign an ID. We note an even fainter galaxy which, although not in the >5 optical catalogue, is seen in the optical thumb-nails and seems to lie directly on the SCUBA centroid.

gmw_00003217.name: SMMJ123618+621552
gmw_00003217.overview: SMMJ123618+621552 is an infrared galaxy associated with a strong 1.4 GHz VLA radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. This radio counterpart exhibits a very steep spectrum and is coincident with a faint, formally undetected HK-band source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper treats a steep-spectrum 1.4 GHz VLA source as the counterpart to SMMJ123618+621552 and notes nearby optical and HK-band candidates.

quotes.quote: A strong 1.4 GHz VLA source ( 151 Jy) with a very steep spectrum lies 3.5 \ to the South-west. An 8.5 GHz source is also seen there, though not formally listed in the catalogue. We consider this radio source to be the counterpart. The only optically detected galaxy in the search region is a faint optical source 2 \ North of the VLA position, but a fainter (and formally undetected) HK^ source is coincident with the radio.

gmw_00003218.name: SMMJ123621+621710
gmw_00003218.overview: SMMJ123621+621710 is an infrared galaxy associated with an extended radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. The radio source is coincident with an optically detected galaxy that exhibits an elongated morphology and extended soft X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper identifies an extended radio source coincident with SMMJ123621+621710, associated with an elongated optical galaxy and extended soft X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: An extended radio source is coincident with the SCUBA position. Its North-western end is coincident with an optically detected galaxy that has an elongated morphology perpendicular to the radio extension. Although not listed in the 2 Ms \ catalogue, there is extended soft X-ray emission clearly seen. We use this VLA source as the SCUBA counterpart.

gmw_00003219.name: SMMJ123622+621616
gmw_00003219.overview: SMMJ123622+621616 is an infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376] that has been identified as a possible Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. Its optical vicinity contains a pair of galaxies separated by less than 1 arcsecond, along with three other nearby objects [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper rejects a distant northern radio source for SMMJ123622+621616 and notes discrepant X-ray and FIR-radio redshift estimates plus nearby optical galaxies.

quotes.quote: 2000AJ....119.2092B associates this object with the radio source 13 \ to the North. This is well outside our search radius however, and we do not assign it as the correct ID. 2003AJ....125..383A use this object as a possible example of a Compton-thick AGN, but they noted the inferred redshift ( z=0.46^ +0.03 _ 0.02 ), based on a 2.3 iron line in the X-ray spectrum, was considerably different than that obtained via fits to the FIR-radio SED of the galaxy ( z=2.4^ +1.1 _ 0.8 ). In the optical catalogues, there is a pair of galaxies separated by less than 1 \ and 3 other objects within the 7 \ search radius.

gmw_00003220.name: SMMJ123634+621407
gmw_00003220.overview: SMMJ123634+621407 is an infrared galaxy located in a field dense with optically detected sources [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. Although an X-ray detected Lyman-break galaxy and an infrared ISO source lie nearby, the galaxy lacks a robustly identified counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper weighs an offset X-ray LBG and ISO/8.5 GHz source near SMMJ123634+621407 but concludes there is no robust counterpart.

quotes.quote: Although the field is dense in optically detected sources, one unique object in the vicinity is an X-ray detected LBG with z=3.408 . The 11.4 \ offset is rather large, especially considering the 850 \ flux density is well above the confusion level and the SNR is high. The ISO 15 \ detection 5.3 \ to the South is the more appealing counterpart, and there is an 8.5 GHz object coincident with the ISO centroid. However it is not listed in the 8.5 GHz catalogue, and no 1.4 GHz or optical flux is detected here. We conclude that there is no robust counterpart.

gmw_00003221.name: SMMJ123637+621155
gmw_00003221.overview: SMMJ123637+621155 is an infrared galaxy detected as a SCUBA source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is located within four arcseconds of two optical candidate galaxies with spectroscopic redshifts of 0.779 and 0.557, though these are unlikely to be direct counterparts [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper discusses weak radio emission and two spectroscopic optical candidates near SMMJ123637+621155, finding the low-redshift optical galaxies unlikely as direct counterparts.

quotes.quote: A 4 radio detection by the WSRT (but not VLA) does not appear coincident with any other object, but the VLA map does reveal radio flux, though not quite at the same position. Seemingly unrelated are two optical candidates, which both have spectroscopic redshifts, and lie within 4 \ of the SCUBA source. The first is a z=0.779 galaxy with R=22.5, R-HK^ =1.6 . The second is a z=0.557 galaxy with R=22.4, R-HK^ =1.6 , which also happens to line up with a soft X-ray source. At these redshifts it is difficult to understand the lack of a radio detection if one of these two objects were the SCUBA counterpart.

gmw_00003222.name: SMMJ123645+621449
gmw_00003222.overview: SMMJ123645+621449 is an infrared galaxy associated with a SCUBA source [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is coincident with two 1.4 GHz VLA radio sources, the brighter and closer of which is also detected at 8.5 GHz [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper finds two radio and X-ray detections near SMMJ123645+621449, chooses the nearer brighter radio source, and notes redshifts are needed.

quotes.quote: There are two 1.4 GHz VLA sources coincident with \ detections near the SCUBA source, and we choose the one closest to the SCUBA centroid (also the brighter of the two). This object is also detected at 8.5 GHz. The other radio source is detected by ISO. Redshifts would be useful for determining if the radio pair is indeed spatially related.

gmw_00003223.name: SMMJ123650+621316
gmw_00003223.overview: SMMJ123650+621316 is a sub-millimeter galaxy located at a redshift of 0.475 [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is detected across multiple wave-bands, including radio frequencies, 15 micrometers, X-ray, and optical wavelengths [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper describes a multi-wavelength counterpart for SMMJ123650+621316 at redshift 0.475, while cautioning that confusion and radio/sub-mm flux ratios complicate the identification.

quotes.quote: The counterpart that meets our criterion is detected in most wave-bands -- both radio frequencies, 15 , X-ray, and optical, and also has a redshift of 0.475 . The faintness of this sub-mm source suggests that confusion noise may be a particular problem, but if the radio flux is coming from the SCUBA source, then the radio/sub-mm flux density ratio is a factor of 10 too low for this redshift. Note that this SCUBA source is considered as two distinct objects (HDF 850.4/HDF 850.5) by 2003MNRAS.344..887S , but the combined sub-mm data that make up the Super-map yield only a single source. Since its profile is quite Gaussian, we do not treat the possibility of it being two sources.

gmw_00003224.name: SMMJ123656+621201
gmw_00003224.overview: SMMJ123656+621201, also known as HDF 850.2, is a faint submillimeter galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is associated with a faint radio source and lies near a pair of interacting Lyman-break galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper describes SMMJ123656+621201, also called HDF 850.2, as a faint SCUBA source with a likely radio ID and nearby interacting LBG candidates.

quotes.quote: This SCUBA object is commonly called HDF 850.2, and at only 3.7 mJy, has a position that may be affected by confusion noise. The faint radio source 5.8 \ to the North seems like the correct ID, but has no optical counterpart. We note that there is a pair of LBGs just to the South-west, one of which is \ detected and has radio emission just below the VLA catalogues' threshold. The LBG was discussed by 2002ApJ...576..625N , who noted that if at redshift 3, this galaxy is almost certainly an AGN, given its X-ray luminosity. If at the same redshift, the small separation of the LBGs suggests they are interacting, which makes it an appealing choice for the SCUBA counterpart.

gmw_00003225.name: SMMJ123701+621146
gmw_00003225.overview: SMMJ123701+621146 is an infrared galaxy that is coincident with a radio and X-ray detected extremely red object [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. This object may be gravitationally lensed by a nearby foreground galaxy located at a redshift of 0.884 [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper describes SMMJ123701+621146 as coincident with a radio and X-ray ERO, with possible ISO contamination and lensing by a foreground galaxy.

quotes.quote: Directly on top of the SCUBA centroid is a striking radio and X-ray detected ERO. The object 2 \ to the South-east of the ERO has a redshift of 0.884 and an ISO 15 \ detection. However, it is possible that the ISO flux is actually coming from the ERO. Given the proximity of the source, the ERO may well be lensed by the foreground ( z=0.884 ) galaxy.

gmw_00003226.name: SMMJ123607+621019
gmw_00003226.overview: SMMJ123607+621019 is an infrared galaxy with an optical emission that suggests a disturbed system [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is located in a very dense surrounding area of galaxies, which may contaminate its photometric redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper says SMMJ123607+621019 has an obvious radio-galaxy ID, disturbed optical emission, dense surroundings, and possibly contaminated photometric redshift.

quotes.quote: A \ detected radio galaxy seems the obvious ID, although the radio/sub-mm flux density ratio is too low for the redshift of 0.47 (determined from optical photometry). The optical emission suggests a disturbed system, and the surrounding area is very dense with galaxies. Indeed, 2002AJ....124.1839B note that the photometric redshift may be contaminated by flux from neighbouring galaxies.

gmw_00003227.name: SMMJ123635+621237
gmw_00003227.overview: SMMJ123635+621237 is a faint submillimeter source identified as HDF 850.7 [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It lies in a complex field where the most appealing candidate is a red galaxy at a redshift of 1.219 that is detected in radio, mid-infrared, and X-ray wavelengths [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMMJ123635+621237 with HDF 850.7 and discusses a complex field containing a red galaxy detected in radio, mid-IR, and X-rays.

quotes.quote: This faint sub-mm source is HDF 850.7 in the 2003MNRAS.344..887S catalogue. It lies in a complex field, with the most appealing candidate being a z=1.219 red galaxy. This has detections in the radio, mid-IR, and X-ray. Unfortunately this candidate lies 7.6 \ to the West of the SCUBA centre, but again this may be due to confusion noise. The redshift is compatible with that derived from the CY estimator.

gmw_00003228.name: SMMJ123636+620658
gmw_00003228.overview: SMMJ123636+620658 is a bright submillimeter galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. A nearby fainter source exhibits radio emission and hard X-ray flux, but it is located over 11 arcseconds away and is not considered a counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper discusses a bright foreground galaxy and fainter radio/X-ray source near SMMJ123636+620658, but rejects them as too distant for a counterpart.

quotes.quote: There is a very bright (presumably foreground) galaxy with radio emission to the North. A fainter source next to it appears distorted (and perhaps redder). From this fainter object there is radio emission and hard X-ray flux. However, this radio source is distant (over 11 \ away), and sub-mm confusion is not a big issue here, since the source is so bright. We therefore conclude there is no counterpart.

gmw_00003229.name: SMMJ123652+621352
gmw_00003229.overview: SMMJ123652+621352 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.355 [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It has been detected in both X-ray and 8.5 GHz radio observations, and it is identified as part of an interacting pair of galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper places SMMJ123652+621352 on a z=1.355 galaxy with X-ray and 8.5 GHz detections and notes its HDF 850.8 interacting-pair identification.

quotes.quote: 3 moderate redshift ISO objects are within 11 , but the SCUBA centre lies on top of non-ISO detected z=1.355 galaxy which also has \ and 8.5 GHz detections. This is the only case where we base the radio ID on the 8.5 GHz data and not the 1.4 GHz. 2003MNRAS.344..887S note that this source (HDF 850.8) is part of an interacting pair of galaxies.

gmw_00003230.name: SMMJ123653+621119
gmw_00003230.overview: SMMJ123653+621119 is an infrared galaxy with multiple optically detected sources in its vicinity [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. A nearby soft X-ray Balmer-break galaxy at a redshift of 0.89 has an inferred star formation rate of 30 solar masses per year [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper finds many optical candidates around SMMJ123653+621119 and considers a soft X-ray Balmer-break galaxy, but treats the radio-free ID as speculative.

quotes.quote: There are too many optically detected sources present to choose from. The nearby soft X-ray source does not have any detectable radio flux, but is present in the X-ray study of star-forming galaxies by 2002ApJ...576..625N . This Balmer-break galaxy has a redshift of 0.89 and an inferred SFR of 30 M_ yr ^ 1 . The small sub-mm flux density is still moderately high for this SFR, and without a radio detection we can only speculate that this is the correct ID.

gmw_00003231.name: SMMJ123659+621452
gmw_00003231.overview: SMMJ123659+621452 is an infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. It is located near two radio and ISO-detected objects that exhibit similar fluxes and photometric redshifts [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper describes two plausible redshifted radio/ISO/X-ray objects near SMMJ123659+621452 whose similar fluxes predict similar photometric redshifts.

quotes.quote: 7 \ to the South-east is a z=0.762 X-ray, 1.4 GHz radio and ISO detected object. 7 \ to the West lies a z=0.849 8.5 GHz radio and ISO detected object. This latter object also has a faint 1.4 GHz flux. Both objects have very similar radio and 15 \ fluxes, and hence both predict a similar redshift when using the 850 \ fluxes as a photometric redshift indicator.

gmw_00003232.name: SMMJ123741+621225
gmw_00003232.overview: SMMJ123741+621225 is an infrared galaxy associated with a northern radio source that overlaps with soft X-ray flux [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376]. Submillimeter Common-User Bolometer Array contours suggest the system is a very faint galaxy pair with some radio flux [arXiv:astro-ph/0408376].

sources.summary: The paper discusses a northern radio and soft X-ray source near SMMJ123741+621225, while the SCUBA contours favor a faint galaxy pair with radio flux.

quotes.quote: There is a radio source 7.6 \ to the North. It overlaps with soft X-ray flux as seen in the \ image, although it is not formally listed in the Alexander et al. (2003) \ catalogue. The SCUBA contours prefer a very faint galaxy pair which appears to have some radio flux.

gmw_00003233.name: NGC 5713
gmw_00003233.overview: NGC 5713 is a barred, multiple-arm spiral galaxy [arXiv:2206.10051] characterized by intensely star-forming regions and dust lanes [arXiv:0904.4252]. It is undergoing a strong tidal interaction with its companion galaxy NGC 5719 [arXiv:2206.10051], which has produced tidal tails and connecting tidal bridges [arXiv:2206.10051].

sources.summary: WiNDS presents NGC 5713 as a barred, multiple-arm spiral in a strongly interacting system with NGC 5719 and PGC 135857, producing tidal features and perturbed velocity fields.

quotes.quote: NGC 5713 is oriented nearly face-on, with inclination i 10^ , and shows a significant lopsided morphology. This can be seen in panel a) of Figure fig:candidate_ngc5713 , where we show its XDSS B band image. NGC5713 has an R_ opt 13.5 kpc, it is a barred and multiple arm spiral galaxy SABb-type 1991rc3..book.....D , and is located at 33.4 Mpc. The H_ V _ los map, shown on panel b) covers approximately a region of 0.9 R_ opt , and shows an amplitude of 100 \ km \ s^ 1 .

The galaxy is currently undergoing a very strong tidal interaction with its similar mass Sab-type companion, NGC 5719 2007A&A...463..883V , and shows two HI tidal tails. Both galaxies (NGC 5713 and NGC 5719) show an optical radius at a similar galactocentric distance and are connected by two HI tidal bridges. The projected distance between these two galaxies is 77 kpc. In addition, NGC 5713 is interacting with the Sm-type dwarf galaxy, PGC 135857, which is located at a projected distance of 64 kpc 2007A&A...463..883V .

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS imaging of NGC 5713 as a demanding test case for adaptive smoothing, emphasizing its complex structure, star-forming regions, and central stellar knots.

quotes.quote: In this section we test the performance of adaptsmooth on the image of a galaxy, using different modes and parameters. We choose NGC 5713 as imaged in band in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS, SDSS . This galaxy displays a complex enough structure so that the code can be tested in relatively extreme conditions, while the SDSS is chosen as representative of present-day sources of astronomical images.

The panel a) of Fig. fig:galaxy_adsmooth shows the original SDSS band image of the galaxy. Panels b) and ) show the adaptsmooth ed images obtained for a minimum S/N of 20, with median average, maximum smoothing level of 20 and no level cuts. A careful comparison of the two smoothed images shows that a number of localized sharp features that are seen in b) completely disappear, smoothed out, in ) . These include stars, but also star-forming regions and bright stellar knots in the central regions of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5713 as an intensely star-forming, dusty, peculiar galaxy where different stellar-population models and color methods strongly affect mass estimates.

quotes.quote: Contrary to the ``normal'' galaxy NGC 4321, NGC 5713 is characterized by intensely star forming regions and prominent dust lanes. Models based on CB07 and BC03 predict very different M/L especially in presence of very young stellar populations (see Fig. fig_SPS_c ), as shown in the top-right panel of Fig. fig_N5713_mass_comp . All blue regions (cf.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5713 as NGC 5719's interacting companion, linking its optical-radio velocity offset and gas-stellar decoupling to counter-torque effects.

quotes.quote: The top panel in Figure fig3 shows the DSS optical image of the young pair of galaxies, NGC 5719 and NGC 5713. Large quantities of hydrogen has been detected from the galaxies and the gas has been found to be counter-rotating with respect to the main stellar disk in NGC 5719 2007A&A...463..883V . HI is also detected from between the galaxies 2007A&A...463..883V . The old stellar disk in NGC 5719 is in retrograde rotation with NGC 5713 while the counter-rotating gas disk and 20 of stars in NGC 5719 are in prograde rotation with NGC 5713. We suggest that the torque acting on NGC 5719 due to NGC 5713 has modified the spin angular momentum of NGC 5719 leading to a change in direction of rotation of the gas disk. Moreover the HI major axis of NGC 5719 (PA= 121^ ) makes an angle of 25^ with the optical disk (PA= 96^ ) 2007A&A...463..883V . As an effect of the counter-torque on NGC 5713, we find that there is an offset of 65 kms ^ 1 in it optical and radio heliocentric velocities listed by Hyperleda. Thus, we infer that the torques have led to counter-rotation in NGC 5719 and change in its gas spin axis ie change in its spin angular momentum whereas a torque on NGC 5713 has led to a decoupling in the systemic motions of the gas and stellar disks.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5713 as the face-on Sbc companion interacting with NGC 5719, showing distortion, tidal tails, disturbed gas kinematics, and probable gas loss into the system.

quotes.quote: Its face-on Sbc companion NGC 5713 has an overall undisturbed look in the optical, but it shows a conspicuous bar-like distortion pointing toward NGC 5719 in the near-infrared (Mulchaey et al. 1997). The pair belongs to the rich LGC 386 group (Garcia 1993). The projected separation between the two galaxies is 77 kpc (11 5) and their systemic velocities differ by about 160 .

We speculate that a substantial fraction (at least 10 ) of the entire \ mass of the system that now resides in the tidal features around NGC 5719, may once have belonged to NGC 5713, which is presently relatively gas-poor for its morphological classification.

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 5713 has regions with excess optical attenuation relative to dust emission; as a distant sample galaxy, its Balmer-decrement extinction is relatively insensitive to dust mass.

quotes.quote: Three galaxies, NGC 2798, NGC 5713 and NGC 3077, have regions that even given the estimated errors would appear to be forbidden by a foreground screen model, having significantly more optical attenuation than would be expected given the amount of dust detected in emission.
NGC 5713 is the second most distant galaxy in our sample, and may suffer a similar effect.

We find that even within the range of distances and scales probed by our sample the sensitivity of A _V to dust mass changes, as in our most distant galaxies NGC 2146 and NGC 5713 the measured A _ V,g is relatively insensitive to the dust mass surface density.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5713 as a starburst/LIRG whose interaction with NGC 5719 likely supplied accreted gas and whose global CO ratio is lower than its nuclear excitation.

quotes.quote: The sample is dominated by red, dusty spirals such as NGC 2861, 3907B, 4030, 5478, 5690, 5740, CGCG 013-010, 018-077; but also contains blue low-surface-brightness disks (NGC 5496, 5584, 5691, 5705, UGC 09215), dust-lane early-types (NGC 5719, 5750), and the starburst/LIRG NGC 5713. NGC 5719 is noteworthy since its warped dust lane and counter-rotating gas and stellar disks indicate that dust and gas were accreted from an interaction with NGC 5713 Vergani2007,Coccato2011 . Perhaps surprisingly, the starburst NGC 5713 has a global R_ 32 of just 0.82 0.07 , although this is likely to be much higher in the nucleus measured a high excitation of R_ 31 =1.65^ +0.22 _ 0.29 in the central 15 Yao2003 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5713 as an isolated barred spiral with shallow gradients, though a possible bar-end break appears only in some diagnostics.

quotes.quote: The final division of our sample yields two barred isolated galaxies (NGC 2903 5713), three unbarred isolated galaxies (NGC 0628, 3938, 4254), one barred weakly interacting galaxy (NGC 0337 There is some evidence that NGC 0337 might be undergoing an interaction or minor merger. See notes for column 8 in Table tab:venga-subsample . ), and one unbarred weakly interacting galaxy (NGC 5194).
Specifically, the isolated barred galaxies (NGC 2903 NGC 5713) show very shallow N202-KD02 gradients of 0.33 0.01 in units of dex ( R / ) ^ 1 and -0.023/ 0.003 in units of dex kpc ^ 1 .

The case is less clear for our other isolated barred galaxy NGC 5713 which appears to show a break in the radial profile at the end of its bar using the R_ 23 based diagnostics but the same break is not seen in the other diagnostics, although the scatter in R_ 23 is large beyond the end of the bar.

gmw_00003234.name: SDSS J1458+459
gmw_00003234.overview: SDSS J1458+459 is a radio-quiet quasar that is coincident with an unresolved 43 Jy radio source [arXiv:1608.04586]. It is the only radio-quiet quasar in its sample with possible extended structure, which includes a second radio component of comparable flux density located 5 arcseconds to the east [arXiv:1608.04586].

sources.summary: The paper flags SDSS J1458+459 as the lone radio-quiet quasar with possible extended structure, noting an unresolved 43 Jy source and a nearby comparable component of uncertain association.

quotes.quote: With one possible exception, we did not find evidence for any extended structure in any member of the radio-quiet population. SDSS J1458+459 is coincident with an unresolved 43 Jy radio source. A second source having comparable flux density is located about 5 arcseconds to the east and close to a fainter un-cataloged optical feature.
