gmw_00003285.overview: BG1 is the central brightest cluster galaxy of the galaxy cluster Abell 3921 [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544]. The peak of the cluster's X-ray emission is offset by 17 arcseconds to the northwest of BG1's optical position [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544].

sources.summary: The paper identifies BG1 as A3921's central brightest galaxy, measures an X-ray centroid offset from its optical position, and argues the hot central gas is unlikely to be produced by BG1 alone.

quotes.quote: The two galaxies are at 7 ( 690 h_ 75 ^ 1 kpc; RA = 22^h48^m49^s , Dec = 64^o23 10 (J2000)) to the north-west (BG2) and at 8 ( 800 h_ 75 ^ 1 kpc; RA = 22^h49^m04^s , Dec = 64^o20 35 (J2000)) to the WNW (BG3) from the central brightest galaxy BG1 (RA = 22^h49^m58^s ; Dec= 64^o25 46 (J2000)). The peak of the X-ray emission is shifted by 17 \ to the NW with respect to the optical position of BG1.

In principle, while the high temperature of the first bin in the temperature profile might be produced by an active galactic nucleus (AGN) in the central galaxy BG1, we can find no evidence for a second component in the spectrum from this bin. In addition, the large extent of the spatial residuals (2 arcmin 130 kpc) make it unlikely that what we observe could be produced by BG1 alone.

gmw_00003286.name: BG2
gmw_00003286.overview: BG2 is a bright galaxy located in the western region of the galaxy cluster A3921 [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544]. It is believed to harbor a weak active galactic nucleus, supported by faint radio emission detected at 2.5 GHz and associated X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544].

sources.summary: BG2 is one of the western bright galaxies in A3921; its X-ray emission coincides with residual structure and is interpreted as possibly non-thermal emission from a weak central AGN.

quotes.quote: Within this extended residual structure there are two peaks, other than BG3, one of which coincides with the position of galaxy BG2. The residuals are elongated in the direction of the main cluster to the East and display a sharp edge towards the NW.

However, the non-thermal model implies that BG2 harbours an active nucleus. A3921 was observed with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) at 2.5 GHz and 1.5 GHz. The 2.5 GHz radio map (M. Hardcastle, private communication) shows faint radio emission in the centre of the optical counterpart of BG2. No radio emission is detected in the lower frequency observation (1.5 GHz), suggesting that the source may indeed be an AGN.

gmw_00003287.name: BG3
gmw_00003287.overview: BG3 is an X-ray bright galaxy located near the residual emission of the cluster A3921 [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544]. It is plausibly the central galaxy of a small group or a highly disrupted subcluster that exhibits extended thermal X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0409544].

sources.summary: BG3 is treated as an X-ray bright western galaxy near A3921's residual emission, plausibly the central galaxy of a disrupted subcluster or small group with extended thermal emission.

quotes.quote: We detect extended residuals, roughly centred on the galaxy BG3, at more than 5 significance, supporting the previous indications of the double nature of A3921. In this framework, BG3 would be the central galaxy of a subcluster which is already highly disrupted by interaction with the main cluster, in agreement with the analysis of Arnaud et al. ( ).
The X-ray spectrum of BG3, on the other hand, looks more thermal. The statistical quality here is slightly better, with 406 MOS+pn photons in the (0.3-3.0) keV energy range.

The relatively high X-ray luminosity suggests that BG3 belongs to the category of X-ray bright galaxies (e.g. Forman, Jones Tucker ; Trinchieri, Fabbiano, Canizares ), for which the diffuse X-ray emission from the IGM dominates over the emission of a population of non-resolved sources (mainly low mass X-Ray binaries; e.g., Sivakoff, Sarazin, Irwin ). However, the emission around BG3 looks more extended than that of a normal early-type galaxy. With our data we cannot exclude the possibility that the relatively high X-ray temperature is due to the emission from a small galaxy group with BG3 at its centre, and which presumably has not yet collided with the main cluster or subcluster.

gmw_00003288.name: ESO 153-G003
gmw_00003288.overview: ESO 153-G003 is classified as an S0 galaxy, though it may alternatively be an edge-on Sa galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It features a thin dust lane along its major axis and contains a rotating inner disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 153-G003 as an S0 or possible edge-on Sa galaxy with a major-axis dust lane, little outer rotation, inner velocity substructure, and a rotating inner disk.

quotes.quote: ESO 153-G003 Classified as an S0 galaxy in NED, ESO 153-G003 reveals a thin dust lane along the major axis and may actually be an edge-on Sa galaxy. We placed the slit along the minor axis for this galaxy. In this case, the velocity profile is generally consistent with no rotation in the outer regions. The inner regions suggest some velocity substructure but this could be due to a small misalignment of the slit by 1 arcsec or the major axis dust lane. The velocity dispersion profile is fairly flat within the inner region indicative of a rotating disk.

gmw_00003289.name: ESO 218-G002
gmw_00003289.overview: ESO 218-G002 is a galaxy that exhibits a large position-angle twist in its isophotes [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It displays a slow, roughly solid-body but lop-sided rotation profile and is surrounded by faint nearby sources [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper reports ESO 218-G002 has a large isophotal position-angle twist, nearby faint sources and possible background clustering, plus slow, roughly solid-body but lop-sided rotation.

quotes.quote: ESO 218-G002 From the imaging study of Reda et al. (2004), ESO 218-G002 has a large position angle twist in its isophotes but otherwise shows no obvious fine structure. ESO 218-G002 lies in the direction of the `Great Attractor' (Dressler, Faber Burstein 1991), and near the border of the so-called Norma cluster. Along the slit at radii 15^ '' on both sides of nucleus there is an excess of light due to faint sources which are too faint for redshift measurements.

It may be possible that there is a background cluster in the vicinity of ESO 218-G002. At 83 arcsec from ESO 218-G002 there is an X-ray source (1RXS J122104.3-523617). The velocity profile of ESO 218-G002 is roughly solid-body but lop-sided.

gmw_00003290.name: ESO 318-G021
gmw_00003290.overview: ESO 318-G021 is a galaxy that lacks any obvious fine structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. Its center contains a young nuclear stellar population, along with weak oxygen and possible hydrogen emission where the gas co-rotates with the stars [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper finds ESO 318-G021 lacks obvious fine structure, shows weak central oxygen and possible H emission, has gas co-rotating with stars, and contains a young nuclear stellar population.

quotes.quote: ESO 318-G021 The galaxy shows no obvious fine structure (Reda et al. 2004). There is weak [ OIII ] emission at the centre of the galaxy which is also slightly extended. The gas rotation is the same sense and same magnitude as the stars. There may also be some H emission but it is weak and rotation is not obvious. There is strong \ absorption indicative of a young nuclear stellar population.

gmw_00003291.name: NGC 682
gmw_00003291.overview: NGC 682 is classified as a featureless elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It exhibits a lop-sided velocity profile that suggests a possible central kinematically distinct core, while its Hermite terms indicate a non-equilibrium structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 682 as a featureless elliptical with lop-sided velocity, possible central kinematically distinct core, flat central dispersion, and Hermite terms suggesting non-equilibrium structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 682 The DSS and 2MASS imaging suggests a featureless elliptical. The velocity profile is lop-sided suggesting a possible central KDC of size 4 arcsec (1.4 kpc). In the same central regions the velocity dispersion profile is relatively flat. The hermite terms suggest a non-equilibrium structure. We note that the spectrum was taken at an angle intermediate to the major and minor axes.

gmw_00003292.name: NGC 1045
gmw_00003292.overview: NGC 1045 is a disturbed galaxy that is a possible recent merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It features a tidal plume, dust, boxy isophotes, and velocity substructure, with a velocity dispersion that peaks sharply at its center [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 1045 as a disturbed possible recent merger, with a tidal plume, dust, boxy isophotes, solid-body rotation, central velocity-dispersion peak, and velocity substructure.

quotes.quote: NGC 1045 reveals a tidal plume, evidence for dust and boxy isophotes (Reda et al. 2004), suggestive of a recent merger. The velocity profile shows solid-body rotation rising to 100 km/s at our largest radii of 25 arcsec (7.3 kpc). The velocity dispersion is sharply peaked at the galaxy centre. The 3 and 4 profiles (and the inner rotation profile) suggest some velocity substructure.

gmw_00003293.name: NGC 1162
gmw_00003293.overview: NGC 1162 is a fairly round, featureless elliptical galaxy with a major axis that is difficult to define [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. Its central regions show evidence of a rotating disk and a velocity dispersion dip [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1162 as a fairly round, featureless elliptical whose major axis is hard to define; its central regions show a rotating disk and dispersion dip.

quotes.quote: NGC 1162 The DSS and 2MASS imaging suggest a featureless elliptical, with fairly round inner regions. The galaxy major axis is therefore difficult to define. We estimate a position angle (P.A.) of around 51 degrees, whereas we placed our slit on an intermediate axis of P.A. = 280 degrees. The central regions show evidence for a rotating disk. The velocity dispersion profile reveals a dip in the central 3 arcsec.

gmw_00003294.name: NGC 2271
gmw_00003294.overview: NGC 2271 is an elongated early-type E/S0 galaxy located at a distance of approximately 31.8 Mpc [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It features an old stellar population [arXiv:1502.06148], strong rotation with kinematic signatures of an inner disk [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:astro-ph/0604452], and no visible dust features [arXiv:1502.06148].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 2271 as an isolated E/S0 with an old stellar population, inner disk signatures, a flat inner color profile, and no visible dust.

quotes.quote: NGC 2271 is a E/S0 at a surface brightness fluctuation distance of 31.8 Mpc tonry01 present in the catalogue of reda04 . reda07 measured an age of 11.5 0.5 Gyr, with no evidence of radial age or metallicity gradients, while hau06 found strong rotation and kinematic signatures of an inner disk, which is also found on optical imaging desouza04 .

NGC 2271 shows a flat color profile in the inner 30 along the major axis, with noticeable color gradient at larger radius (Fig. fig:colorn2271 ). This is the normal behaviour for early-type galaxies e.g. labarbera12 , and compatible with the negligible age and metalicity gradients found by reda07 inside 0.5R_e . No dusty features are visible.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2271 as an elongated early-type galaxy with literature claims of a tiny KDC, strong rotation, disk-like kinematics, but no confirmed KDC in these data.

quotes.quote: NGC 2271 The DSS and 2MASS imaging indicates an elongated ( = 0.3) early-type galaxy. Koprolin Zeilinger (2000) have suggested that NGC 2271 contains a KDC within the central 1.5 ^ '' although it is not obvious from their kinematic profiles. They measure a rotation between the flat part of the rotation curve and the central regions of 263 36 km/s and a velocity dispersion within the central 5 arcsec of _o = 237 7 km/s.
The 3 profile generally supports the presence of a disk. We are unable to confirm the claim of Koprolin Zeilinger (2000) of a KDC in NGC 2271.

gmw_00003295.name: NGC 4240
gmw_00003295.overview: NGC 4240 is a nearly round, featureless elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452] that is considered isolated [arXiv:1502.06148]. It features a kinematically distinct core [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:astro-ph/0604452], a flat velocity dispersion profile [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:astro-ph/0604452], and a north-south color asymmetry [arXiv:1502.06148].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 4240 as an isolated galaxy with mild age and metallicity gradients, a kinematically distinct core, uncertain globular-cluster bimodality, and notable color asymmetry.

quotes.quote: NGC 4240 was taken from the reda04 catalogue. reda07 found a central age of 7.5 Gyr followed by a mild age gradient. The existence of an also shallow metallicity gradient is explained as indication of a past major merger. It possesses a kinematically distinct core and a mostly flat velocity dispersion profile hau06 .

NGC 4240 shows a flatter color profile (Fig. fig:colorn4240 ), similar to the one measured on the field elliptical NGC 7507 salinas12 . This agrees with the shallow age and metallicity gradients found by reda07 . A notable feature is the (g-i) 0.15 color difference, for the inner 15 , between the South and North sections.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4240 as a nearly round, featureless elliptical with a central kinematically distinct core, lop-sided rotation, flat velocity dispersion, and central Hermite peaks.

quotes.quote: NGC 4240 The DSS imaging suggests a featureless, nearly round elliptical with a nearby bright star to the West. The galaxy reveals a KDC in the central 4 arcsec (5.0 kpc) and a lop-sided rotation (i.e. 100 km/s vs 50 km/s). The velocity dispersion profile is quite flat. The 3 and 4 profiles peak at the galaxy centre.

gmw_00003296.name: MCG-01-27-013
gmw_00003296.overview: MCG-01-27-013 is a featureless elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It exhibits a weak kinematically decoupled core within its central region, little to no rotation in its outer areas, and a non-equilibrium structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper describes MCG-01-27-013 as a featureless elliptical with weak central KDC evidence, little outer rotation, nearly constant velocity dispersion, and non-equilibrium Hermite profiles.

quotes.quote: MCG-01-27-013 The DSS and 2MASS imaging suggest a featureless elliptical. The velocity profile reveals evidence for a weak KDC within 5 arcsec (2.9 kpc) and little or no rotation in the outer regions. The velocity dispersion profile is fairly constant with radius. The hermite profiles indicate a non-equilibrium structure.

gmw_00003297.name: MCG-02-13-009
gmw_00003297.overview: MCG-02-13-009 is an E+A galaxy that exhibits oxygen emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. It features a centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile, slow rotation in its inner regions, and no detectable rotation in its outer parts [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper describes MCG-02-13-009 as an E+A, oxygen-emitting galaxy with faint neighboring galaxies, slow inner rotation, no clear outer rotation, and centrally peaked velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: MCG-02-13-009 The DSS imaging indicates several faint galaxies, but redshifts are not available for them. The galaxy has E+A type spectra and [ OIII ] emission. The galaxy shows some evidence of slow rotation in the inner regions but is consistent with no rotation in the outer parts. A centrally peaked velocity dispersion profile is seen.

gmw_00003298.name: MCG-03-26-030
gmw_00003298.overview: MCG-03-26-030 is a galaxy characterized by disky isophotes [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452]. Its velocity profile reveals rapid rotation in the inner 2.3 kiloparsecs that flattens to nearly 100 km/s in the outer regions, supported by a fairly constant velocity dispersion and a disky component [arXiv:astro-ph/0604452].

sources.summary: The paper reports MCG-03-26-030 has disky isophotes, rapid inner rotation that flattens near 100 km/s, fairly constant dispersion, and a Hermite profile indicating a disk component.

quotes.quote: MCG-03-26-030 The imaging study of Reda et al. (2004) found this galaxy to have disky isophotes. The velocity profile shows evidence for rapid rotation in the inner 4 arcsec (2.3 kpc), reaching a constant velocity of almost 100 km/s in the outer regions. The velocity dispersion is fairly constant. The 3 term shows an symmetric profile indicating a disky component is present.

gmw_00003299.name: SDSS J131341.57+471757.2
gmw_00003299.overview: SDSS J131341.57+471757.2 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0287 [arXiv:1307.2366]. It exhibits an irregular structure and contains a bright star-forming knot [arXiv:1307.2366].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the PTF11qcj host as a z=0.0287 SDSS galaxy whose emission contaminates SN spectra; SDSS-based host subtraction is complicated by irregular structure and a star-forming knot.

quotes.quote: PTF11qcj is located at = 13^ h 13^ m 41.51^ s , = +47^ 17 57 0 (J2000), at an angular distance of 0.68 from the nucleus of the galaxy SDSS Sloan Digital Sky Survey York2000 J131341.57+471757.2, at z=0.0287 (Figure scoperta ). Assuming H_0=71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _M=0.27 , _ =0.73 , this redshift correspond to a luminosity distance of d_L 124 Mpc.

Indeed, this spectrum and the ones taken after 2011 November 26 show the SN signal strongly mixed with underlying emission from the host galaxy. We have attempted to subtract this contribution using a spectrum of the host galaxy obtained by the SDSS survey http://skyserver.sdss3.org/dr9/en/get/specById.asp?id=1642855417023326208 . We scaled the host spectrum until the strong emission lines approximately matched in intensity the lines observed in our SN spectra, rebinning the spectra as needed to match the line width, and then subtracted the host spectrum (resampled onto the SN spectrum wavelength scale) from our observed spectra.

We stress that the host-subtraction procedure we attempted is quite rough due to the irregular nature of the host and an apparent bright star-forming knot seen in pre-explosion SDSS imaging, co-located with the SN.

gmw_00003300.name: B0128+437
gmw_00003300.overview: B0128+437 is a lenticular or late-type lensing galaxy [arXiv:1909.01349] that acts as a gravitational lens for a background quasar [arXiv:1909.01349, arXiv:1409.1326]. The galaxy's asymmetric mass distribution [arXiv:1909.01349] and rich, dusty interstellar medium [arXiv:astro-ph/0412677] are associated with flux-ratio and astrometric anomalies in the lensed images [arXiv:0912.2344, arXiv:1409.1326].

sources.summary: The paper models B0128+437 as a galaxy-scale gravitational lens whose asymmetric mass distribution and image subcomponents challenge simple smooth parametric lens models.

quotes.quote: The galaxy-scale gravitational lens B0128+437 generates a quadrupole-image configuration of a background quasar that shows milli-arcsecond-scale subcomponents in the multiple images observed with VLBI. As this multiple-image configuration including the subcomponents has eluded a parametric lens-model characterisation so far, we determine local lens properties at the positions of the multiple images with our model-independent approach. Using , we also succeed in setting up a global free-form mass density reconstruction including all subcomponents as constraints. We compare the model-independent local lens properties with those obtained by \ and those obtained by the parametric modelling algorithm . A comparison of all three approaches and a model-free analysis based on the relative polar angles of the multiple images corroborate the hypothesis that elliptically symmetric models are too simplistic to characterise the asymmetric mass density distribution of this lenticular or late-type galaxy.

B0128+437 is a galaxy-scale gravitational lens that has been controversially discussed in the literature. It was discovered by as a gravitational lensing configuration of four multiple images of a quasar. Three of these images show three subcomponents each in the radio band, , . So far, no lens model with a simple, smooth mass distribution has been found that can explain the positions of all four quasar images and their subcomponents.

sources.summary: The paper presents optical, infrared, and VLBI radio observations of CLASS B0128+437, finding faint optical detection, unresolved lens-galaxy separation, lensed-source-dominated infrared emission, jet substructure, and anomalous image B.

quotes.quote: We present new observations of the gravitational lens system CLASS B0128+437 made in the optical, infrared and radio regimes. Hubble Space Telescope observations detect only a very faint, extended object in I band with no obvious emission from the lensed images visible; no detection at all is made in V band. The lens system is detected with much higher signal to noise with UKIRT in K band and, although resolved, the resolution is not sufficient to allow the lensed images and the lens galaxy to be separated. A careful astrometric calibration, however, suggests that the peak of the infrared emission corresponds to the two merging images A and B and therefore that the lensed images dominate at infrared wavelengths.

The new radio data consist of high resolution VLBI radio images at three frequencies, 2.3, 5 and 8.4 GHz, made with the VLBA and the 100-m Effelsberg telescope. These reveal that the lensed source consists of three well-defined sub-components that are embedded in a more extended jet. Due to the fact that the sub-components have different spectral indices it is possible to determine, unambiguously, which part of each image corresponds to the same source sub-component. Our main finding is that one of the images, B, looks very different to the others, there being no obvious division into separate sub-components and the image being apparently both broader and smoother.

sources.summary: The paper describes CLASS B0128+437 as a compact four-image lensed z=3.12 quasar whose image B is scatter-broadened by a dusty, likely spiral lens galaxy ISM.

quotes.quote: Another lens system where scatter-broadening has been suggested is CLASS B0128+437 (Phillips et al., ). This is a four-image lens system, the lensed source being a z=3.12 quasar and the maximum separation of the images only 540 mas. Originally identified as a lens from MERLIN observations, subsequent follow-up with the VLBA at 5 GHz (Biggs et al. ) resolved each image into a jet with three prominent sub-components (Fig. vlbamaps ). The latter, however, were only present in three of the four imagesComparison between maps made with uniform and natural weighting indicated that the image with the missing sub-components was extended over larger angular scales than the other images, in conflict with what would be expected if lensing alone was determining the relative sizes of each image. The most natural explanation for this is that image B is being scatter-broadened in the ISM of the lensing galaxy.

Of the 22 lens systems that were discovered during the course of the JVAS/CLASS surveys, B0128+437 has proved to be one of the most interesting, both for the distortion of image B and the inability of smooth mass models to reproduce the observed image positions. Our new EVN map shows the area of scatter-broadening in image B extremely well and new NICMOS data confirm that a rich ISM obscures this image's line of sight. An area of star formation, possibly contained within a spiral arm, might be the origin of the dust and turbulent ionised gas.

sources.summary: The paper describes B0128+437 as a fold lens with a large flux anomaly, resolved jet components, and evidence favoring additional substructure in the lensing galaxy.

quotes.quote: B0128+437 is a system with both an extremely high flux anomaly, as seen at several different frequencies Biggs/Browne:2004,Phillips/Norbury:2000 , as well as multiple components in the source. Images A, C and D have been resolved into three different components embedded into a more extended jet. Since image B's components are not well-defined, we can only directly consider the integrated flux ratio here Biggs/Browne:2004 .

Observationally, this system exhibits a very large flux anomaly in the range of R=0.263 Koopmans/etal:2003 to R=0.582 Biggs/Browne:2004 . KGP have studied this system and found from simulations an expected flux anomaly in the range of 0.1 to 0.4 , with a preferred value around R=0.25 . This points unambiguously to additional substructure in the lensing galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper reports AO observations of B0128+437, detecting the lensing galaxy, measuring its K-band luminosity and mass-to-light ratio, and interpreting its lensing anomalies.

quotes.quote: We use high-resolution adaptive optics (AO) imaging on the Keck II telescope to study the gravitational lens B0128+437 in unprecedented detail, allowing us to resolve individual lensed quasar components and, for the first time, detect and measure properties of the lensing galaxy. B0128+437 is a small separation lens with known flux-ratio and astrometric anomalies. We discuss possible causes for these anomalies, including the presence of substructure in the lensing galaxy, propagation effects due to dust and a turbulent interstellar medium, and gravitational microlensing.

We use the Keck NIRC2 plus LGS AO to observe the gravitational lens system B0128+437, and we are able to confirm the presence of the lensing galaxy and clearly separate the lensing galaxy flux from the source galaxy flux for the first time. We take advantage of the high-quality AO imaging to present new science results for the B0128+437 lens system. These results can be summarized as follows: For the most probable redshift z_l=1.145 , we measure a total K-band luminosity _ 10 (L_K)=(11.07 0.05) h_ 70 ^ 2 L_ , an aperture luminosity within the Einstein ring radius _ 10 (L_ K, Ein )=(10.61 0.05) h_ 70 ^ 2 L_ , and a K-band M/L ratio M/L_ K,Ein =(0.68 0.05) h_ 70 M_ /L_ for the lensing galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats B0128+437 as a fold-caustic four-image lens with a z=3.124 quasar and favored z_l=1.145 lens; image positions fit but flux ratios remain discrepant.

quotes.quote: B0128+437 The fold-caustic lens B0128+437 consists of one bright image A, and three fainter images B, C and D. The images A and C are minima, and B and D are saddles. The quasar redshift is z_S=3.124 mckean2004 and that of the primary lensing galaxy is either z_L=0.645 or 1.145 lagattuta2010 . Combining with the previous photometric and spectroscopic data, the latter choice is favoured than the former mckean2004, lagattuta2010 . Therefore, we assume z_l=1.145 in what follows.
Although the positions of lensed images and the centroid of the primary lensing galaxy G can be fitted by an SIE plus an ES, the predicted flux ratios show discrepancy with the data.

sources.summary: The paper models CLASS B0128+437 as a lensed quasar with no flux-ratio anomaly, while noting Keck imaging evidence for a face-on late-type lens galaxy.

quotes.quote: From Figure  fig:mwdm , we also notice that the lens systems PG 1115+080 and CLASS B0128+437 show a clear preference for warmer models, especially towards the region where no detectable perturber is expected. This is due to the fact that these two systems show no flux-ratio anomaly, so that a smooth macro lens model can successfully predict the image flux ratios.

In our sample, Keck adaptive-optics IR imaging of CLASS B0128+437 shows evidence of a face-on late-type galaxy Mckean2004,Lagattuta2010 and the lens model for CLASS B2045+265 requires a significant elliptical component to the mass model mckean07 .

gmw_00003301.name: B0712+472
gmw_00003301.overview: B0712+472 is a quadruply imaged strong gravitational lens system consisting of a background radio-loud quasar at redshift z = 1.34 lensed by a foreground galaxy at z = 0.41 [arXiv:1701.06575, arXiv:1503.02074]. The lensing galaxy features a highly inclined, edge-on stellar disc [arXiv:1701.06575, arXiv:1905.04182] that explains its observed radio flux-ratio anomalies [arXiv:1701.06575, arXiv:1707.07680].

sources.summary: The paper models CLASS B0712+472 as a four-image lens whose inclined lens-galaxy disc can explain radio flux-ratio anomalies without requiring dark substructure.

quotes.quote: The gravitational lens system B0712+472 has four lensed images with a maximum separation of 1.274 arcseconds in a fold configuration (see Fig. fig:b0712radio ). It was discovered as part of the Cosmic Lens All-Sky Survey CLASS; CLASS1,CLASS2 , as reported by jackson98 . Spectroscopy of the system revealed that the lens galaxy is at redshift z_ = 0.41 , while the background object is a radio-loud AGN at z_s = 1.34 Fassnacht98 . The high-resolution imaging with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) showed that the lensing galaxy has a highly inclined disc, whilst the host galaxy of the background AGN is lensed into extended arcs in the optical and near-infrared (NIR) imaging.

Our disc + SIE model is able to reproduce the observed radio-wavelength flux ratios in the B0712+472 system without the need for additional dark-matter substructure. Of course our model does not rule out a possible substructure contribution to the image flux densities, but it demonstrates that a straightforward mass distribution that is motivated by the observed lensing galaxy morphology can explain the flux ratio anomaly in this system. Our work on the B0712+472 system is now the second case in which a disc component in the lensing galaxy can explain an observed significant flux-ratio anomaly in the fold images of a radio-loud lens system.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes B0712+472 as a four-image lens with marginal radio flux-ratio anomaly evidence and tests whether relaxing flux constraints improves macromodel fits.

quotes.quote: B0712+472 is a 4-image lens with an image configuration intermediate between a cusp and fold jack . The optical flux ratios strongly violate the cusp and fold relations, but at radio wavelengths the violation is marginal cuspreln,foldreln . The difference suggests that the optical flux ratios are affected by microlensing. We focus on the radio flux ratios, and fit the data given by jack00 .

The internal+external shear model differs from the data at only 88 per cent confidence, so it is a reasonably good fit. Nevertheless, it is interesting to consider whether the fit can be improved by relaxing the flux constraints, as shown in Table tb:0712tbl . Among three-flux models, only ABD yields a noticeably better fit.

sources.summary: The paper says B0712+472 is both a cusp and fold lens, with optical cusp-relation violation, close circular images, and radio/photometric behavior consistent with model predictions.

quotes.quote: B0712+472 is classified by KGP as both a cusp and a fold. B0712+472 violates the cusp relation, as discovered in Keeton et al. (2003), but only in optical data, which is evidence more for microlensing than for small-scale structure. The four images are relatively circular and easily distinguishable from each other, but there is a faint "bridge-like'' feature between components A and B, which are very close together Jackson/Nair:1998 , suggesting a slightly extended source at the highest resolution.

Spectroscopically, the quasar has a relatively flat spectrum which, along with most photometric estimates, imply a core-dominated source. The KGP Monte Carlo simulations predict R_ fold to be between -0.1 and 0.15. Our value for R_ fold from fitting the lens to an SIS+ES is -0.023, in agreement with the KGP simulations.

sources.summary: The paper describes B0712+472 as a fold-caustic lens with z=1.339 source and z=0.4060 lens, using radio fluxes and CASTLES astrometry; positions fit but flux ratios do not.

quotes.quote: B0712+472 The fold-caustic lens B0712+472 consists of two bright images A and B, and two fainter images C and D. The source and lens redshifts are z_S=1.339 and z_L=0.4060 fassnacht1998 . We use the radio flux ratios in koopmans2003 of four images at 5 GHz averaged over a period of 8.5 months. For the astrometry, we use the data from CASTLES data base. The positions of lensed images and the centroid G of the primary lensing galaxy are well fitted by an SIE and an ES assuming that the error in the position of G is 0.05 arcsec.
However, the flux ratios are not well fitted.

sources.summary: The paper treats B0712+472 as a confirmed edge-on disc lens whose observed flux-ratio anomaly can be reproduced more readily when baryonic disc structure is included.

quotes.quote: The idea that baryonic components such as edge-on discs can generate perturbations to the lensing potential similar to those produced by dark substructures and, therefore, can cause flux-ratio anomalies in lensed quasars has been supported by previous investigations of the B1555+375 and B0712+472 systems Hsueh2016,Hsueh17 . Additionally, Gilman2017 have shown that the luminous structure in elliptical lenses may account for 10 to 15 percent of flux-ratio anomalies. In this work, we select galaxies in the Illustris simulation with well-developed disc structure and compare them to typical elliptical lenses.

In recent investigations of two confirmed edge-on disc lenses, B0712+472 and B1555+375, Hsueh2016,Hsueh17 show that the inclusion of exponential disc components in the lens model is sufficient to reproduce the observed flux-ratio anomalies without any need to invoke dark matter substructure. Those results have motivated the work presented in this paper. We have found that our general disc lens sample, which includes a range of inclination angles, has a higher probability of causing high flux anomaly systems than the elliptical lens sample.

sources.summary: The paper treats B0712+472 as the second-strongest variability case: its B/A ratios are deviant, C/A is less significant, and the evidence remains weak.

quotes.quote: In the case of B0712+472, the reduced ^2 values of the (B/A)_ n flux-ratio curves and also _r^2 seem more significant. In Fig.1 we see that a large number of epochs are deviant over the entire observing season. Deviations of the (C/A)_ n flux-ratio curve from unity are less significant, probably because image C has a larger fractional error than images A and B. Even though there is some evidence in this system for extrinsic variability between the two fold images, we conservatively regard it also as weak and we will concentrate our discussion on B2045+265. In Sect.4, however, we further discuss what a possible reason for some of the higher values of _r^2 and extrinsic variability can be.
It is interesting to note that B0712+472, probably the system with second-best evidence for extrinsic variability in our sample, also has a low Galactic latitude b=+23^ .

sources.summary: The paper uses B0712+472 to test measurement uncertainties, finding that adding positional and flux-ratio noise only mildly reduces wiggles and does not make the lens model acceptable.

quotes.quote: We turn instead to B0712+472. We assume the image positions to be precise to within 3 mas, which is slightly conservative compared to the 1 mas uncertainties claimed by Jackson_0712_position . For the flux ratios, we use the data of Koopmans_0712_flux , who found the uncertainties in the flux ratios of images B, C, and D relative to A to be 7.2 , 8.9 , and 43 , respectively.

Figure fig:uncertainty shows the results for B0712+472. As we would expect, including measurement uncertainties produces models with slightly smaller wiggles. However, the changes are not significant enough to transform an implausible model into an acceptable solution.

sources.summary: The paper describes CLASS B0712+472 as a galaxy-scale strong lens, giving its discovery context, lens or source properties, image configuration, and relevant environmental or model information for the survey.

quotes.quote: CLASS B0712+472 (herein B0712) was discovered as part of the JVAS/CLASS patnaik92b, jackson1995 survey of flat spectrum radio sources. Follow-up imaging revealed a quadruply imaged z_s 1.33 quasar and a V=22.2 early-type lensing galaxy jackson1998 . The maximum image separation is 1.27 . fassnacht1998 confirm z_l=0.4060 and z_s=1.339 . B0712 is a flux anomaly lens with the major flux density discrepancy involving the B and D images.

sources.summary: The paper reports radio and optical measurements for B0712+472, finding weak radio evidence but high-confidence optical flux-ratio anomalies that make it promising for microlensing monitoring.

quotes.quote: B0712+472 Jackson et al. \ (1998) give three different measurements of the radio fluxes for B0712+472 at 5 GHz and one measurement at 15 GHz. The optical and near-IR data from CASTLES (also see Jackson, Xanthopoulos, Browne 2000) yield =0.619 0.050 in V-band, 0.572 0.147 in I-band, and 0.473 0.092 in H-band. Thus, there is evidence for a radio flux ratio anomaly, but only at the 96--98 confidence level. At optical/near-IR wavelengths, by contrast, there is no overlap between the observed and predicted probability curves, and hence evidence for an optical flux ratio anomaly at high confidence. That possibility makes B0712+472 a promising system for optical monitoring to look for variability that would indicate microlensing.

sources.summary: The paper includes CLASS B0712+472 in lens modeling with an edge-on stellar disc that explains much of its flux-ratio anomaly and contributes about 15 percent within the Einstein radius.

quotes.quote: CLASS B0712+472 and CLASS B1555+375 both have an edge-on disc that lies across the merging images, where the flux-ratio anomalies are most significant Jackson1998,Hsueh2016,Hsueh2017 . We apply the best-fit models found by Hsueh2017,Hsueh2016 and let the disc mass be the only free parameter.

The disc mass fraction of CLASS B0712+472 and CLASS B1555+375 is about 15 per cent within the Einstein radius, which is consistent with the range of disc mass fraction in the SWELLS survey Brewer2014 .

Here, we have already included edge-on discs as a higher-order component in the lens models for the cases of CLASS B0712+472 and CLASS B1555+375, since these two lenses show solid evidence of stellar discs in high-resolution IR imaging Hsueh2016,Hsueh2017 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies B0712+472 as a radio cusp-caustic lens whose flux-ratio anomaly is not unlikely under extragalactic CDM halo simulations.

quotes.quote: The three with R_ cusp measured in the radio (B0712+472, B2045+265 and B1422+231) clearly violate the relation although B1422+231 is less clear than the others.

We can also compare figure fig:r_prob1422 to lens B0712+472 which has a similar configuration to B1422+231 although a lower source redshift. It is easily seen that its value of R_ cusp =0.26 0.02 is not particularly unlikely (there is a 12 probability of it being larger) and thus does not require an additional explanation beyond the expected population of extragalactic halos.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B0712+472 as a fold-lens anomaly: its optical fluxes strongly violate the fold relation, while radio data do not, implying likely microlensing.

quotes.quote: Comparing these predictions with the data, we find that five of the the 12 known lenses with fold configurations have flux ratio anomalies: B0712+472, SDSS 0924+0219, PG 1115+080, B1555+375, and B1933+503.

B0712+472: The optical data grossly violate the fold relation, but the radio data do not (as in the cusp relation; see Paper I). The wavelength dependence suggests that the optical anomaly is caused by microlensing.

gmw_00003302.name: B2045+265
aliases_external: GB6 J2047+2643:[FBC99] E, lens galaxy of B 2045+265
gmw_00003302.overview: B2045+265 is an early-type lensing galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0507056] that is likely elliptical [arXiv:1511.01097, arXiv:astro-ph/0210318], exhibiting star-forming spectral features [arXiv:astro-ph/0507056] and extrinsic flux-ratio variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0302189]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens for a background quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0502436, arXiv:1905.04182], producing a tight four-image cusp configuration with a strong flux-ratio anomaly [arXiv:astro-ph/0502436, arXiv:1905.04182].

sources.summary: The paper models B2045+265 as a cusp-caustic lens with z=1.28 source and z=0.8673 lens; a possible companion G2 enables an excellent fit but has extreme ellipticity.

quotes.quote: B2045+265 The cusp-caustic lens B2045+265 consists of three bright images A, B, and C and one faint image D. The source and lens redshifts are z_S=1.28 and z_L=0.8673 fassnacht1999 . We use the radio flux ratios koopmans2003 of four images at 5 GHz averaged over a period of 8.5 months. For the astrometry, we use the infrared components of B2045 obtained by adaptive optics imaging at 2.2 m mckean2007 . In addition to a primary lensing galaxy G1 at z_L , a possible companion galaxy G2 may reside near G1 though the redshift has not been known mckean2007 .

All the observed data are fitted extremely well by an SIE(for G1)+ES(for environment)+SIE(for G2) model giving ^2_ tot / dof =0.03 . However, the best-fitting ellipticity of G2 seems too large e( G2 )=0.867 . Such a large value can only be expected from either an edge-on disc system or a tidally disrupted dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper discusses B2045+265 as probably elliptical rather than late-type, requiring a large lens mass and having possible group and dwarf-satellite evidence.

quotes.quote: B2045+265 (hereafter 2045 ) is probably an elliptical galaxy 2007MNRAS.378..109M . It was initially classified as a late-type Sa galaxy 1999AJ....117..658F , however the velocity dispersion seems too high. As the source redshift is rather low, a large lens mass is required. 2007MNRAS.378..109M therefore conclude that it is more likely an elliptical galaxy. North-west of the lens there is evidence for a potential group at a similar redshift as the lens 1999AJ....117..658F .

There is furthermore evidence for a dwarf satellite galaxy within the Einstein radius of the lens system 2007MNRAS.378..109M . As the measurements are however inconclusive, we choose to not include this satellite in our models.

sources.summary: The paper treats B2045+265 as a tight four-image lensed quasar whose radio and optical cusp-relation violation requires models with relaxed flux constraints.

quotes.quote: B2045+265 is a 4-image radio and optical lens with the source quasar at redshift z_s = 1.28 and the lens galaxy at redshift z_l = 0.87 fas . It is the tightest known cusp configuration lens, and it exhibits a strong violation of the cusp relation in both radio and optical bands cuspreln . We seek to fit the 5 GHz MERLIN radio data from fas , taking radio component E to indicate the position of the lens galaxy.

Trying to fit all of the fluxes still gives a poor fit ( ^2/ = 90/4 ). Dropping one of the flux constraints improves the fit, but even the best case is still not an acceptable fit (the ACD model has ^2/ = 20/3 ). Dropping two flux constraints, however, yields three acceptable models.

sources.summary: The paper measures B 2045 + 265 as a lensing early-type galaxy with star-forming spectral features, high dark-matter fraction, and a total density slope flatter than isothermal.

quotes.quote: For B 2045 + 265, observation was made on 2002 June 13 (UT). The slit was placed along the major axis of the lensing galaxy (PA =120.1^ ). We obtained seven 1800 seconds exposures, and the total exposure time was 3.5 hours.

For B 2045 + 265, although signal-to-noise ratio of the observed spectrum is worse than that of HST 14113 + 5211 due to both fainter apparent brightness and shorter exposure time achieved for the galaxy, we have clearly detected Ca II H and K absorption lines and G band feature (Fig fig:spectrum-b ). We note, however, that these features are less prominent than those of HST 14113 + 5211. We also note that B 2045 + 265 shows another possible prominent absorption feature at just red edge of the A band feature.

sources.summary: The paper describes G2 as a tidally disrupted dwarf galaxy in CLASS B2045+265 that may explain a flux-ratio anomaly, though its required flattening makes that attribution uncertain.

quotes.quote: Recent deep Hubble Space Telescope and Keck imaging of this system by Mc07 have revealed the presence of a tidally disrupted dwarf galaxy G2. This may be the cause of the flux ratio anomaly, although caution is needed as modelling suggests that G2 must be very highly flattened ( q = 0.13 ).

The most obvious examples are the Sixth Object in MG0414+0534 Sc93 and G2 in CLASS B2045+265 Mc07 . In both these cases, a single piece of substructure gives a substantial improvement in the fit of a smooth model of the lens galaxy.
A hint that this may sometimes be the case is given by the unrealistically large flattening deduced for satellite G2 in CLASS B2045+265 using model-fitting in Mc07 .

sources.summary: The paper models G2 as the companion galaxy in B2045+503, noting its lens-plane redshift, high ellipticity, fitted parameters, and inability to remove the observed parity anomaly.

quotes.quote: B2045+503 exhibits a cusp configuration with three images positioned near each other. Its source and lens redshifts have been measured at z_ s =1.28 and z_ l =0.867 , respectively fassnacht_etal99 . The companion galaxy (G2) lies between the lensing galaxy (G1) and the cusp images and has a photometric redshift consistent with the lensing galaxy mckean_etal07 . The best-fit smooth model by mckean_etal07 for G1 and G2 finds that the ellipticity of G1 is oriented 90 degrees away from the cusp images while a large external shear is pointed along the line from G1 to the cusp images. In addition, their best-fit model for G2 is very elliptical ( q=0.133 ) and pointed along the same direction as the shear.

Including the previously found best-fit parameters for G2 -- Einstein radius, axis ratio, orientation, and position -- we can significantly reduce the ^2 of our original best-fit, but the magnification perturbations are not reduced in this case: _ f,- < 0 and _ f,- < _ f, all others .

sources.summary: The paper treats B2045+265 as a flux-anomaly cusp lens whose image flux ratios may be explained if the source quasar lies in a positive fold-relation region.

quotes.quote: A typical flux-anomaly cusp type lens is B2045+265 fas99,mck07 , whose cusp relation is 0.501 0.035 koo03 . The middle image has the smallest flux among the triplet, so B2045+265 was thought to strongly violate the cusp relation kee03,mck07 . Similarly, without considering the perturbation of the substructures, we suppose that the source quasar lies in the positive region of fold relation map. Then, we can naturally understand that the fluxes of two side images can surpass that of the middle image, and the flux ratios can be thought to be normal. In Fig. 6(b), the smaller the negative region is, the easier the fluxes of two side images surpass that of the middle one.

sources.summary: The paper finds B2045+265 has robust radio and optical flux-ratio anomalies, with image B too faint for smooth lens potentials and the lens galaxy consistent with an elliptical.

quotes.quote: Despite a suggestion by M oller et al. \ (2003) that the lens galaxy in B2045+265 might be a spiral, its structural and dynamical properties are fully consistent with being an elliptical (Rusin et al. \ 2003a), and no disk-like structure is evident in Hubble Space Telescope images (C. Kochanek, private communication).

B2045+265 Fassnacht et al. \ (1999) give eight measurements of the radio fluxes for B2045+265 at 1.4, 5, 8.5, and 15 GHz, from different radio arrays with different resolutions. The weighted average of the optical/near-IR data yields =0.506 0.013 ; the excellent agreement with the radio data suggests that the measured value of is robust and independent of wavelength, and that the small inferred uncertainties on are realistic. Rcusp-obs joint show that the existence of a flux ratio anomaly in B2045+265 is beyond doubt. Image B is simply much too faint to be consistent with smooth lens potentials, no matter which input ellipticity and octopole distributions are used.

sources.summary: The paper finds unambiguous extrinsic flux-ratio variability in B2045+265, argues Galactic refractive scintillation is the likely cause, and notes longer-timescale correlated structure.

quotes.quote: One gravitational lens systems, B2045+265, shows unambiguous evidence for extrinsic variability, based on the reduced ^2 values significantly larger than unity (Tables 1 2) and visually apparent long-term variations in its flux-ratio curves (Fig.1). One possible explanation for the variations is radio microlensing similar to B1600+434 (Koopmans de Bruyn 2000, 2003). However, because B2045+265 has a Galactic latitude b 10^ and is the lowest Galactic latitude system in our sample, Galactic refractive scintillation is the more likely explanation.

Refractive scintillation could therefore explain the observed extrinsic variations up to a time-scale of possibly several weeks in B2045+265 for reasonable lensed-images sizes. However, the structure function shows correlated variations on time-scales that are much longer.

sources.summary: The paper treats B2045+265 as an extreme cusp-caustic lens but finds its observed anomaly compatible with CDM halos and host-lens substructure.

quotes.quote: The three with R_ cusp measured in the radio (B0712+472, B2045+265 and B1422+231) clearly violate the relation although B1422+231 is less clear than the others.

Lens B2045+265 is a more extreme cusp caustic case. Figure fig:r_prob2045cosmic shows the results for simulations with just extragalactic CDM halos. With a halo mass range of 10^6 <m<10^9 the observed R_ cusp does not appear strongly disfavored -- 15 chance of it being larger. Considering the additional substructure within the host lens, the observed R_ cusp seems perfectly consistent with CDM.

sources.summary: The paper finds B2045+265 requires very high-order multipoles and retains localized deviations near the bright images, suggesting structure local to the images rather than smooth lens ellipticity.

quotes.quote: Let us now turn our attention to B2045+265. We find models that fit the data of Fassnacht_2045 exactly. Unlike the case of B1422+231, the isodensity contour we obtain for =9 is completely unreasonable (see Figure fig:2045_order9 ) and we must include multipoles of order 17 to obtain a remotely plausible model (see Figure fig:2045_order17 ).

Similar to B1422+231, we find that including higher order multipoles reduces the RMS wiggle, but pronounced deviations from ellipticity remain primarily near the bright images. This suggests that the structure required to fit the anomaly in B2045+265 truly is local to the images.

sources.summary: The paper discusses CLASS B2045+265 as a lensed quasar with a strong cusp-image flux anomaly, rejecting a proposed luminous satellite as a foreground star.

quotes.quote: The lens system CLASS B2045+265 shows a strong de-magnification on the central image of the cusp triplet Fassnacht1999 . This strong flux-ratio anomaly was thought to be due to the presence of a luminous satellite detected in NIR imaging, although the lens model was peculiar in that the satellite needed to be highly elognated mckean07 . However, additional Keck adaptive optics imaging at a different epoch has shown proper motion of the luminous object, which indicates that it is very likely to be a star. Therefore, we do not include this additional component in the lens model.

gmw_00003303.overview: The active galactic nucleus 1546 + 027 [arXiv:1101.3284] is classified as a high-polarization flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1203.6071]. Its optical spectrum resembles a regular broad-line quasar [arXiv:1203.6071], while its broadband spectral energy distribution exhibits a double-humped profile [arXiv:1101.3284].

sources.summary: The paper uses MOJAVE source 1546 + 027 as an example, showing its broadband SED has good frequency coverage and is reasonably fit by two polynomial components.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:1SED shows the broadband SED of the MOJAVE source 1546 + 027. The frequency coverage of the SED is reasonably good, see also Chang (2010) for details . As shown in Figure fig:1SED , two second-order polynomial fits describe the double-humped SED reasonably good.

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSSJ154929.43 + 023701.1 as a high-polarization FSRQ whose SDSS spectrum resembles a regular broad-line quasar rather than a featureless blazar.

quotes.quote: SDSSJ154929.43 + 023701.1 . This object is classified as an FSRQ hea07 with high polarization sca97 . The optical spectrum from SDSS, however, contains strong , , and even \ lines. Even some weak forbidden lines are prominent, e.g., the two [ Ne v ] lines around 3400 . This is another case in which a blazar has a regular broad line quasar spectrum.

gmw_00003304.name: Cosmic Eye
gmw_00003304.overview: The Cosmic Eye is a strongly lensed Lyman-break galaxy at a redshift of approximately 3.07 [arXiv:1310.2655, arXiv:1612.00805]. It exhibits continuous star formation [arXiv:0910.0840], a possible post-starburst nature [arXiv:1408.0816], and complex interstellar absorption from both outflowing and redshifted gas [arXiv:0910.0840, arXiv:1612.00805].

sources.summary: The paper includes the Cosmic Eye as a strongly lensed z=3.07 Lyman-break comparison galaxy, using combined photometry while noting blending and possible differential magnification.

quotes.quote: For comparison, our sample includes two previously well-studied lensed galaxies, MS1512-cB58 and the Cosmic Eye, for which we also provide updated Herschel measurements. We perform SED-fits of the full photometry of each object as well for the optical and infrared parts separately, exploring various star formation histories, using different extinction laws, and exploring the impact of nebular emission.

Cosmic Eye: This is an equally strongly lensed Lyman break galaxy (LBG) at z=3.07 discovered by 2007ApJ...654L..33S . It is magnified by a factor of =28 3 times by a foreground z = 0.37 cluster and a z = 0.73 massive early-type spiral galaxy 2007MNRAS.379..308D . For our work we use the combined photometry of 2007ApJ...665..936C and 2009ApJ...698.1273S .

The Cosmic Eye is surrounded by several other equally infrared-bright objects. Figure fig:eye250 shows the SPIRE 250 m contours of the region around the Eye overlaid on top of an HST/ACS optical (F606W) image. Also overlaid on the same image is a MIPS 24 m redscale, indicating four sources of infrared emission other than the Cosmic Eye within the same SPIRE resolution element.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Eye as a rare z>3 lensed star-forming galaxy with CO detections, an inferred low molecular gas fraction, and possible post-starburst character.

quotes.quote: We adopt for these two strongly-lensed galaxies at z_ H = 2.729 and 3.0733, respectively, the CO(1--0) integrated fluxes obtained with the Expanded Very Large Array (EVLA) by riechers10 . The Eye is one of the rare z>3 star-forming galaxies with a CO detection.

Detections of the CO(3--2) line with the PdBI and measurements of their respective integrated fluxes were also reported by baker04 in cB58 and coppin07 in the Eye. Those are used in Sect. sect:luminosity-corrections to study the CO luminosity correction factors of star-forming galaxies at z>1 .

Combined with our updated stellar masses from sklias14 , we infer lensing-corrected molecular gas fractions f_ gas = 0.41 in cB58 and f_ gas = 0.18 in the Eye. Although they are very similar to the molecular gas fractions reported by riechers10 , f_ gas = 0.32 in cB58 and f_ gas = 0.13 in the Eye, if we consider the stellar masses assumed by Riechers et al. \ and our prescriptions for the CO luminosity correction factor and the CO--H _2 conversion factor, the corresponding molecular gas fractions get significantly higher by a factor of 2 and 2.5, respectively, compared to ours. The revised molecular gas fraction of the Eye is particularly low, it is the lowest among z>1 SFGs with CO measurements from the literature (see Sect. sect:fgas ). This may partly be attributed to the ``post-starburst'' nature of the Eye as inferred by sklias14 from a detailed SED analysis.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the lensed z=3.07331 Lyman-break galaxy Cosmic Eye, finding complex interstellar absorption from outflowing and redshifted gas plus young stars with about 0.4 solar metallicity.

quotes.quote: We report the results of a study of the rest-frame ultraviolet (UV) spectrum of the Cosmic Eye (J213512.73 010143), a luminous ( L 2 L^ ) Lyman break galaxy at z_ sys =3.07331 magnified by a factor of 25 via gravitational lensing by foreground mass concentrations at z = 0.73 and 0.33. The spectrum, recorded at high resolution and signal-to-noise ratio with the ESI spectrograph on the Keck ii telescope, is rich in absorption features from the gas and massive stars in this galaxy.

The interstellar absorption lines are resolved into two components of approximately equal strength and each spanning several hundred \ in velocity. One component has a net blueshift of 70 \ relative to the stars and H ii regions and presumably arises in a galaxy-scale outflow similar to those seen in most star-forming galaxies at z = 2 -3. The other is more unusual in showing a mean redshift of +350 \ relative to z_ sys ; possible interpretations include a merging clump, or material ejected by a previous star formation episode and now falling back onto the galaxy, or more simply a chance alignment with a foreground galaxy.

The C iv P Cygni line in the stellar spectrum is consistent with continuous star formation with a Salpeter initial mass function, stellar masses from 5 to 100 , and a metallicity Z 0.4 Z_ . Compared to other well-studied examples of strongly lensed galaxies, we find that the young stellar population of the Cosmic Eye is essentially indistinguishable from those of the Cosmic Horseshoe and MS 1512-cB58.

sources.summary: The Cosmic Eye has CO(3-2) IRAM PdBI observations, a measured line width and flux, CO associated with component B1, and an adopted total-system magnification correction.

quotes.quote: Eye The Cosmic Eye was observed in the CO(3-2) line with the IRAM PdBI coppin07 . The line width is 190 24 and the total line flux integrated over the line is 0.50 0.07 Jy . The CO emission appears to be spatially associated with component B _1 of the system, and coppin07 therefore suggest that the appropriate magnification correction factor for the CO line flux is 8 rather than the value of 30 found for the entire system dye07 . However, riechers10 report a detection of the CO(1-0) with the VLA that is spatially consistent with the bulk of the rest-frame UV emission, leading them to conclude that the total magnification value of the system should be used. This is the approach we adopt here.

sources.summary: The paper presents the Cosmic Eye as a strongly lensed z=3.073 LBG whose high-resolution Keck/ESI spectrum resolves redshifted absorption interpreted as gas accretion.

quotes.quote: Keck/ESI coverage of many of these transitions in a spectrum of the ``Cosmic Eye'' is shown in Fig.  fig:6 , adapted from . The Cosmic Eye is a Lyman break galaxy (LBG) at z=3.073 which is heavily magnified by foreground massive structures, producing two overlapping arcs on the sky. The Keck/ESI spectroscopy obtained by has a FWHM velocity resolution 75  km s^ 1 , and successfully resolves at least two distinct absorption components in several transitions. In fact, the authors note that this spectrum exhibits a strong, redshifted absorption component with v 350  km s^ 1 in addition to a blueshifted component presumably arising from winds. This may be interpreted as yet another detection of gas accretion, the first at z 3 .

gmw_00003305.name: 2MASX J16093905-3203443
gmw_00003305.overview: The galaxy 2MASX J16093905-3203443 is part of an interacting galaxy pair [arXiv:2109.07942]. This interacting system is expected to contain relatively high amounts of dust originating from active star formation [arXiv:2109.07942].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J16093905-3203443 as SN 2018bsz's interacting host, deriving host extinction from a MUSE spectrum and noting the pair may contain dust from active star formation.

quotes.quote: Several sources can contribute to the extinction towards SN 2018bsz: a foreground (Milky Way) component; interstellar extinction within the host galaxy 2MASX J16093905-3203443; and (potentially) any circumstellar extinction surrounding the progenitor. The first of these components can be determined from Milky Way dust maps to be E(B-V) _ MW =0.214 mag 2011ApJ...737..103S . The latter two components are more problematic, and we consider a number of indirect approximate methods to measure them.

We extracted the host galaxy spectrum from our MUSE datacube at the position of SN 2018bsz using a circular shape aperture with 0 .6 radius. After correcting for Milky Way extinction, we fitted and removed the stellar continuum using starlight 2005MNRAS.358..363C , and then measured the Balmer decrement in the spectrum. Assuming that the intrinsic value of the Balmer decrement of H /H is 2.86 1989agna.book.....O , we found E(B-V) _ host to be 0.32 mag. The host of SN 2018bsz is part of an interacting galaxy pair, where in principle one may expect relatively high amounts of dust from active star formation e.g. 2013ApJ...768...90L .

gmw_00003306.name: J0313-1806
gmw_00003306.overview: J0313-1806 is a high-redshift quasar located at z=7.642 [arXiv:2110.10176]. It is powered by a supermassive black hole that shines 680 million years after the Big Bang [arXiv:2110.10176] and accretes at a low Eddington rate [arXiv:2110.10176].

sources.summary: J0313--1806 is described as the z=7.642 record quasar, powered by a massive SMBH 680 Myr after the Big Bang; its low accretion rate tightens black-hole seed constraints.

quotes.quote: Currently, the record for the highest redshift quasar is held by J0313--1806, a source at z=7.642 powered by a SMBH of (1.6 0.4) 10^9 Wang_2021 . Based on its redshift, J0313--1806 is shining a mere 680 Myr after the Big Bang.

Interestingly, the two quasars J1243+0100 and J0313--1806 show much tighter parameter spaces, because their estimated Eddington ratios are = 0.34 0.2 Matsuoka_2019a and = 0.67 0.14 Wang_2021 , respectively. These quasars are accreting at lower Eddington rates (i.e., at a slower pace), and this significantly tightens the allowed parameter space, with M_ , seed > 10^5 for J1243+0100 and M_ , seed > 10^ 3.5 for J0313--1806, while also requiring lower radiative efficiencies 0.1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats J0313-1806 as one of four most distant quasars whose SMBH can be explained by a Supermassive Dark Star heavy-seed pathway rather than only direct collapse.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we demonstrate that Supermassive Dark Stars (SMDSs) offer an equally valid solution to the mystery of the first quasars, by examining the four most distant known quasars: UHZ1, J0313-1806, J1342+0928, and J1007+2115, with particular emphasis on UHZ1.

In this paper we point out an alternative interpretation: the mystery posed by the SMBHs powering the most distant four quasars (UHZ1, J0313-1806, J1342+0928, and J1007+2115) can be equally well solved by SMBHs seeded by Supermassive Dark Stars.
Thus, SMDSs can provide natural seeds for the SMBHs powering the most distant quasars: UHZ1, J0313–1806, J1342+0928, J1007+2115, for which a low mass seed is highly unlikely  Bogdan:2023UHZ1 .

gmw_00003307.name: J1007+2115
gmw_00003307.overview: J1007+2115 is one of the most distant known quasars in the universe [arXiv:2312.13837]. The supermassive black hole powering this distant quasar may have been seeded by a supermassive dark star [arXiv:2312.13837].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1007+2115 as one of four most distant quasars whose SMBH can be explained by a Supermassive Dark Star heavy-seed pathway rather than only direct collapse.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we demonstrate that Supermassive Dark Stars (SMDSs) offer an equally valid solution to the mystery of the first quasars, by examining the four most distant known quasars: UHZ1, J0313-1806, J1342+0928, and J1007+2115, with particular emphasis on UHZ1.

In this paper we point out an alternative interpretation: the mystery posed by the SMBHs powering the most distant four quasars (UHZ1, J0313-1806, J1342+0928, and J1007+2115) can be equally well solved by SMBHs seeded by Supermassive Dark Stars.
Thus, SMDSs can provide natural seeds for the SMBHs powering the most distant quasars: UHZ1, J0313–1806, J1342+0928, J1007+2115, for which a low mass seed is highly unlikely  Bogdan:2023UHZ1 .

gmw_00003308.name: [ HB89 ] 1352-104
gmw_00003308.overview: [ HB89 ] 1352-104 is a quasar that exhibits detectable brightness variations [arXiv:2006.15045]. During the Kepler K2 Campaign 06, the object experienced a short outburst lasting three days, with visible variability both before and after the event [arXiv:2006.15045].

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar [ HB89 ] 1352-104 as a K2 background-survey verification case, showing a short outburst, a three-day C06 event, and visible variability before and after the outburst.

quotes.quote: An example detection figure is shown in Fig. fig:Visual for a short outburst from quasar [ HB89 ] 1352-104. The top of the figure contains diagnostic information on the object and position; left shows the event light curve, with diagnostic information, such as thruster firings and quality flags, the background and nearest science target light curves, and the identified event duration in orange; right shows the reference image at the top and the brightest frame from the event at the bottom. Selected frames from the corresponding event video for [ HB89 ] 1352-104 are shown in Fig. fig:movie .

Two examples of short transient events detected by K2 :BS are variability in quasar [ HB89 ] 1352-104, and KSN 2015K. During C06, quasar [ HB89 ] 1352-104 experienced an outburst that lasted 3 days, as seen in Fig. fig:Visual . Variability pre- and post- outburst are also visible.

gmw_00003309.name: J0148+0600
gmw_00003309.overview: J0148+0600 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:2309.04614, arXiv:2403.07986] whose host galaxy exhibits a regular elliptical shape [arXiv:2309.04614]. The quasar resides in a proto-cluster containing 47 nearby emitting galaxies [arXiv:2403.07986] and features a deep Gunn-Peterson absorption trough in its spectrum [arXiv:1807.04229, arXiv:2309.04614].

sources.summary: The paper treats ULAS J0148+0600 as an exceptional quasar sightline, using Becker2015 measurements instead of its own lower-S/N spectrum because its deep GP trough gives stringent IGM opacity limits.

quotes.quote: For the majority of quasar spectra that we use in our analysis the IGM opacity has been analyzed before. However, we have co-added the data from multiple observation runs (see sec:reduction for details), in order to achieve higher signal-to-noise. However, for one object, ULAS J0148+0600 , the data obtained by Becker2015 with VLT/X-Shooter in a 10 h observation, has a higher S/N ratio than our spectrum. This sightline exhibits a particularly deep GP trough Becker2015 , and hence the enhanced S/N ratio results in more stringent opacity limits, representing the strongest fluctuations in the IGM opacity at this redshift. In order to model the IGM fluctuations correctly, it is important to include these outliers Chardin2015, Daloisio2015, DaviesFurlanetto2016 . Thus we construct a master compilation of opacity measurements, presented in the left panel of Fig. fig:tau_alpha , and replace our optical depth measurements within the forest along just the sightline of ULAS J0148+0600 with the more precise measurements obtained by Becker2015 (orange data points). This mainly adds the two most stringent limits at z=5.634 and z=5.796 to our analysis, since the better data quality results in higher sensitivity in the GP troughs.

sources.summary: The paper detects the host of J0148+0600 as a regular elliptical-shaped galaxy in PSF-subtracted images and notes two projected companions of uncertain association.

quotes.quote: J0148+0600 (z=5.977) was initially discovered by jiang15 . This quasar is known for the large Gunn-Peterson trough in its spectruum e.g., becker15 The PSF-subtracted images of J0148+0600 (Figure fig:J0148images ) indicate that its host galaxy has a regular elliptical shape. J0148+0600 has two projected companions in the north and the south directions, and it is unclear whether these two objects are associated with the quasar.

sources.summary: The paper treats ULAS J0148+0600 as an extreme high-redshift quasar sightline whose long absorption trough drives the largest Ly forest optical depths and is rare in the simulations.

quotes.quote: We note that the tail of the largest optical depths that is most challenging to match corresponds to the long absorption trough of ULAS J0148+0600, which we have previously shown to be rare in our simulations.

We note however that the most extreme points, with _ eff, Ly 6 in that redshift bin, are measurements from the sightline ULAS J0148+0600 which is the sightline that hosts the extreme 110 Mpc/ h absorption trough identified in becker2015 .

We further note that the sightline that these observations are measured along is the long absorption trough of ULAS J0148+0600, which we have already shown to be rare in radiative transfer simulations such as these.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0148+0600 as a z~6 quasar in a spectacular overdense proto-cluster, with 47 nearby emitting galaxies and a <2 cMpc density contrast of 138.

quotes.quote: With a total of N_ gal =47 galaxies observed in the complete observed volume around the quasar J0148+0600, this quasar resides in a spectacular proto-cluster within the Epoch of Reionization with an overdensity of (<2 cMpc )=138 32 .

While J0148+0600 with 47 emitting galaxies in its surroundings likely resides in one of the highest-density peaks, and traces one of the largest high-redshift proto-cluster reported to date, J1030+0542 on the other hand appears to reside in an average density region with no enhancement compared to a random field which is in contrast to the proto-cluster reported in this field by Mignoli2020 .

We find a large diversity in the observed density contrast between the different quasar fields, ranging from density enhancements of (<2 cMpc )=138 32 - one of the most spectacular proto-clusters within the Epoch of Reionization discovered to date encompassing 47 emitting galaxies around the quasar J0148+0600 -- to (<2 cMpc )=7 8 , indicating no significant galaxy enhancement around the quasar J1030+0564 in strong contrast to results by Mignoli2020 .

gmw_00003310.name: IC 1101
aliases_local: ultra-luminous central cD galaxy of Abell 2029
gmw_00003310.overview: IC 1101 is an ultra-luminous central cD galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0404088, arXiv:1509.04782] and the brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 2029 [arXiv:1809.01137, arXiv:1808.00488]. It features the largest core size measured in any galaxy to date [arXiv:1910.10240], hosts a wide-angle-tail radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0404088], and exhibits ongoing star formation [arXiv:1206.1676].

sources.summary: The paper corrects the A2029 BCG photometry using SDSS DR7 magnitudes for stellar-mass estimates and identifies IC 1101 as having the cluster members' largest velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: The stellar masses of the brightest galaxies of A2029 and A2033 we initially derive from SDSS DR12 are underestimated. Their SDSS DR12 cModel magnitudes are significantly overestimated: = 17.29 for the A2029 BCG and = 17.44 for the A2033 BCG. Their DR7 cModel magnitudes are more reasonable: = 13.59 for the A2029 BCG and = 14.20 for the A2033 BCG, similar to R-band magnitudes of these galaxies listed in NED. Therefore, we use the SDSS DR7 cModel magnitudes to estimate the stellar masses for these BCGs.

The brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of A2029 (IC 1101) has the largest velocity dispersion, = 430 17 among the cluster members. The velocity dispersions of the brightest galaxies of SIG ( = 307 6 ) and A2033 ( = 357 6 ) are also very large. Interestingly, the velocity dispersions of the brightest galaxies in A2029 and its subsystems are roughly correlated with the masses of the systems. Sohn18b estimate the velocity dispersions of the A2029, A2033 and SIG systems: _ A2029 = 967 25 , _ A2033 = 701 74 , and _ SIG = 745 62 . The relation between the central galaxies and the cluster velocity dispersions suggests a connection between the brightest galaxy properties and the properties of the host system.

sources.summary: The paper uses Abell 2029 as an example of a BCG whose kinematic profile can be modeled with variable anisotropy, while stellar-population mass-to-light ratios remain constant.

quotes.quote: To illustrate this, we use Abell 2029 as an example. For this BCG, Figure A267_varML shows the model fit when all three mass components ( + CEN + DM) are used, and the resulting best-fitting value for a constant _ DYN = 6.59 (solid black line, ^ 2 /DOF=2.47 ). We also show a fit for a variable _ z () ( _ z = [ 0.6, 0.3, --0.05, --0.15, --0.30 ] , with _ DYN =7.0 , where the linear radial increments are for the different Gaussians used in the stellar mass description, shown with a blue line, ^ 2 /DOF=1.88 ). We also show lines for constant _ DYN at 6.0, 7.0 and 7.5, and we see that a similar fit could be achieved by varying _ DYN () from e.g. 7.5 down to 6.0 and again up to 7.5.

However, from the analysis we find that the stellar mass-to-light ratio derived for Abell 2029 from stellar populations _ POP is constant between our central aperture (0 -- 5 kpc) and the outer aperture (5 -- 15 kpc) within the errors, with an average value of 4.08 1.04 (for a Salpeter IMF). It is also unlikely that the mass-to-light ratio will decrease and then again increase sharply over this radial range.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 1101 as A2029's exceptionally massive brightest cluster galaxy and finds it nearly coincident with the Chandra X-ray and kinematic centers of the cluster.

quotes.quote: A2029 contains the most massive BCG (IC 1101) in the local universe Uson91 .
Indeed, the offset between the A2029 BCG and the Chandra X-ray position is only 0.42 kpc (cf. 1 kpc in Lauer14 ).

Next, we examine the position of the A2029 BCG with respect to the cluster center in redshift space (Figure rv ). The projected offset ( R_ cl ) is 123 170 kpc and the radial velocity offset in the rest frame of the cluster is 170 107 . The positional uncertainties include the error in the caustic center Serra11 . Within the uncertainty, IC 1101 lies on the kinematic center of A2029.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 1101 as A2029's brightest cluster galaxy, whose inferred star-formation gamma-ray luminosity is near limits while radio, X-ray, and UV/H evidence suggest AGN or starburst contributions.

quotes.quote: The lower limit luminosity of the BCG (IC 1101) in the cool-core cluster A2029 in particular is quite close to the upper limit. The core of A2029 shows extended radio emission which suggests it hosts an AGN Taylor1994 . A2029 has also been observed in X-rays, but it is unclear whether the X-ray emission is due to an AGN since the emission is not point-source-like and does not fit a power-law spectrum, as is typical for X-ray AGNs Clarke2004 . Additionally, the star formation rate calculated nominally from the presented radio luminosity in McDonald2011 using the relationship in Kennicutt1998 yields an SFR that is higher than that of any local starburst galaxy; therefore is it most probable that only a fraction of the radio luminosity is due to star formation. However, the large ratio of far UV to H emission suggests that the BCG of A2029 is an older starburst galaxy with ongoing star formation activity McDonald2011 , so it is included in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper includes IC 1101 as the largest-core BCG in its 13-galaxy large-core sample and uses its prior decompositions as the modeling template.

quotes.quote: Together with the large-core BCG IC 1101 2017MNRAS.471.2321D , these 13 galaxies constitute the largest sample of ``large-core'' galaxies studied to date. We aim to revisit the structural scaling relations of our full sample of 41 core-S ersic galaxies (13 ``large-core'' galaxies plus 28 ``normal-core'' galaxies, 2014MNRAS.444.2700D ) over a comprehensive dynamic range in core size ( R_ b 0.02 - 4.2 kpc), spheroid luminosity ( 20.70 mag M_ V 25.40 mag), spheroid stellar mass ( M_ * 8 10^ 10 7 10^ 12 M_ ) and SMBH mass ( M_ BH 2 10^ 8 2 10^ 10 M_ ). The case of the BCG IC 1101 is published in 2017MNRAS.471.2321D and the remaining 12 galaxies consist of 9 galaxies with r_ > 0.5 kpc drawn from the 2007ApJ...664..226L , 4C +74.13 r_ = 1.54 kpc 2009ApJ...698..594M , the BCG of Abell A2261 r_ = 3.95 kpc 2012ApJ...756..159P and the giant cD galaxy NGC 4486 ( 2011MNRAS.415.2158R , R_ b 0.5 kpc; 2013AJ....146..160R , R_ b 0.7 kpc).

In 2017MNRAS.471.2321D , the 1D and 2D decompositions of the high-resolution F702W and F450W data of the BCG IC 1101 revealed a S ersic intermediate-scale component, an outer exponential halo and an inner Gaussian component that are additional to the core-S ersic spheroid light distribution. This galaxy has the largest core size measured in any galaxy to date ( R_ b 4.2 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC1101 as an extremely luminous, large cD central galaxy in Abell 2029 where merger-driven black-hole growth and high Bondi accretion are plausible.

quotes.quote: Abell 2029: The central host galaxy, IC1101, is listed as one of the optically most luminous and largest cD-galaxy 1979ApJ...231..659D, 2004ApJ...617..879L . Therefore, a merger driven growth of its central black hole at the upper limits ( 5- 7 10^ 10 ) of cosmological predictions 2015MNRAS.451.1177L seems reasonable. Additionally, IC1101 is embedded within a massive and relaxed galaxy cluster 2003ApJ...586..135L . If classical Bondi accretion with its quadratical SMBH mass dependency ( 1952MNRAS.112..195B , our Section A_Models ) is realized, the nuclear SMBH should accrete at high levels.

sources.original_name: ultra-luminous central cD galaxy of Abell 2029
sources.summary: The paper describes Abell 2029's central cD galaxy as an ultra-luminous radio-galaxy host with steep-spectrum wide-angle-tail lobes, an inverted-spectrum compact core, and a synchrotron age near 12 million years.

quotes.quote: The radio source associated with the ultra-luminous central cD galaxy of Abell 2029 has a very steep radio spectrum ( = 1.5 above 100 MHz) and has been discussed in detail by Taylor et al. (1994). The two radio lobes exhibit a pronounced ``wide-angle-tail (WAT)'' morphology with an overall extent of at least 80 kpc (H _o =50 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 used throughout this paper). The compact central core has an inverted radio spectrum ( =+0.2); the spectrum steepens to = 2.7 near the edges of the radio tails. From synchrotron ageing arguments, these authors estimated an age of 1.2 10^7 years.
