sources.summary: XMM-Newton finds no embedded AGN in A2029's central cD galaxy, although hot hardness-ratio spots appear nearby, and the galaxy core shows super-solar iron abundance attributed to the cD galaxy.

quotes.quote: The X-ray data of A2029 obtained with XMM-Newton show no evidence of an embedded AGN in the central region of this cluster, which was suggested from the analysis of restored ASCA image data, although some hot spots are seen within or around the central cD galaxy.

The iron abundance in the central region (core of the central cD galaxy) exceeds the solar abundance and rapidly decreases with . Since the value at outer region is quite normal (similar to other clusters of galaxies), the very high iron abundance should be attributed to the central cD galaxy.
There is no evidence indicating the existence of an AGN in the central region of the cD galaxy of A2029, although some hot (high hardness ratio) spots are seen within or around the cD galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC1101 as a BCG previously reported with a rising velocity-dispersion profile; this study cannot confirm that profile and finds no significant rotation.

quotes.quote: IC1101: This galaxy is one of the few known BCGs that were previously found to have a rising velocity dispersion profile (Fisher et al. \ 1995a). This result cannot be confirmed here since the derived velocity dispersion profile is not measured to the same radius as in the above mentioned study. No significant rotation is detected in the radial velocity profile.

gmw_00003311.name: 4C 14.11
gmw_00003311.overview: 4C 14.11 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy that exhibits a triple structure [arXiv:2208.09685]. This structure consists of a central radio core and two radio lobes positioned on opposite sides [arXiv:2208.09685].

sources.summary: The paper studies 4C 14.11 as an FR II radio galaxy, maps its lobes and hotspots at 1450/8440 MHz, and finds nonthermal lobe emission indicating an old AGN.

quotes.quote: The goal of this paper is to investigate the flux and spectral index distribution of FR II radio galaxy 4C 14.11. We focused on the distribution of flux and spectral indices over the lobes, as well as in their hot spots. For that purpose, we used publicly available observations of this radio galaxy given at 1450 and 8440 MHz. Particularly, we used Leahy's Atlas of radio-emitting double radio sources, Jodrell Bank Centre for Astrophysics in Manchester, as well as NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database. We found that the non-thermal (synchrotron) radiation dominates over the areas of the lobes.

4C 14.11 (cross-identifications: 4C +14.11; PKS 0411+14; WISEA J041429.81+141623.6; 2MASS J04142982+1416231; LEDA 2819197) was added to the 3CRR sample by lain83 as its flux in the more accurate 4CT survey was high enough that it should have been included in 3CR.
It is a FR II type of radio galaxy. It as a triple source, with a radio core, and two radio lobes located on opposite sides.

gmw_00003312.name: UGCA 292
gmw_00003312.overview: UGCA 292, also known as CVn I dwA, is a low-surface-brightness, extremely metal-poor dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1307.4899, arXiv:2205.12977] located approximately 3.6 Mpc away [arXiv:2006.01735]. It is highly gas-rich but inefficient at converting its gas into stars [arXiv:1307.4899], and its structure features a single Type 1 H I hole [arXiv:2006.01735].

sources.summary: The paper uses CVnIdwA as one of three rotation-curve examples, finding the feedback-modified profile fits well while the NFW halo mass agrees with the fitted value.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:exrots , we show three example rotation curve fits for CVnIdwA, WLM and NGC 6822, chosen to span the range of stellar masses in our full sample (see Table tab:data ). (We show the full sample, including the `rogues', in Appendix app:rotcurves .) The black contours show the median (black), 68 (dark grey) and 95 (light grey) confidence intervals of our fitted rotation curve models (see sec:massmodel ). The vertical green dashed line shows the projected stellar half light radius R_ 1/2 . The thin vertical black line marks the inner data point used for the fit, R_ min (where this is not marked R_ min = 0 ). The blue and green lines show the rotation curve contribution from stars and gas, respectively. The top three panels of Figure fig:exrots show fits using the \ profile, the bottom three using an \ profile, where we set R_ min to ensure that the outer rotation curve is well-fit (see sec:rotmethod for details of our fitting methodology and priors). As can be seen in Figure fig:exrots , in all three cases the \ profile provides an excellent fit to the data, while the \ profile gives a poor fit, reaffirming the long-standing `cusp-core' problem (see sec:intro ).

The good fits that we find when using the \ profile are particularly striking since, like the \ profile, it has only two free parameters: M_ 200 and (see sec:massmodel ). However, despite the \ profile giving a poor fit to the rotation curve shape, the NFW-derived M_ 200 are actually in good agreement with those from our \ fits. For NGC 6822, we find M_ 200, NFW = 2.0_ 0.2 ^ +0.13 10^ 10 M _ ; for WLM, M_ 200, NFW = 5.2_ 1.2 ^ +2.1 10^ 9 M _ ; and for CVnIdwA, M_ 200, NFW = 0.79_ 0.3 ^ +0.5 10^ 9 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper highlights UGCA292 among the lowest-luminosity nearby XMPs, describing this low-surface-brightness subset as unusually gas-rich and inefficient at converting H i gas into stars.

quotes.quote: We call attention to the six sources with the lowest g band luminosity -- UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. These are all nearby (D < 6 Mpc), low-surface brightness galaxies, that also turn out to be XMPs.

We find an anti-correlation between the M _ H i /L _g ratio and the luminosity, whereby fainter XMPs are more gas-rich than brighter XMPs. The result suggests that the less luminous sources have converted a smaller fraction of their H i gas into stars. However, our sources deviate from the observed correlation found for dwarf galaxies (cyan line; bottom; Fig. 5; Staveley-Smith, Davies Kinman 1992) particularly at low luminosities, which correspond to the low-surface brightness, nearby galaxies UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. In this low luminosity regime, the XMP galaxies appear to be 10 to 100 times more gas-rich than typical dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper finds CVn I dwA is a low-surface-brightness, extremely metal-poor dwarf where H I disc flaring most strongly changes the mass model, allowing a halo about three times more massive.

quotes.quote: In Fig. fig:cvidwa we show the mass model for the dwarf galaxy CVn I dwA, which shows the most extreme changes when including the H i disc flaring within our sample. The flaring not only gets rid of the negative | V_ HI | V_ HI in the innermost regions, but it also decreases the contribution of the gas disc at larger radii, allowing for a dark matter halo about three times more massive.

Finally, we note the peculiar position of the galaxy CVn I dwA in the above figures. At M_ 200 10^ 8.8 , CVn I dwA has a higher-than-average M_ bar /M_ 200 with a median value of about 50 per cent the cosmological baryon fraction, although the uncertainties are large. CVn I dwA, is a low surface brightness dwarf with an extremely low metallicity cvnidwa_metals . It would be interesting to further study this galaxy in the future, in the context of recent results that suggest the existence of a number of extended diffuse dwarf galaxies with baryon fractions comparable to the cosmological average (e.g. huds2019,agc114905 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds UGCA 292 has asymmetric H I profiles around its main HII region, locally higher dispersions, h_4 > 0 regions offset from HII regions, and the sample's largest rotational velocity.

quotes.quote: In UGCA 292 (Figure cvnah3hiha ) we also find a large incidence of asymmetric profiles, h_3 > 0 in the western part of the galaxy and h_3 < 0 in the southeast. In between those two regions of asymmetric profiles, and coincident with the most luminous HII region in the galaxy, we find profiles which are indistinguishable from pure Gaussians (Figure puregauss ). These simple Gaussian profiles have higher dispersions, 10--11 , than are found elsewhere in the galaxy (7.5--9.5 ). Some regions of h_4 > 0 are found, generally offset by one beamwidth (300 pc) from HII regions (Figure cvnah4hiha ). UGCA 292 is the galaxy in this sample with the largest rotational velocity, and if beam smearing were important it would be expected to cause an antisymmetric pattern similar to the observed one with h_3 > 0 on one side of the galaxy and h_3 < 0 on the other.

sources.summary: UGCA292 is described as anomalous and highly discrepant, but the paper emphasizes that its small stellar sample and uncertain SFH inflate the measurement uncertainty.

quotes.quote: Another galaxy, UGCA292, is also somewhat anomalous in Figure fig:agbfrac . UGCA929 is predicted to have the largest fraction of young stars, but its TP-AGB population does not account for a correspondingly large flux fraction compared with the other galaxies. However, UGCA292 is the least-populated galaxy in the sample (see Fig. 2), which causes not only the increased uncertainties depicted in the figure, but also potential difficulty in the derivation of the SFH.

The third most discrepant galaxy, UGCA292 with model/data flux ratio =4.39 , is the least populated galaxy in our sample. Therefore its large measurement uncertainties are driven by both small number statistics and large uncertainties in the SFH.

sources.summary: The paper describes CVnIdwA as an extremely metal-poor Canes Venatici dwarf with one Type 1 H I hole and estimated H I disk length and scale height.

quotes.quote: CVnIdwA Canes Venatici I dwarf A (CVnIdwA) or UGCA 292 is an extremely metal-poor dwarf galaxy in the Canes Venatici constellation. The galaxy is situated about 3.6 Mpc away from the Milky Way with V-band magnitude 12.4 , and has only one hole: a Type 1 hole of diameter 288 pc. The estimated \ disk length and the scale height of the galaxy are about 1.4 kpc and 240 pc respectively.

gmw_00003313.name: 3C 109
gmw_00003313.overview: The active galactic nucleus 3C 109 is classified as a broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:2005.09077, arXiv:astro-ph/0605747] and a hidden quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0207654]. It hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2005.09077] and exhibits moderate intrinsic X-ray absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0603090] likely caused by a line of sight grazing its obscuring torus [arXiv:astro-ph/0605747].

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 109 as a broad-line radio galaxy, constraining its X-ray cutoff, absorption, reflection, Eddington ratio, and revised black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the central engine in the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 109. To investigate the immediate surrounding of this accreting, supermassive black hole, we perform a multi-epoch broad-band spectral analysis of a joint / observation (2017), an archival observation (2005) and the 105-month averaged BAT data. We are able to clearly separate the spectrum into a primary continuum, neutral and ionized absorption, and a reflection component.

We constrain the high-energy cutoff of 3C 109 (E _ cut = 49 +7 5 keV ) for the first time. The reflector is found to be ionized (log = 2.3 +0.1 0.2 ) but no relativistic blurring is required by the data. SED analysis confirms the super-Eddington nature of 3C 109 initially ( _ Edd > 2.09).
We obtain a new black hole mass estimate of 9.3 10^ 8 M _ , which brings all Eddington ratio estimates into agreement and does not require 3C 109 to be super-Eddington.

sources.summary: The paper uses NuSTAR observation 60301011004 of 3C 109 to show that bias correction removes an apparent hotter-when-brighter high-energy cutoff trend.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:3C109 we show the NuSTAR observation 60301011004 of 3C 109 as a real example. In this observation, the source and background count rates of FPMA are 0.23 and 0.02 /s respectively, and the frms is 0.09. The crossed fraction (see Figure fig:lcs for definition) is 69 , thus the effects of the biases are expected to be strong in this observation. The correction factors we derived are [ f_ 1 ^ high , f_ 2 ^ high , f_ 1 ^ low , f_ 2 ^ low ] = [ 1.056, 0.868, 0.953, 1.092 ] . The 1 E_ cut contours through fitting the spectra with model pexrav, Magdziarz_1995 of the high and low states, before (dashed line) and after (solid line) the correction are plotted in Figure fig:3C109 .

Before applying our corrections, the best-fit increases from 58^ +18 _ 11 keV for the low state to > 240 keV for the high state (the statistical errors and lower limit are at 1 confidence level for one interesting parameter), yielding a ``hotter/softer-when-brighter" variation trend (larger in the brighter state). However, such a pseudo ``hotter/softer-when-brighter" trend vanishes after correcting the biases ( = 77^ +32 _ 18 / 130^ +147 _ 47 keV for the low/high states, thus no clear evidence of variation is detected).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 109 as an FR II radio galaxy with polarization like 3C 234, arguing its HST compact core is scattered hidden-quasar light.

quotes.quote: The same argument applies to 3C 109, a FR II radio galaxy with a redshift of z=0.3056, just above the distance limit of our sample. Its polarization properties are reminiscent of those of 3C 234 and it was similarly interpreted as a hidden quasar. Also in this case, we have tested that the scattered continuum matches its CCC flux The continuum flux in 3C 109 is as polarized as the broad lines (indicating the absence of a significant diluting component) at a level of 7 .

Given a polarized flux at 7000 \ of 2 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 A ^ 1 this translates into a scattered component of 3 10^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 A ^ 1 . The nuclear source in the HST images has a flux of 2.8 10^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 A ^ 1 . . Remarkably, its CCC also lies on the correlation (again within 2 ).

sources.summary: The XMM-Newton study describes 3C 109 as a luminous broad-line radio galaxy with high intrinsic X-ray luminosity, a large Eddington ratio, and excess absorption likely from a grazing torus sightline.

quotes.quote: We report results from an XMM--Newton observation of the broad--line radio galaxy 3C 109 (z=0.3056).

The 2--10 keV intrinsic luminosity of 3C 109 is of the order of 2--3 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 regardless of the adopted model. A recent black hole mass estimate of 2 10^8 M_ implies that L_ bol /L_ Edd > 1 . If partial covering is excluded, the observed reflection fraction (of the order of unity), steep photon index (1.86), and Fe line equivalent width (about 100 eV) all suggest to exclude that the X--ray continuum is strongly beamed indicating that the large Eddington ratio is associated with a radiatively efficient accretion process and making it unlikely that the innermost accretion disc is replaced by a thick radiatively inefficient medium such as in advection--dominated accretion models.

The better quality of the XMM--Newton data enables us to attribute the excess absorption to slightly ionized gas in the line of sight, located at the redshift of 3C 109. The most likely interpretation for the excess absorption is that the line--of--sight is grazing the obscuring torus of unified models, which is consistent with the inclination inferred from the Fe line profile (about 40 ^ ) and with the hybrid radio--galaxy/quasar nature of 3C 109.

sources.summary: The paper finds 3C 109 has moderate intrinsic absorption consistent with earlier ROSAT and ASCA results, but no Chandra evidence for the previously reported iron line.

quotes.quote: 3C 109 The moderate intrinsic absorption we find in the spectrum of this BLRG is in good agreement with the results of earlier ROSAT and ASCA observations (Allen Fabian 1992; Allen \ 1997) given the slightly different Galactic absorption column we adopt. The photon indices and spectral normalizations are also consistent within the joint 90 per cent confidence limits. We find no evidence for the iron line reported by Allen \ (1997), although the signal to noise is relatively low in the Chandra data because of the annular extraction region we use.

gmw_00003314.name: HCG 22A
gmw_00003314.overview: HCG 22A is an E2 elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1009.3010, arXiv:astro-ph/0210574] and the brightest member of the HCG 22 compact group [arXiv:1009.3010, arXiv:astro-ph/0210574]. It hosts a centrally concentrated globular cluster system [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574] that exhibits a bimodal color distribution [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1199 as HCG 22a, the brightest/dominant central galaxy in HCG 22, with group optical luminosity, X-ray emission, and richest globular-cluster system centered on it.

quotes.quote: HCG 22, as originally catalogued by hic82 , consists of three giant galaxies, E2 NGC 1199 (HCG 22a) the Sa NGC 1190 (HCG 22b) and the SBcd NGC 1189 (HCG 22c), with magnitudes in the range from M_B=-20.4 to 18.1 hic89 . An additional smaller member galaxy was latter identified by dec97 , leading to a group mean radial velocity of 2629 33 km s^ 1 and velocity dispersion of 40 28 km s^ 1 rib98 . The group's angular diameter is about 6' . This group is in an earlier evolutionary stage, as indicated by the low number of optical signs of galaxy interaction men94 , its larger crossing time ( 0.2 \ t_ Hubble ), high spiral fraction hic92 and H i content ver01 . This group shows no signs of intragroup light, where most of the optical luminosity is concentrated on its brightest galaxy (NGC 1199).

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 22A as an E2 brightest group galaxy with a normal-to-poor, centrally concentrated globular cluster system and bimodal globular-cluster colors.

quotes.quote: HCG 22A - brightest galaxy of the triplet HCG 22, classified as an E2 galaxy, at 33.1 Mpc , observed with Keck/LRIS in the B and R bands.
We have obtained specific frequencies of 3.6 1.8 and 5.2 3.2 using the core and power law models, respectively, showing a normal to poor GCS, considering the error bars.

The high slope fitted to the radial profile ( = 2.5 0.3 ; see fig figh22 ) shows a GCS concentrated toward the center of the galaxy (the ``regular'' values range from 1.0 to 2.0 (Ashman Zepf, 1998)). The color distribution of GCs around HCG 22A was analyzed with the KMM (Ashman et al., 1994) which has detected the presence of two sub--populations with peaks at (B-R)_0 = 1.13 0.04 and 1.42 0.04 (see fig figh22 ), corresponding to metallicities of [ Fe/H ] = -1.45 0.21 dex and 0.55 0.21 dex . 62 of the GCs belong to the blue population and 38 to the red one.

gmw_00003315.name: HCG 68A/B
gmw_00003315.overview: HCG 68A/B is an interacting galaxy pair classified as S0 and E2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574], located at the dynamical center of its group [arXiv:0802.1737]. The system contains a poor globular cluster system [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574], while the individual galaxy HCG 68a hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.05779] and compact molecular gas [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper says CARMA clarified HCG 68a's earlier nondetection, finding compact, broad molecular gas and unusual CO rotation; it also notes AGN-related far-infrared contribution and strong MOHEG emission.

quotes.quote: HCG 68a (Fig fig:hcg68 ) was previously un-detected by the IRAM 30m in the \ survey young+11 , but was later detected by lisenfeld+14 . The CARMA observation helps shed light on why this was the case. The molecular gas in HCG 68a is not only compact (unresolved by the CARMA beam), but also very broad ( v 2000 ). HCG 68a also appears to have rotation velocities in its CO emission that makes it an outlier on the M _ gal - v_ rot,CO relation davis+11a .

DecompIR decompir suggests that about 9 of the far-IR emission bitsakis+14 originates from the AGN in this system. The AGN in HCG 68a has an 2--10 keV X-ray luminosity of L_X = 1.6 10^ 40 ergs s ^ 1 evans+10 , which could account for the majority of the far-IR emission if the obscuring column of molecular gas is sufficiently high (which the CARMA observations suggest might be the case). HCG 68a is also one of the strongest MOHEGs, with L_ H_2,warm /L_ 7.7 m PAH = 0.741, and is an outlier in IR color space amongst the CO-imaged HCG galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats HCG 68A/B as an interacting first-ranked galaxy pair whose combined globular cluster system is poor and strongly concentrated toward the pair center.

quotes.quote: HCG 68A/B - two first ranked galaxies at the quintet HCG 68, classified as S0 and E2, at 23.8 Mpc , observed with CFHT/MOS in the R band. Pair in probable interaction, it was not possible to separate the individual GCSs, so we have analyzed it as a single GCS centered at the pair geometric center.

HCG 68A/B The magnitude limits applied to this galaxy pair was 20.0 R 23.5 and the GCLF turnover is located at R = 23.8 0.3 , very close to our detection limit. The high value estimated to the radial profile ( 2.8 0.6 ) shows, as in the case of HCG 22A, a GCS very concentrated toward the geometric center of the galaxy pair. The S_N values are 1.3 0.8 for the two models applied showing a poor GCS. Even if we consider an upper limit case where the whole GCS would belong to the elliptical galaxy, the specific frequency is not superior to 3.1 1.7 . The flattening of the central region, that can be seen in the radial profile (fig figh68 ), can be caused by the projection or by the merger of the GCSs of the two galaxies and turns our S_N estimated on an upper limit.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5353 and NGC 5354 as the S0 pair at the group dynamical center, separated by 9 kpc and likely heading toward a major merger.

quotes.quote: However it turns out that the dynamical center is coincident with the second and third brightest galaxies, the S0 pair NGC 5353 and NGC 5354.

The dominant pair that one suspects are in the process of merging are NGC 5353 and NGC 5354. They are only 9 kpc apart in projection and differ in velocity by 245 . NGC 5353 and NGC 5354 are the second and third most luminous galaxies and the sum of their luminosities exceeds that of NGC 5371.

The distribution of these mostly dE dwarf galaxies is clearly locating the dynamical center of the group to be around the pair of S0 galaxies NGC 5353 and NGC 5354. There is a distinct core of early types with an impending major merger (between NGC 5353 and NGC 5354).

sources.summary: The paper resolves NGC 5353 from NGC 5354 in ISO data, separately extracts its far-infrared fluxes, and treats the companion galaxies as individual FIR sources.

quotes.quote: The top panels in Figure fig:arrays show that the galaxies NGC 5353 and NGC 5354 are only about 70 apart, with the latter being in the north. This is sufficiently close for the two galaxies to be covered by the ISOPHOT C100 and C200 arrays, that have 3 3 and 2 2 pixels of size 46 and 92 each, respectively, and to allow the galaxies to be resolved on the C100 array. As shown on the left panels in Figure fig:arrays , NGC 5353 and NGC 5354 were respectively observed exactly with the center and corner pixels of the C100 array, ensuring that the galaxies would be clearly resolved from each other on this array. Those C100 pixels which are not centered on the two sources show some low-level flux (bottom-left histogram of Figure fig:arrays ), which stems from the wings of the point spread function, primarily of the central source. The same applies for the observations at 60 m. The right panels of Figure fig:arrays show that at 180 m NGC 5353 is centered on the 2 2 pixels of C200 array, so that its flux is distributed over all four pixels, and NGC 5354 is located on the corner pixel containing most of its flux.

gmw_00003316.name: HCG 93A
gmw_00003316.overview: HCG 93A, also known as NGC 7550, is an E1 elliptical galaxy and the brightest member of the HCG 93 group [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574]. It exhibits clear signs of interaction, including a central dust spiral and an outer shell, alongside a fully retired stellar population and a measurable amount of molecular gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0210574, arXiv:2009.08383].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 93A as an E1 brightest group galaxy with signs of interaction, a shell, and an extremely poor but normally to highly concentrated globular cluster system.

quotes.quote: HCG 93A - brightest galaxy of the quartet HCG 93, an E1 galaxy, at 67.0 Mpc , observed with CFHT/SIS in the R band. Clear signs of interaction with other group members like a central dust spiral and a shell around it.

HCG 93A To this galaxy we have used 22.5 R 25.5 as magnitude limits and the GCLF turnover is located at R = 26.3 0.3 . The radial profile of selected objects shows a concentration of objects close to the center and two different flat levels that could be used as background level estimators (see fig figh93 . We have performed three cases of analysis: (1) the background level was estimated using the whole flat area, from 0.^ 69 ( 13 kpc ) until 1.^ 59 ( 31 kpc ); (2) we have estimated the background level using the outer flat region, from 1.^ 14 ( 22 kpc ) until 1.^ 59 ; (3) no background estimative was used, all selected objects were considered as GCs. The S_N values estimated for the three cases are extremely low (see table tabh93 ), showing a very poor GCS. The slopes of each case can also be seen in table tabh93 , and indicate a normal to high concentration GCS.

sources.summary: NGC7550 is used as a CO-detected example of a fully retired galaxy whose equivalent-width map is quiescent while measurable molecular gas remains.

quotes.quote: The centres of all the galaxies in this example are well detected in CO, but the continuum and \ equivalent width ( ) maps show that the objects are in three different phases of their evolution.

The last galaxy displayed in the figure, NGC7550, is fully retired, as suggested by W_ H <6 basically everywhere and by the yellow colour of the whole continuum map. The galaxy, however, still possesses a measurable amount of molecular gas.

gmw_00003317.name: NE-BCG
gmw_00003317.overview: The NE-BCG is a brightest cluster galaxy that defines the center of the MACS J0416 cluster [arXiv:1711.02109, arXiv:1511.02522]. It is associated with the Hi-V subcluster [arXiv:1511.02522], and its dark-matter halo is offset from the cluster's northern dark-matter halo center [arXiv:1407.7866].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the northern BCG as the central stellar-mass concentration in MACS J0416, tying the steep inner total and global dark-matter profiles to its dark-matter halo.

quotes.quote: We can see from Figure f:sigmatot that the stellar mass is concentrated mainly in the center, which is coincident with the position of the northern BCG, of the cluster and is embedded in the cluster members, while the hot gas contribution increases moving towards more external regions.

This can be explained considering that the center of the cluster is coincident with the position of the northern BCG, hence the steep total and global dark-matter profiles can be related to the dark-matter halo of the BCG. In the right panels of Figure f:nfwfits , we show the radial dependence of the logarithmic slope (R) , defined as (R) = - dln (R) dln(R) . In the case of a power-law profile _ in = + 1 (see Sect. s:intro ). In the other cases the relation between the two slopes is more complex. This quantity has been calculated numerically for the total matter and global dark-matter surface profiles, as derived from the data and as predicted from the NFW, Hernquist, NIS, and power-law best-fits. We evaluate the slope values only in the radial range between 5 and 100 kpc. In the very center of the cluster (i.e., the region dominated by the northern BCG), the total matter and global dark-matter density profiles are steeper than NFW and Hernquist models. The global DM profile is somewhat flatter than the total mass profile. The diffuse DM profile is significantly flatter than the others. In the diffuse DM component the BCG is not included.

The stellar mass contribution reaches the peak value of f_* = 15 within 20 kpc from the cluster center, due to the presence of the BCG, then decreases to a mean value of 2 at 100 kpc from the cluster center. The hot gas to total mass fraction, instead, increases with the distance from the center. The baryon fraction, evaluated as the sum of the stellar and hot gas components over the total mass of the cluster, has a peak value of 15 in the cluster center, then decreases reaching 10 at 350 kpc. Both the stellar and baryon fractions are in general good agreement with the global values found in the literature. Our baryon fraction is smaller than the cosmological baryon fraction measured by planck2016 , which however refers to large radii. We have evaluated the ratio between the stellar and total mass embedded in substructures. This fraction is 30 near the cluster center, then decreases to 15 at larger clustercentric distances. We have studied the total mass, global and diffuse dark-matter surface density profiles. In the radial range between 5 and 50 kpc, the surface mass density profiles of the total mass and global dark-matter have comparable slopes. In this radial range, if we parametrize (R) as R^ , we obtain values of equal to 0.38 0.01 and 0.36 0.01 for the total and global dark-matter, respectively. These profiles appear steeper than a NFW profile. The diffuse dark-matter component has a profile much flatter near the cluster center that cannot be approximated with a power-law. The difference among these three profiles is related to the BCG dark-matter halo and persists up to 30 kpc from the cluster center, which is approximately three times the value of the effective radius of the BCG.

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as one of MACS 0416's two brightest cluster galaxies and compares the galaxy luminosity centers with significantly offset dark-matter halo centers.

quotes.quote: There, we have marked the two brightest galaxies of the cluster, G1 and G2 (see also Table tab5 ), and the multiple image systems studied in this paper (see Section sec:lensmod:multsys ).

From Tables tab5 and tab3 and Figure fi22 , we remark that the reconstructed centers of two cluster dark-matter halos are significantly separated, more than 3 away, from the centers of the two brightest cluster galaxies. In detail, the northern halo is at a projected distance of approximately 50 kpc from G1 and a smaller projected distance of approximately 30 kpc separates the centers of the southern halo and G2. Interestingly, the northern halo is preferentially displaced towards the North-East direction, whereas the southern halo towards the South-West direction, resulting in a projected distance between the two cluster dark-matter halos of approximately 300 kpc. We have checked that (1) fixing the halo centers to those of the brightest cluster galaxies results in ^ 2 values that are approximately a factor of 4 higher than those obtained with the halo centers free to vary, and (2) increasing the F160W magnitude value of G1 by 0.3 mag, i.e. mimicking an overestimate of the galaxy luminosity due to a possible contamination from the intra cluster light, reduces the offset between the centers of the northern halo and G1 by only about 10 .

sources.summary: The paper uses the NE-BCG to define the cluster center, associates it with the Hi-V subcluster and C overdensity, and links an X-ray cavity to a weak AGN outburst.

quotes.quote: As shown in gri15 , the barycenter of the cluster lies approximately halfway along the line connecting the two BCGs (R.A.=04:16:08.60, Dec.=-24:04:25.2), 23'' from NE-BCG. However, the X-ray emission clearly shows a more prominent peak coincident with the position of the NE-BCG see ogr15 . We decided to adopt the position of the NE-BCG and the peak of the X-ray emission (R.A.=04:16:09.14, Dec.=-24:04:03.1) as the center of the cluster throughout our analysis.
According to the indicator test by geb91 , the NE-BCG can be readily associated in velocity space to the Hi-V subcluster and its projected position is coincident with the C peak spatial overdensity.
The cavity was most likely inflated by a recent weak outburst of the AGN in the NE BCG.

gmw_00003318.name: SW-BCG
gmw_00003318.overview: The SW-BCG is an isolated brightest cluster galaxy that is spatially displaced from the bulk of its dynamically bound subcluster [arXiv:1511.02522]. This displacement may be explained by dynamical friction or by a close encounter and tidal interaction with another massive galaxy [arXiv:1511.02522].

sources.summary: The paper describes the SW-BCG as a displaced, isolated brightest cluster galaxy with peculiar velocity, association with secondary X-ray/lensing features, and possible tidal or dynamical-friction origin.

quotes.quote: The SW-BCG, however, has a >99 peculiar velocity with respect to the velocity distribution of galaxies of the Lo-V subcluster and it is spatially separated from both the SW and C overdensities (see Figure figk2g , lower panel). Interestingly, the SW-BCG seems to be unrelated to any galaxy concentration, although it is coincident with the position of the secondary peak of X-ray surface brightness see ogr15 and the SW mass halo detected via strong lensing analysis by gri15 .

We find a clear spatial displacement between the SW BCG and the bulk of the Lo-V subcluster to which it seems to be dynamically bound. As our 3D analysis has shown, the Lo-V subcluster is highly substructured, being composed by two spatial overdensities of galaxies (separated by 600 kpc) and an isolated BCG sitting halfway between the central overdensity and the SW sub-clump.
The displacement of the SW-BCG could be explained by dynamical friction or by close encounter and tidal interaction with some other massive galaxy in the cluster core.

gmw_00003319.name: PSO J004.3936+17.0862
gmw_00003319.overview: PSO J004.3936+17.0862 is a high-redshift quasar with a very small proximity zone [arXiv:2002.01811]. Its host galaxy's circumgalactic medium may be traced by a high-ion absorption system, and two other dust continuum sources are located nearby [arXiv:2002.01811].

sources.summary: PSO J004+17 has a very small proximity zone; a high-ion absorber is not optically thick, may trace the host CGM, and continuum sources lie nearby.

quotes.quote: PSO J004+17 This object has a very small proximity zone of R_p=1.16 0.15 pMpc ( R_ p, corr =1.71 0.22 pMpc). Although we find a high-ion absorption system within the quasar's proximity zone, it is not optically thick, and thus could not have prematurely truncated the proximity zone (see Fig. fig:abs_J004 ). The redshift of the absorption system coincides with the quasar's systemic redshift and might thus be due to the circumgalactic medium of the quasar's host galaxy. The dust continuum map shown in Fig. fig:cont_map reveals two other continuum sources in the vicinity of this quasar.

sources.summary: The appendix says this quasar required assumptions for iron-template fitting because a line fell in telluric absorption and low signal-to-noise limited the line model.

quotes.quote: PSO J004.3936+17.0862 In the spectrum of this quasar the line falls into one of the telluric absorption bands. To properly fit the continuum, including the iron contribution, we have assumed a FWHM for the iron template of FWHM _ FeII =2500 km s ^ 1 and set the redshift to the systemic redshift. The low signal-to-noise ratio of this spectrum did not justify to use more than one Gaussian component to model the line.

gmw_00003320.name: PSO J007.0273+04.9571
gmw_00003320.overview: PSO J007.0273+04.9571 is a quasar [arXiv:2010.06902]. Its emission line complex is partially affected by strong telluric absorption [arXiv:2010.06902].

sources.summary: The appendix says this quasar needed unweighted continuum fitting, with part of the line complex in telluric absorption and resulting properties needing caution.

quotes.quote: PSO J007.0273+04.9571 To properly fit the continuum over the entire observed wavelength range, we disabled the fit weights for the continuum model. While we do fit the line complex, the red-ward part of the line falls into a window of strong telluric absorption. We caution against over-interpreting the resulting properties in this case.
